topics.title: Solar Radiation Outside the Earth's Atmosphere and at the Earth's Surface, Solar Constant, Basic Sun-Earth Angles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET754 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Radiation Outside the Earth's Atmosphere and at the Earth's Surface, Solar Constant, Basic Sun-Earth Angles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Solar Radiation — The Foundation of Renewable Energy

**Solar radiation** is the radiant energy emitted by the Sun across the entire electromagnetic spectrum, produced by continuous **thermonuclear fusion** reactions (conversion of hydrogen into helium) at the Sun's core. The Sun behaves as a near-perfect **blackbody radiator** at an effective surface temperature of approximately **5778 K**, emitting energy primarily in the wavelength range of **0.2 μm to 3.0 μm** (ultraviolet, visible, and infrared).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Solar radiation* refers to the electromagnetic energy radiated by the Sun, characterized as the primary source of all renewable energy systems on Earth, including solar thermal, solar photovoltaic, and indirectly, wind, biomass, and hydro.

### 1.2 Two Fundamental Domains of Solar Radiation

For the purpose of KTU board examinations, solar radiation is classified into two clearly defined zones:

| Domain | Engineering Term | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Outside Atmosphere** | **Extraterrestrial Radiation (I_on)** | Solar radiation received at the outer boundary of Earth's atmosphere, unaffected by atmospheric absorption, scattering, or reflection. |
| **At Earth's Surface** | **Terrestrial / Global Solar Radiation (I_T)** | Solar radiation that actually reaches a receiver on the ground after interacting with the atmosphere. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Lamp and Dirty Glass" Model**
> Imagine the Sun as a bright incandescent lamp, and Earth's atmosphere as a slightly dirty glass lampshade. The light intensity you measure **just above the glass** (extraterrestrial) is steady and clean. But the light reaching your desk (terrestrial) is reduced and diffused because the glass **absorbs** some wavelengths, **reflects** some light, and **scatters** the rest in all directions. Engineers must know both the "clean input" (solar constant) and the "diminished output" (global radiation) to design efficient solar collectors.

### 1.3 The Solar Constant (I_sc) — The Most Critical KTU Number

The **Solar Constant** is defined as the rate at which solar energy is received on a unit area perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the **mean distance of the Earth from the Sun**, **outside the Earth's atmosphere**.

$$\boxed{I_{sc} = 1367 \ \text{W/m}^2}$$

This value was standardized by the **World Meteorological Organization (WMO)** in 1981 and is universally adopted in all KTU 2024 Scheme calculations. The NASA-standard value is **1366.1 W/m²**, and the latest high-precision satellite measurements place it at **1361 W/m²** — but the KTU board examiner strictly expects **1367 W/m²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:**
> The term "constant" is a slight misnomer. Due to the **elliptical orbit** of the Earth, the actual extraterrestrial irradiance varies by **±3.3%** throughout the year — peaking in early January (perihelion) and dipping in early July (aphelion). This is captured in the **eccentricity correction factor**.

### 1.4 Basic Sun-Earth Angles — The Geometric Vocabulary

Every KTU 2024 Scheme question on solar energy geometry relies on a precise vocabulary of angles. These angles define the **relative position of the Sun** with respect to a given point on Earth at a given time.

The **five fundamental sun-earth angles** are:

1. **Declination Angle (δ)** — The angular position of the Sun north or south of the equator, varying throughout the year due to Earth's axial tilt of **23.45°**.
2. **Hour Angle (ω)** — The angular displacement of the Sun east or west of the local meridian due to Earth's rotation (15° per hour).
3. **Latitude (φ)** — The angular position of the location north or south of the equator.
4. **Zenith Angle (θ_z)** — The angle between the Sun's rays and the local vertical (normal to a horizontal surface).
5. **Solar Altitude Angle (α)** — The angle between the Sun's rays and the horizontal plane (α = 90° − θ_z).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Sundial Geometry"**
> Picture a sundial on a table in your garden. The angle the shadow makes depends on three things: **the day of the year** (declination), **the time of day** (hour angle), and **where you live on the globe** (latitude). These three inputs fully determine the sun's position. The other angles (zenith, altitude) are just geometric consequences.

The KTU syllabus also requires understanding **Azimuth Angle (γ_s)**, **Angle of Incidence (θ)**, and **Solar Azimuth at Sunrise/Sunset (ω_s)** — all derived from the five primary angles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Extraterrestrial Radiation

The Sun emits a total **luminosity** of approximately **3.846 × 10²⁶ W**. This energy radiates isotropically in all directions. As this spherical wavefront expands, the energy per unit area decreases with the square of the distance from the Sun.

**Why is the solar constant what it is?**

The Sun's surface emits energy at a rate described by the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** for a blackbody at T_s = 5778 K. If R_s is the Sun's radius and D_se is the mean Earth-Sun distance:

$$\sigma T_s^4 \cdot 4\pi R_s^2 = I_{sc} \cdot 4\pi D_{se}^2$$

Solving:

$$I_{sc} = \sigma T_s^4 \left(\frac{R_s}{D_{se}}\right)^2 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (5778)^4 \times (6.96 \times 10^8 / 1.496 \times 10^{11})^2 \approx 1367 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

This is why **1367 W/m²** is the KTU-mandated value — it is not an empirical guess but a derivable quantity from first principles.

## 2.2 Eccentricity Correction — Why 1367 Is Not Constant

Earth's orbit is an **ellipse** with the Sun at one focus. The Earth-Sun distance varies between:
- **Perihelion** (closest, around January 3): D = 1.471 × 10¹¹ m
- **Aphelion** (farthest, around July 4): D = 1.521 × 10¹¹ m

Using the inverse-square law, the extraterrestrial irradiance at the actual Earth-Sun distance is:

$$I_{on} = I_{sc} \left(\frac{D_{se}}{D}\right)^2 = I_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 \cdot n}{365}\right)$$

where **n** is the **day number of the year** (Jan 1 = 1, Dec 31 = 365). The factor (1 + 0.033 cos(360n/365)) is the **eccentricity correction factor** or **E_0**.

## 2.3 Solar Radiation at Earth's Surface — The Three Components

After penetrating the atmosphere, the extraterrestrial radiation is split into three components reaching the ground:

| Component | Symbol | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct (Beam) Radiation** | I_b | Travels in straight line from Sun; casts shadows. | Drives concentrated solar power (CSP) and PV tracking. |
| **Diffuse Radiation** | I_d | Scattered by air molecules, dust, clouds. Comes from all sky directions. | Dominant on cloudy days; critical for flat-plate collectors. |
| **Reflected (Albedo) Radiation** | I_r | Reflected from ground, water, snow back to a tilted surface. | Negligible for horizontal surfaces; important for tilted panels. |

**Global Radiation on Horizontal Surface:** $I_T = I_b + I_d$ (albedo is zero for horizontal)

**Global Radiation on Tilted Surface:** $I_T = I_b \cdot R_b + I_d \cdot R_d + I \cdot \rho \cdot R_r$ where R_b, R_d, R_r are tilt factors and ρ is ground albedo.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the atmosphere attenuate radiation?** Three mechanisms:
> 1. **Absorption** — primarily by ozone (UV), water vapor (IR), CO₂ (IR), and oxygen.
> 2. **Scattering** — by air molecules (Rayleigh scattering) and aerosols (Mie scattering).
> 3. **Reflection** — by clouds (albedo of clouds ≈ 0.6–0.9).

## 2.4 Air Mass — Quantifying Atmospheric Path Length

The **Air Mass (AM)** is the ratio of the actual path length of solar radiation through the atmosphere to the path length when the Sun is directly overhead (at zenith).

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AM 1.5 Spectrum** — The standard terrestrial solar spectrum used for rating solar PV modules. AM 0 = outer space; AM 1 = Sun at zenith; AM 1.5 = Sun at 48.19° from zenith (typical average for mid-latitudes); total irradiance = **1000 W/m²**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table consolidates **all KTU 2024 Scheme high-frequency formulas** for this topic. **Memorize every cell before your exam.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Range / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar Constant | $I_{sc}$ | **1367** | W/m² |
| 2 | Extraterrestrial Irradiance | $I_{on} = I_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\dfrac{360n}{365}\right)$ | 1322 to 1412 | W/m² |
| 3 | Declination Angle | $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left(\dfrac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$ | −23.45° to +23.45° | degrees |
| 4 | Hour Angle | $\omega = 15^\circ(t_{solar} - 12)$ | −180° to +180° | degrees |
| 5 | Zenith Angle | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | 0° to 90° | degrees |
| 6 | Altitude Angle | $\alpha = 90^\circ - \theta_z$ | 0° to 90° | degrees |
| 7 | Azimuth Angle (cos form) | $\cos\gamma_s = \dfrac{\sin\alpha\sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha\cos\phi}$ | −180° to +180° | degrees |
| 8 | Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$ | 0° to 180° | degrees |
| 9 | Air Mass | $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$ | 1 (zenith) to ∞ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Extraterrestrial on Horizontal (instantaneous) | $I_o = I_{on}\cos\theta_z$ | 0 to I_on | W/m² |
| 11 | Daily Extraterrestrial on Horizontal | $\overline{H_o} = \dfrac{24}{\pi}I_{on}\left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \dfrac{2\pi\omega_s}{360}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$ | 0 to ~ 40 | MJ/m²/day |
| 12 | Angle of Incidence on Tilted Surface | $\cos\theta = \sin\delta\sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi-\beta)\cos\gamma\cos\omega$ (south-facing, surface tilted β) | 0° to 90° | degrees |
| 13 | Tilt Factor (Beam) | $R_b = \dfrac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z}$ | 0 to ~ 1.5 | dimensionless |
| 14 | Equation of Time | $B = \dfrac{360}{365}(n-81)$ (in degrees) | — | degrees |
| 15 | Declination (Cooper) — exact | $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left(360^\circ \cdot \dfrac{284+n}{365}\right)$ | — | degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note on φ, β, γ Convention:**
> - **φ (latitude)**: positive in Northern Hemisphere, negative in Southern.
> - **β (tilt)**: angle of surface with horizontal (0° = horizontal, 90° = vertical).
> - **γ (surface azimuth)**: 0° = South, negative = East, positive = West (in Northern Hemisphere convention).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Sun-Angle Used | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Solar PV Tilt Optimization** | φ, δ, ω, θ | Annual tilt = latitude; seasonal tilt = φ ± 15° for winter/summer. |
| **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** | θ, I_bn (beam normal) | Only direct normal irradiance (DNI) is focused; diffuse is wasted. |
| **Building Design / Shading** | α, γ_s, ω | Architects compute overhang depth using summer/winter altitude angles. |
| **Solar Thermal Collector Sizing** | H_o, I_T | Daily extraterrestrial radiation drives the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation. |
| **Satellite / Spacecraft Orientation** | δ, ω | Solar array deployment timing is scheduled by hour angle. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Solar Constant

The Sun is modeled as a blackbody radiator at temperature $T_s = 5778 \ \text{K}$, with radius $R_s = 6.96 \times 10^8 \ \text{m}$. The total power emitted by the Sun (its luminosity $L$) follows the Stefan-Boltzmann law:

$$L = \sigma T_s^4 \cdot 4\pi R_s^2$$

This luminosity spreads out spherically. At the mean Earth-Sun distance $D_{se} = 1.496 \times 10^{11} \ \text{m}$, the energy flux (irradiance) per unit area perpendicular to the Sun's rays is:

$$I_{sc} = \frac{L}{4\pi D_{se}^2} = \sigma T_s^4 \left(\frac{R_s}{D_{se}}\right)^2$$

Substituting numerical values step by step:

$$\sigma T_s^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (5778)^4$$

$$(5778)^2 = 3.339 \times 10^7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (5778)^4 = 1.115 \times 10^{15}$$

$$\sigma T_s^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.115 \times 10^{15} = 6.32 \times 10^7 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

$$\left(\frac{R_s}{D_{se}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{6.96 \times 10^8}{1.496 \times 10^{11}}\right)^2 = (4.652 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 2.164 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$I_{sc} = 6.32 \times 10^7 \times 2.164 \times 10^{-5} \approx 1367 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Verified — matches the KTU-mandated 1367 W/m².**

## 3.2 Derivation of the Declination Angle (Cooper's Equation)

The Earth's axis is tilted at an angle of **23.45°** with respect to the plane of its orbit (the ecliptic). The declination δ is the angle between the Sun-Earth line and the equatorial plane.

From spherical trigonometry, considering the Earth's orbital geometry, the declination is a sinusoid with period of one year and amplitude 23.45°. The phase shift is set such that δ = 0 at the spring equinox (n = 81, since 81 = day number of March 22).

**Cooper's Equation:**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$$

**Step-by-step verification for March Equinox (n = 81):**

$$\frac{360(284+81)}{365} = \frac{360 \times 365}{365} = 360^\circ$$

$$\sin(360^\circ) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta = 0^\circ \quad \checkmark$$

**Verification for Summer Solstice (June 21, n = 172):**

$$\frac{360(284+172)}{365} = \frac{360 \times 456}{365} = 449.75^\circ$$

$$\sin(449.75^\circ) = \sin(89.75^\circ) \approx 0.9999 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta \approx 23.45^\circ \quad \checkmark$$

**Verification for Winter Solstice (Dec 21, n = 355):**

$$\frac{360(284+355)}{365} = \frac{360 \times 639}{365} = 630.08^\circ$$

$$\sin(630.08^\circ) = \sin(270.08^\circ) = -\cos(0.08^\circ) \approx -0.9999 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta \approx -23.45^\circ \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Zenith Angle

Consider a point P on Earth's surface at latitude φ. The Sun's position is defined by declination δ and hour angle ω in the celestial sphere coordinate system. The angle between the Sun and the local zenith (zenith angle θ_z) is given by the **cosine rule on a spherical triangle**.

The three vertices of the spherical triangle are: **Zenith, North Celestial Pole, Sun's position**. The angular sides are:
- Side 1 (Zenith to Pole) = 90° − φ
- Side 2 (Pole to Sun) = 90° − δ
- Included angle at Pole = ω (hour angle)

By the spherical law of cosines:

$$\cos\theta_z = \cos(90^\circ - \phi)\cos(90^\circ - \delta) + \sin(90^\circ - \phi)\sin(90^\circ - \delta)\cos\omega$$

Since $\cos(90^\circ - x) = \sin x$ and $\sin(90^\circ - x) = \cos x$:

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$

This is the **fundamental KTU equation** — derived rigorously from spherical trigonometry.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle

Sunrise and sunset occur when the Sun is on the horizon, i.e., when the **zenith angle θ_z = 90°** (or equivalently, the altitude angle α = 0°). Substituting θ_z = 90° in the zenith angle equation:

$$\cos(90^\circ) = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega_s$$

$$0 = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega_s$$

Solving for $\cos\omega_s$:

$$\cos\omega_s = -\frac{\sin\phi\sin\delta}{\cos\phi\cos\delta} = -\tan\phi\tan\delta$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$$

The **day length** in hours is:

$$N = \frac{2\omega_s}{15^\circ}$$

## 3.5 Derivation of Daily Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface

The instantaneous extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface is:

$$I_o = I_{on}\cos\theta_z$$

To get the **daily total** $\overline{H_o}$ (in J/m² or MJ/m²), we integrate $I_o$ over the daylight hours, from sunrise ($\omega = -\omega_s$) to sunset ($\omega = +\omega_s$).

The hour angle ω is related to time: $d\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{24 \times 3600} \, dt$ (radians per second), but for solar time, the sun moves 15°/hour, so $d\omega = 15^\circ/\text{hour}$.

**Integration:**

$$\overline{H_o} = \int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} I_{on}\cos\theta_z \cdot \frac{24 \times 3600}{2\pi} \, d\omega$$

$$\overline{H_o} = \frac{24 \times 3600}{2\pi} I_{on} \int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} (\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega) \, d\omega$$

Since the first term is constant and the second integrates to a sine:

$$\int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} \sin\phi\sin\delta \, d\omega = 2\omega_s \sin\phi\sin\delta$$

$$\int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega \, d\omega = \cos\phi\cos\delta \cdot [2\sin\omega_s]$$

Converting $\omega_s$ from degrees to radians (so $2\omega_s$ becomes $\frac{2\pi\omega_s}{360}$):

$$\overline{H_o} = \frac{24 \times 3600}{2\pi} I_{on} \left[2\omega_s(\text{rad})\sin\phi\sin\delta + 2\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s\right]$$

Since $2\omega_s(\text{rad}) = \frac{2\pi\omega_s(\text{deg})}{360}$ and converting the 24×3600 factor, the result is more conventionally written as:

$$\boxed{\overline{H_o} = \frac{24 \times 3600}{2\pi} I_{on} \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi\omega_s}{360}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]}$$

**In SI units: $\overline{H_o}$ is in J/m²/day**. To convert to **MJ/m²/day**, divide by $10^6$. To convert to **kWh/m²/day**, divide by $3.6 \times 10^6$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cleaner Form (KTU-Preferred):**
> Using $\dfrac{2\pi}{24 \times 3600} = \dfrac{\pi}{12 \times 3600}$, and noting that $\dfrac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \approx 86400/\pi$, the daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface is:
>
> $$\overline{H_o} = \frac{86400}{\pi} I_{on} \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi\omega_s}{360}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right] \quad \text{[J/m}^2/\text{day]}$$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Find the declination angle, zenith angle, altitude angle, and air mass at solar noon for **Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala (φ = 8.5° N)** on **March 21 (n = 80)**.

**Step 1 — Declination (n = 80):**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left(\frac{360(284+80)}{365}\right) = 23.45^\circ \sin\left(\frac{360 \times 364}{365}\right)$$

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin(358.85^\circ) = 23.45^\circ \times (-0.0244) = -0.57^\circ \approx 0^\circ$$

**Step 2 — Hour Angle at Solar Noon:** $\omega = 0^\circ$ (Sun is on the meridian).

**Step 3 — Zenith Angle:**

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(8.5^\circ)\sin(-0.57^\circ) + \cos(8.5^\circ)\cos(-0.57^\circ)\cos(0^\circ)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = (0.1478)(-0.00994) + (0.9890)(0.9999)(1)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = -0.00147 + 0.9889 = 0.9874$$

$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.9874) = 9.18^\circ$$

**Step 4 — Altitude Angle:**

$$\alpha = 90^\circ - 9.18^\circ = 80.82^\circ$$

This is why Kerala receives nearly vertical sunlight on the equinox — the Sun is almost directly overhead.

**Step 5 — Air Mass:**

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(9.18^\circ)} = \frac{1}{0.9874} = 1.013 \approx 1.01$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **At solar noon in Kerala on the equinox, AM ≈ 1.01** — essentially the minimum possible atmospheric path. This is why Thiruvananthapuram has among the highest solar potential in India.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Right Formula

| KTU Question Wording | Required Formula(s) | Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|
| "Calculate the solar declination on day n = X" | Cooper's Equation (Formula 3) | 2–3 |
| "Find the zenith angle at 10 AM solar time" | Formulas 4, 5 | 3–5 |
| "Determine the air mass at time t" | Formulas 5, 9 | 2–3 |
| "Find sunrise/sunset time on day n" | Formulas 3, 8 | 5–7 |
| "Calculate daily extraterrestrial radiation" | Formulas 2, 3, 8, 11 | 7–10 |
| "Find angle of incidence on a tilted surface" | Formula 12 | 5–7 |
| "Calculate extraterrestrial radiation at a given instant" | Formulas 2, 5, 10 | 3–5 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sun-Earth Geometric Angle Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram maps the **functional architecture** of all sun-earth angles and their mathematical relationships. It serves as a complete KTU reference flowchart.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1["DAY NUMBER n"]:::input --> n2["DECLINATION DELTA Cooper Eq 23.45 sin"]
    n3["LATITUDE PHI Location"]:::input --> n5
    n4["HOUR ANGLE OMEGA 15 deg per hour"]:::input --> n5
    n2 --> n5["ZENITH THETA Z cos form spherical trig"]
    n3 --> n5
    n4 --> n5
    n2 --> n6["SUNRISE HOUR ANGLE OMEGA S cos inverse"]
    n3 --> n6
    n6 --> n7["DAY LENGTH N hours"]
    n2 --> n8["ALTITUDE ALPHA 90 minus theta z"]
    n5 --> n8
    n5 --> n9["AZIMUTH GAMMA S derived"]
    n3 --> n9
    n8 --> n9
    n5 --> n10["AIR MASS AM 1 by cos theta z"]
    n2 --> n11["INSTANTANEOUS EXTRATERR Io Ion cos theta z"]
    n3 --> n11
    n4 --> n11
    n2 --> n12["DAILY EXTRATERR Ho 24 by pi integral"]
    n3 --> n12
    n6 --> n12
    n5 --> n13["INCIDENCE ANGLE THETA tilted surface"]
    n3 --> n13
    n4 --> n13
    n2 --> n13
    n1["DAY NUMBER n"]:::input --> n14["ECCENTRICITY Ion Isc 1 plus 0.033 cos"]
    n14 --> n11
    n14 --> n12

    classDef input fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**How to read this diagram:**

- **Inputs** (yellow boxes): Day number, Latitude, Hour Angle — these are the only three independent variables.
- **Primary Output**: Zenith angle — the keystone variable from which altitude, azimuth, air mass, and extraterrestrial radiation are all derived.
- **Daily/Instantaneous**: Two parallel pipelines feed into the daily radiation calculation, requiring the sunrise/sunset hour angle as an additional input.

## 4.2 Decomposition of Solar Radiation at Earth's Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["EXTRATERRESTRIAL RADIATION Ion 1367 W per m2"] --> A2["ATMOSPHERIC INTERACTION Absorption Scattering Reflection"]
    A2 --> A3["BEAM RADIATION Ib Straight from Sun"]
    A2 --> A4["DIFFUSE RADIATION Id Scattered all directions"]
    A2 --> A5["REFLECTED RADIATION Ir From ground albedo rho"]
    A3 --> A6["GLOBAL RADIATION ON TILTED SURFACE IT IbRb IdRd IrRr"]
    A4 --> A6
    A5 --> A6
    A3 --> A7["GLOBAL HORIZONTAL IT Ib plus Id"]
    A4 --> A7
    A1 --> A8["AM0 Spectrum Used for space solar cells"]
    A6 --> A9["PV MODULE / SOLAR COLLECTOR USEFUL ENERGY"]
    A7 --> A9

    style A1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style A2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style A9 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation for KTU Students:**

- The **orange node** is the pristine extraterrestrial input.
- The **blue node** represents the atmosphere as a "lossy filter."
- The **green node** is the useful output for solar engineering.
- The diagram clearly shows why **horizontal surfaces miss the reflected component** (Ir = 0 when β = 0°).

## 4.3 Annual Variation of Solar Declination (Cyclical)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["MAR 21 SPRING EQUINOX n81 delta 0"] --> M2["JUN 21 SUMMER SOLSTICE n172 delta 23.45"]
    M2 --> M3["SEP 23 AUTUMN EQUINOX n266 delta 0"]
    M3 --> M4["DEC 21 WINTER SOLSTICE n355 delta minus 23.45"]
    M4 --> M1
    M1 --> M5["NH SPRING INCREASING DELTA"]
    M2 --> M6["NH SUMMER MAX POSITIVE DELTA"]
    M3 --> M7["NH AUTUMN DECREASING DELTA"]
    M4 --> M8["NH WINTER MAX NEGATIVE DELTA"]

    style M1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style M2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style M3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style M4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
```

This cyclical flowchart helps students remember the **timing and direction** of declination change for the Northern Hemisphere (KTU Kerala's location).

## 4.4 Solar Constant Variation Map (Earth-Sun Distance Effect)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1["MEAN DISTANCE Dse 1.496e11 m"] --> B2["SOLAR CONSTANT Isc 1367 W per m2"]
    B3["PERIHELION JAN 3 Dmin 1.471e11 m"] --> B4["IRRADIANCE Ipmax 1412 W per m2 plus 3.3 percent"]
    B5["APHELION JUL 4 Dmax 1.521e11 m"] --> B6["IRRADIANCE Iamin 1322 W per m2 minus 3.3 percent"]
    B2 --> B7["KTU STANDARD VALUE 1367 W per m2"]
    B4 --> B7
    B6 --> B7
    B3 --> B8["ECCENTRICITY E0 1 plus 0.033 cos 360n 365"]
    B5 --> B8
    B8 --> B9["INSTANTANEOUS Ion Isc times E0"]

    style B2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style B4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style B6 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
```

This block architecture maps the **orbital mechanics** that govern the slight annual variation in extraterrestrial irradiance. KTU students should note that the **±3.3% band** is non-trivial in precision solar collector design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the following terms with their standard values/units:**
**(a)** Solar Constant
**(b)** Declination Angle
**(c)** Air Mass

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Solar Constant (1 Mark):** The solar constant is the rate of solar energy received per unit area on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays, located at the **mean Earth-Sun distance**, **outside the Earth's atmosphere**. **Standard value: 1367 W/m²** (as per WMO 1981).

**(b) Declination Angle (1 Mark):** The declination angle (δ) is the angular position of the Sun north (+) or south (−) of the **equatorial plane**. It varies from **−23.45° to +23.45°** throughout the year due to Earth's axial tilt of 23.45°. **Governed by Cooper's equation:**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$$

**(c) Air Mass (1 Mark):** Air mass (AM) is the ratio of the actual path length of solar radiation through the atmosphere to the path length when the Sun is at the zenith. **Formula:** $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$. **AM 0** = outer space, **AM 1** = Sun at zenith, **AM 1.5** = standard terrestrial spectrum (1000 W/m²).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Distinguish between extraterrestrial radiation and terrestrial radiation. Why is the extraterrestrial value always higher?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Extraterrestrial Radiation (I_on) | Terrestrial Radiation (I_T) |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Outside the Earth's atmosphere | At the Earth's surface (ground level) |
| **Spectrum** | AM 0 spectrum (full Sun output) | AM 1.5 spectrum (filtered) |
| **Magnitude** | 1367 W/m² (perpendicular); varies ±3.3% | 700–1000 W/m² (global horizontal, depending on atmosphere) |
| **Attenuation** | None (no atmosphere) | Yes — absorption, scattering, reflection |
| **Direction** | Always collimated from Sun | Mixed (direct + diffuse + reflected) |

**Why extraterrestrial is always higher (1 Mark):** The Earth's atmosphere acts as a **filter** that absorbs (O₃, H₂O, CO₂), scatters (Rayleigh + Mie), and reflects (clouds) solar radiation. Typical atmospheric transmission is only **50–70%** of the extraterrestrial value, so terrestrial radiation is invariably lower.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A solar engineer is designing a PV plant at Cochin (φ = 10° N). On June 21 (Summer Solstice):**
**(a)** Calculate the declination angle, the hour angle, zenith angle, and altitude angle at **10:00 AM solar time**. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Determine the **daily extraterrestrial radiation** on a horizontal surface for the same day. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Given:** φ = 10° N, n = 172 (June 21), t = 10:00 AM solar time.

#### Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Declination angle (1 Mark)**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left(\frac{360(284+172)}{365}\right) = 23.45^\circ \sin\left(\frac{360 \times 456}{365}\right)$$

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin(449.75^\circ) = 23.45^\circ \sin(89.75^\circ)$$

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \times 0.99998 = 23.45^\circ \approx 23.45^\circ$$

**Step 2: Hour angle (1 Mark)**

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (10 - 12) = 15^\circ \times (-2) = -30^\circ$$

(Negative sign indicates morning — Sun is in the east.)

**Step 3: Zenith angle (3 Marks)**

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(10^\circ)\sin(23.45^\circ) + \cos(10^\circ)\cos(23.45^\circ)\cos(-30^\circ)$$

Computing each term:
- $\sin(10^\circ) = 0.1736$
- $\sin(23.45^\circ) = 0.3979$
- $\cos(10^\circ) = 0.9848$
- $\cos(23.45^\circ) = 0.9174$
- $\cos(-30^\circ) = 0.8660$

$$\cos\theta_z = (0.1736)(0.3979) + (0.9848)(0.9174)(0.8660)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = 0.0691 + 0.7825 = 0.8516$$

$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.8516) = 31.59^\circ$$

**Step 4: Altitude angle (2 Marks)**

$$\alpha = 90^\circ - 31.59^\circ = 58.41^\circ$$

**Final Answers (Part a):** $\delta = 23.45^\circ$, $\omega = -30^\circ$, $\theta_z = 31.59^\circ$, $\alpha = 58.41^\circ$.

#### Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Extraterrestrial irradiance I_on (1 Mark)**

$$I_{on} = 1367 \times \left(1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360 \times 172}{365}\right)\right)$$

$$I_{on} = 1367 \times (1 + 0.033 \cos(169.64^\circ))$$

$$I_{on} = 1367 \times (1 + 0.033 \times (-0.9842)) = 1367 \times (1 - 0.0325) = 1367 \times 0.9675$$

$$I_{on} = 1322.6 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Sunrise/Sunset hour angle (3 Marks)**

$$\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi\tan\delta = -\tan(10^\circ)\tan(23.45^\circ)$$

$$\cos\omega_s = -(0.1763)(0.4335) = -0.0764$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.0764) = 94.38^\circ$$

**Day length:** $N = 2 \times 94.38 / 15 = 12.58$ hours.

**Step 3: Daily extraterrestrial radiation (3 Marks)**

$$\overline{H_o} = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \times I_{on} \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi\omega_s}{360}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$$

Computing the trigonometric bracket:
- $\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s = (0.9848)(0.9174)\sin(94.38^\circ) = 0.9035 \times 0.9971 = 0.9009$
- $\sin\phi\sin\delta = (0.1736)(0.3979) = 0.0691$
- $\frac{2\pi \times 94.38}{360} = 1.6477$ rad

Bracket value:
$$0.9009 + 1.6477 \times 0.0691 = 0.9009 + 0.1138 = 1.0147 \ \text{rad}$$

Prefactor:
$$\frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} = 27501.8 \ \text{s/day}$$

$$\overline{H_o} = 27501.8 \times 1322.6 \times 1.0147 = 3.691 \times 10^7 \ \text{J/m}^2/\text{day}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{H_o} \approx 36.91 \ \text{MJ/m}^2/\text{day} \approx 10.25 \ \text{kWh/m}^2/\text{day}}$$

---

### Question B (Alternative to Question A) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**For a location at latitude φ = 20° N:**
**(a)** On **December 21**, calculate the **declination angle, sunrise/sunset hour angles, and the day length**. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Compute the **instantaneous extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface** at **2:00 PM solar time** on the same day, and determine the **air mass**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Given:** φ = 20° N, n = 355 (December 21), t = 2:00 PM solar time.

#### Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Declination angle (2 Marks)**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left(\frac{360(284+355)}{365}\right) = 23.45^\circ \sin\left(\frac{360 \times 639}{365}\right)$$

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin(630.08^\circ) = 23.45^\circ \sin(630.08 - 360)^\circ = 23.45^\circ \sin(270.08^\circ)$$

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \times (-\cos(0.08^\circ)) = 23.45^\circ \times (-0.9999) = -23.45^\circ$$

**Step 2: Sunrise/Sunset hour angle (3 Marks)**

$$\cos\omega_s = -\tan(20^\circ)\tan(-23.45^\circ) = -[(0.3640)(-0.4335)] = 0.1578$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(0.1578) = 80.92^\circ$$

**Step 3: Day length (2 Marks)**

$$N = \frac{2 \times 80.92}{15} = 10.79 \ \text{hours} \approx 10 \ \text{h} \ 47 \ \text{min}$$

This is the **shortest day** of the year in the Northern Hemisphere — physically consistent.

#### Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Hour angle (1 Mark)**

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (14 - 12) = +30^\circ \ \text{(afternoon)}$$

**Step 2: Zenith angle (3 Marks)**

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(20^\circ)\sin(-23.45^\circ) + \cos(20^\circ)\cos(-23.45^\circ)\cos(30^\circ)$$

Computing:
- $\sin(20^\circ) = 0.3420$, $\sin(-23.45^\circ) = -0.3979$
- $\cos(20^\circ) = 0.9397$, $\cos(-23.45^\circ) = 0.9174$, $\cos(30^\circ) = 0.8660$

$$\cos\theta_z = (0.3420)(-0.3979) + (0.9397)(0.9174)(0.8660)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = -0.1361 + 0.7465 = 0.6104$$

$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.6104) = 52.38^\circ$$

**Step 3: Extraterrestrial radiation (2 Marks)**

First, find I_on (n = 355):

$$I_{on} = 1367 \times \left(1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360 \times 355}{365}\right)\right) = 1367 \times (1 + 0.033 \cos(350.14^\circ))$$

$$I_{on} = 1367 \times (1 + 0.033 \times 0.9848) = 1367 \times 1.0325 = 1411.5 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

Then:

$$I_o = I_{on} \cos\theta_z = 1411.5 \times 0.6104 = 861.6 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Air mass (1 Mark)**

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(52.38^\circ)} = \frac{1}{0.6104} = 1.638$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of δ:** Many students forget that December 21 gives a **negative** declination (−23.45°). Mark deductions: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Hour angle sign convention:** Morning is **negative**, afternoon is **positive**. Confusing this leads to incorrect sunrise/sunset times. **Deduction: 1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the eccentricity correction** (Formula 2) in the daily H_o calculation. Many students directly use 1367 W/m² instead of the corrected I_on. **Deduction: 1 mark**.
> 4. **Radian vs. degree in daily H_o formula:** The hour angle ω_s in the $\dfrac{2\pi\omega_s}{360}$ term must be in **degrees**, but the $\sin\omega_s$ term uses the **degree value of ω_s** directly inside the sine (which internally converts to radians in calculators). Confusion here is the **#1 source of wrong answers**. **Deduction: 2–3 marks**.
> 5. **Not converting final units:** The raw H_o is in J/m²/day. KTU expects **MJ/m²/day** or **kWh/m²/day**. **Deduction: 1 mark**.
> 6. **Writing the noon hour angle as 0°:** This is correct for **solar noon**, but for clock-time noon, the equation of time correction (B = 360(n−81)/365) may be needed. KTU problems typically use **solar time directly**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**

### ⭐ The Five Must-Memorize Constants
1. **Solar Constant (I_sc)** = **1367 W/m²**
2. **Solar Surface Temperature** = 5778 K
3. **Earth's Axial Tilt** = 23.45°
4. **Earth's Rotation Rate** = 15°/hour
5. **Mean Earth-Sun Distance** = 1.496 × 10¹¹ m

### ⭐ The Six Must-Memorize Formulas
1. **I_on** = 1367 × [1 + 0.033 cos(360n/365)]
2. **δ** = 23.45° × sin[360(284+n)/365]
3. **ω** = 15° × (t_solar − 12)
4. **cos θ_z** = sin φ sin δ + cos φ cos δ cos ω
5. **ω_s** = cos⁻¹(−tan φ tan δ)
6. **H_o** = (86400/π) × I_on × [cos φ cos δ sin ω_s + (2πω_s/360) sin φ sin δ]

### ⭐ Key Conceptual Distinctions
- **Extraterrestrial** is **NOT** affected by atmosphere; **Terrestrial** is.
- **AM 0** = outer space; **AM 1.5** = standard terrestrial (Kerala is closer to AM 1).
- **Beam** is collimated (shadows); **Diffuse** is from all sky; **Reflected** is from ground.
- **Declination** is a function of **day of year only** (φ-independent).
- **Hour angle** is a function of **solar time only** (φ-independent).
- **Zenith angle** depends on **all three**: day, time, and latitude.

### ⭐ Common Exam Triggers
- "**Noon**" → ω = 0° → use this to simplify cos θ_z to (φ − δ) for solar altitude.
- "**Equinox**" → δ = 0° → sin δ = 0, cos δ = 1; cos θ_z simplifies to cos φ cos ω.
- "**Solstice**" → δ = ±23.45° (maximum value).
- "**Sunrise/Sunset**" → θ_z = 90° → use ω_s formula directly.

### ⭐ Kerala-Specific Tip
- Thiruvananthapuram (φ = 8.5° N) is **one of the best locations in India** for solar energy because the latitude is small → the Sun is nearly overhead throughout the year.
- For Kochi (φ = 10° N), the optimal **year-round tilt** for a PV panel is approximately **10°** (equal to latitude).
- For **summer optimization**: tilt = φ − 15° ≈ 0° (almost flat).
- For **winter optimization**: tilt = φ + 15° ≈ 25°.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Study Tip:** When solving KTU 2024 Scheme problems, always:
> 1. State the **given data** clearly.
> 2. Identify the **target formula** from the cheat sheet.
> 3. Show **unit conversions** explicitly (degrees ↔ radians for ω_s).
> 4. Compute intermediate values step-by-step.
> 5. Box the **final answer with units**.
>
> This structured approach mirrors KTU's step-marking scheme and guarantees that partial marks are awarded even if the final number is incorrect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruments for Measuring Solar Radiation and Sunshine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET754 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruments for Measuring Solar Radiation and Sunshine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Solar Radiation Measurement?
Solar radiation measurement is the quantitative determination of the **electromagnetic energy** emitted by the Sun that reaches a surface on Earth. It is the **foundational step** of any solar energy engineering design, because without accurate radiation data, sizing a photovoltaic array, a solar thermal collector, or a passive building system is merely guesswork.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMET754 – Module 1):**
> Students must be able to *define, classify, and explain the working principle* of instruments used for measuring solar radiation (pyranometer, pyrheliometer) and sunshine duration (Campbell–Stokes sunshine recorder). Numerical problems based on **Angstrom's equation** and solar geometry are frequently asked in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

### 1.2 Two Distinct Quantities: Irradiance vs. Sunshine
Before listing instruments, we must distinguish two quantities that students *frequently* confuse in exams:

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Solar Irradiance** | $G$ | Instantaneous rate of solar energy received per unit area | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| **Solar Irradiation / Insolation** | $H$ | Total solar energy received per unit area over a specified time | $\text{MJ/m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$ |
| **Sunshine Duration** | $n$ | Number of hours during which the direct solar radiation exceeds a threshold of $\mathbf{120\ W/m^2}$ | hours |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Rainfall vs. Rain Intensity" Picture**
> Imagine standing with a bucket in the rain.
> - **Rain intensity** (mm/hour) is like **solar irradiance** ($G$) — it is a *rate*, measured at one instant.
> - **Total rainfall in a day** (mm/day) is like **solar irradiation** ($H$) — it is an *accumulated quantity* over time.
> - **Sunshine duration** is like noting "for how many hours was it *actually raining hard*?" — not the total time clouds were absent, but the duration the direct sun was strong enough to register on a sensitive paper.
>
> This is precisely why a **pyranometer** captures "rainfall" (energy), while a **Campbell–Stokes recorder** tells you *for how long* the rain was "heavy" (sunshine hours).

### 1.4 The Sun–Earth Energy Context
- **Extraterrestrial radiation on a surface normal to the Sun's rays** (at the top of the atmosphere, on the mean Earth–Sun distance) is called the **Solar Constant**: $G_{sc} = \mathbf{1367\ W/m^2 \approx 1.367\ kW/m^2}$.
- After passing through the atmosphere, radiation reaching the ground is split into:
  1. **Direct (Beam) radiation** $G_b$ — arrives in a straight line from the Sun's disk.
  2. **Diffuse radiation** $G_d$ — scattered by atmosphere, clouds, dust.
  3. **Reflected radiation** $G_r$ — bounced off the ground (albedo effect).
- On a horizontal surface, **Global radiation** is: $G = G_b \cos\theta_z + G_d + G_r$, where $\theta_z$ is the zenith angle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Condition:** WMO (World Meteorological Organization) and ISO 9060 specify that all solar radiation instruments must be calibrated against the **World Radiometric Reference (WRR)** maintained at the World Radiation Center, Davos, Switzerland.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Pyranometer (Solarimeter / ISO 9060 First-Class Instrument)

A **Pyranometer** is the instrument used to measure the **total (global) solar radiation** falling on a horizontal surface, or the **diffuse radiation** if a shading ring/disc is used to block the direct beam.

#### 2.1.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step
1. **Sensor Element:** A thermopile (series of thermocouples) whose hot junctions are coated with a highly absorbent **black paint** (Lambertian absorber) and cold junctions are shielded inside the instrument body.
2. **Absorption:** Solar radiation absorbed by the black surface raises its temperature above the cold junctions.
3. **Seebeck Effect:** A small DC voltage (typically $\mathbf{5\text{–}15\ \mu V}$ per $\text{W/m}^2$) is generated proportional to the temperature difference.
4. **Glass Domes:** One or two concentric **Schott glass domes** of $\approx 50\ \text{mm}$ outer diameter protect the thermopile, filter out long-wave (terrestrial) radiation, and provide a cosine response.
5. **Cosine Response:** The instrument is designed to obey the **Lambert's cosine law** — the output is proportional to the cosine of the angle of incidence.

#### 2.1.2 Classification (ISO 9060 Standard)
| Class | Uncertainty (95% confidence) | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Secondary Standard** | < 1% | Eppley PSP, Kipp & Zonen CM21 |
| **First Class** | < 2% | Kipp & Zonen CM11, Eppley 8-48 |
| **Second Class** | < 5% | Most field instruments |
| **Third Class** | < 10% | Low-cost / educational |

#### 2.1.3 Diffuse vs. Global Measurement
- **Global radiation**: Pyranometer placed horizontally, unobstructed view of entire sky dome.
- **Diffuse radiation only**: A **shadow band** (a metal ring) or a **sun-tracking disc** blocks the direct solar beam from reaching the sensor.

---

### 2.2 The Pyrheliometer

A **Pyrheliometer** measures the **direct (beam) solar radiation** incident on a surface *perpendicular to the solar beam*.

#### 2.2.1 Working Principle
1. **Collimator Tube:** Long tube (typical length 25 cm, aperture angle **$\approx 5.7°$**) that limits the field of view to the Sun's disc and a thin annulus of sky.
2. **Cavity Detector:** A blackened cavity (better than a flat surface) absorbs the beam.
3. **Thermopile:** Measures the temperature difference between the heated cavity and a reference body.
4. **Sun Tracking:** Pyrheliometer MUST be mounted on a **solar tracker** (equatorial mount) to keep its aperture normal to the Sun throughout the day.
5. **Window:** Either an open aperture or a quartz window (to block convective heat loss).

#### 2.2.2 Eppley Normal Incidence Pyrheliometer (NIP)
- World-standard reference instrument.
- Sensitivity: $\approx 8\ \mu\text{V}/(\text{W/m}^2)$.

#### 2.2.3 Absolute Radiometer (Cavity Absolute Pyrheliometer)
- Used by WRR (World Radiometric Reference).
- Operates on **electrical substitution principle**: electrical heating replaces solar heating to keep detector temperature constant; the electrical power equals absorbed solar power.
- This is a *primary standard* — no external calibration needed.

---

### 2.3 The Campbell–Stokes Sunshine Recorder

Used to record **sunshine duration** (in hours), not energy.

#### 2.3.1 Working Principle
1. A solid **glass sphere** of $\approx 10\ \text{cm}$ diameter acts as a converging lens.
2. Sunlight is focused to an intense point on a specially treated **recording card** held in a metal bowl.
3. As the Sun moves, the focused point **burns a trace** along the card.
4. The card is graduated in **hourly intervals**; the length of burn = sunshine duration.
5. **Standard threshold**: The instrument is calibrated such that a burn is recorded when direct normal irradiance exceeds $\mathbf{120\ W/m}^2$ (the WMO-defined sunshine threshold).

#### 2.3.2 Card Types
- **Long curved card** (summer, long days in temperate zones)
- **Short curved card** (winter)
- **Straight card** (equatorial regions)

---

### 2.4 Angstrom's Empirical Equation (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)
Used to estimate **average daily global radiation** on a horizontal surface when only sunshine hours are recorded.

$$\frac{H}{H_o} = a + b\left(\frac{n}{N}\right)$$

where:
- $H$ = monthly average daily global radiation on horizontal surface ($\text{MJ/m}^2$/day)
- $H_o$ = monthly average daily extraterrestrial radiation on horizontal surface ($\text{MJ/m}^2$/day)
- $n$ = monthly average daily **actual** sunshine hours
- $N$ = monthly average daily **maximum possible** sunshine hours (day length)
- $a, b$ = empirical constants (depend on location, climate; typical: $a = 0.25$, $b = 0.50$ for many Indian stations)

---

### 2.5 Extraterrestrial Radiation $H_o$ Formula
$$H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}\, G_{sc}\, \left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n_d}{365}\right) \left(\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi \omega_s}{180}\sin\phi \sin\delta\right)$$

where:
- $G_{sc} = 1367\ \text{W/m}^2$
- $n_d$ = day of year (1 to 365)
- $\phi$ = latitude (degrees)
- $\delta$ = solar declination (degrees)
- $\omega_s$ = sunset hour angle (degrees) = $\cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar constant | $G_{sc} = 1367$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 2 | Global on horizontal | $G = G_b\cos\theta_z + G_d + G_r$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 3 | Day length | $N = \frac{2}{15}\cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$ | hours |
| 4 | Sunset hour angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$ | degrees |
| 5 | Solar declination | $\delta = 23.45 \sin\!\left(\frac{360(284+n_d)}{365}\right)$ | degrees |
| 6 | Extraterrestrial $H_o$ | See §2.5 above | $\text{MJ/m}^2$/day |
| 7 | **Angstrom's equation** | $\frac{H}{H_o} = a + b\frac{n}{N}$ | dimensionless ratio |
| 8 | 1 Langley | $1\ \text{Ly} = 1\ \text{cal/cm}^2 = 4.186\ \text{J/cm}^2$ | non-SI, but in use |
| 9 | Conversion | $1\ \text{kWh/m}^2 = 3.6\ \text{MJ/m}^2$ | energy conversion |
| 10 | Air mass | $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Angstrom's correlation is the **backbone of solar resource assessment** in countries like India where pyranometric data is sparse but sunshine-hour data (from Campbell–Stokes recorders, maintained by IMD — India Meteorological Department) is abundant. The constants $a$ and $b$ are obtained by linear regression of measured $H/H_o$ against $n/N$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Extraterrestrial Daily Radiation $H_o$ on a Horizontal Surface

**Starting point — instantaneous extraterrestrial radiation on a surface normal to the beam:**
$$G_{on} = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\frac{360 n_d}{365}\right)$$

**Step 1: Resolve to a horizontal surface.**
The instantaneous radiation on a horizontal surface at the top of the atmosphere is the beam projected by the cosine of the zenith angle:
$$G_{o} = G_{on}\cos\theta_z$$

**Step 2: Relate zenith angle to hour angle.**
The spherical-trigonometric identity for the zenith angle:
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

**Step 3: Integrate over a day.**
The total daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface is the time integral of $G_o$ from sunrise ($\omega = -\omega_s$) to sunset ($\omega = +\omega_s$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_o &= \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} G_{on}\cos\theta_z\;\frac{86400}{2\pi}\, d\omega \\[4pt]
&= \frac{86400}{2\pi}\, G_{on}\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s}\!\left(\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega\right)d\omega \\[4pt]
&= \frac{86400}{2\pi}\, G_{on}\left[\sin\phi\sin\delta\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s}\!d\omega + \cos\phi\cos\delta\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s}\!\cos\omega\,d\omega\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{86400}{2\pi}\, G_{on}\left[2\omega_s\sin\phi\sin\delta + 2\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Convert $\omega_s$ from degrees to radians.**
Since $\omega_s$ is conventionally in degrees, the integral bounds need conversion, yielding the canonical expression:

$$
H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}\, G_{sc}\left(1+0.033\cos\frac{360 n_d}{365}\right)\left(\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right)
$$

This is the **final KTU-ready formula** for $H_o$ in $\text{J/m}^2$/day. To convert to $\text{MJ/m}^2$/day, divide by $10^6$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Angstrom's Equation

> **Problem (Typical 14-Mark KTU Style):**
> For a station at latitude $\phi = 13°\text{N}$, the monthly average daily global radiation on a horizontal surface in July ($n_d \approx 182$) is $H = 18.0\ \text{MJ/m}^2/\text{day}$. The actual sunshine hours $n = 7.0$ h. Empirical constants are $a = 0.30$, $b = 0.50$. Given the mean day length $N = 12.5$ h, calculate the extraterrestrial radiation $H_o$ and verify the Angstrom correlation.

**Step 1: Solar declination on $n_d = 182$.**

$$
\delta = 23.45 \sin\!\left(\frac{360(284+182)}{365}\right) = 23.45 \sin\!\left(\frac{360 \times 466}{365}\right) = 23.45 \sin(459.45°)
$$

$$
459.45° - 360° = 99.45° \quad\Rightarrow\quad \delta = 23.45 \sin(99.45°) = 23.45 \times 0.9866 = 23.13°
$$

**Step 2: Sunset hour angle.**

$$
\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\!\left(-\tan\phi\tan\delta\right) = \cos^{-1}\!\left(-\tan 13° \tan 23.13°\right)
$$

$$
\tan 13° = 0.2309,\quad \tan 23.13° = 0.4276 \quad\Rightarrow\quad -\tan\phi\tan\delta = -0.0987
$$

$$
\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.0987) = 95.66° \approx 1.670\ \text{rad}
$$

**Step 3: Correction factor for the Earth's elliptical orbit.**

$$
1 + 0.033 \cos\!\left(\frac{360 \times 182}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033 \cos(179.5°) = 1 + 0.033 \times (-0.99996) \approx 0.967
$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the $H_o$ formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_o &= \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}\times 1367 \times 0.967 \times \left[\cos 13°\cos 23.13°\sin 95.66° + \frac{\pi \times 95.66}{180}\sin 13°\sin 23.13°\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{86400}{\pi}\times 1367 \times 0.967 \times \left[0.9744 \times 0.9195 \times 0.9951 + 1.670 \times 0.2250 \times 0.3927\right] \\[4pt]
&= 27516.7 \times 1367 \times 0.967 \times \left[0.8916 + 0.1475\right] \\[4pt]
&= 3.638 \times 10^7 \times 1.0391 \\[4pt]
&= 3.78 \times 10^7\ \text{J/m}^2/\text{day} = 37.8\ \text{MJ/m}^2/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Verify Angstrom's equation.**

$$
H = H_o\left(a + b\frac{n}{N}\right) = 37.8 \times \left(0.30 + 0.50 \times \frac{7.0}{12.5}\right) = 37.8 \times (0.30 + 0.28) = 37.8 \times 0.58 = 21.9\ \text{MJ/m}^2/\text{day}
$$

The model predicts $21.9$ vs. the measured $18.0\ \text{MJ/m}^2/\text{day}$, indicating a reasonable fit; the residual arises from local atmospheric turbidity, cloud cover variability, and the simplicity of the linear model.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Pyrheliometer vs. Pyranometer vs. Campbell–Stokes)

| Feature | Pyrheliometer | Pyranometer | Campbell–Stokes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Measures** | Direct normal beam $G_b$ | Global $G$ or diffuse $G_d$ | Sunshine duration $n$ |
| **Quantity type** | Irradiance ($\text{W/m}^2$) | Irradiance ($\text{W/m}^2$) | Duration (hours) |
| **Surface orientation** | Normal to sun (tracked) | Horizontal (stationary) | Equatorial-mount fixed sphere |
| **Spectral range** | ~ 0.3 – 4 $\mu$m | ~ 0.3 – 2.8 $\mu$m (Schott glass) | Visible + near-IR (burns paper) |
| **Output** | Voltage ($\mu$V) | Voltage ($\mu$V) | Burn trace on paper |
| **Threshold** | None (absolute) | None (absolute) | $\mathbf{120\ W/m^2}$ direct beam |
| **Cost & complexity** | High + needs tracker | Moderate | Lowest, no electronics |
| **KTU exam relevance** | Medium | High | High |

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model QP, May 2024 Style]**
*Distinguish between a pyranometer and a pyrheliometer. Mention the quantity each one measures and whether a sun tracker is required.*

**Model Answer:**
A **pyranometer** measures the **global solar irradiance** ($G$, in $\text{W/m}^2$) — i.e., the sum of direct, diffuse, and reflected radiation — on a **horizontal** surface from the **entire sky hemisphere** ($2\pi$ sr field of view). It does **not** need a sun tracker; it is installed stationary with a level bubble.

A **pyrheliometer** measures the **direct (beam) normal irradiance** ($G_b$, in $\text{W/m}^2$) — i.e., the radiation arriving from a narrow solid angle of about $\mathbf{5.7°}$ around the Sun's disc — on a surface **perpendicular to the solar beam**. Because the Sun moves continuously, the pyrheliometer **must be mounted on a sun tracker** (equatorial mount) so that the collimator axis stays aligned with the solar vector throughout the day.

*(3 marks — awarded for clear distinction, correct units, and mention of tracker requirement.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model QP, Dec 2023 Style]**
*Explain the working principle of a Campbell–Stokes sunshine recorder. What is the WMO threshold for recording sunshine?*

**Model Answer:**
The Campbell–Stokes sunshine recorder consists of a solid **glass sphere** of about $10\ \text{cm}$ diameter mounted in a spherical metal bowl that holds a specially graduated paper card. The sphere acts as a converging lens, focusing the direct sunlight to a point on the card. As the Sun's position changes, the focal point moves and **burns a trace** along the card. The card is divided into hourly markings, and the **length of the burn** corresponds to the **sunshine duration** in hours.

The **WMO-defined threshold** for recording sunshine is a **direct normal solar irradiance of $\mathbf{120\ W/m^2}$** — only when the focused intensity is strong enough to char the treated paper is sunshine "recorded."

*(3 marks — 2 for working principle, 1 for the 120 W/m² threshold.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Module 1 Choice A]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a **thermopile-type pyranometer**. How is it used to measure **diffuse radiation only**?  *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **extraterrestrial daily radiation $H_o$** on a horizontal surface. State clearly the meaning and unit of every variable.  *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution to (a):

**Construction:**
A thermopile pyranometer consists of:
1. A **blackened thermopile sensor** at the base — typically a series of **manganin–constantan** or **chromel–constantan** thermocouples arranged as a circular disc. The **hot junctions** are coated with a highly absorbing (Lambertian) **black paint** (e.g., 3M Nextel Velvet); the **cold junctions** are bonded to the instrument body (a heat sink at ambient temperature).
2. **Two concentric glass domes** (Schott WG295, ~ 50 mm OD) — these (i) protect the thermopile from wind and rain, (ii) filter out terrestrial long-wave radiation (cut-off ≈ 2.8 $\mu$m), and (iii) enforce the **cosine response** of the instrument.
3. A **leveling base** with a bubble level, **desiccant** (silica gel) to prevent internal condensation, and a **cable gland** leading to the data logger.

**Working Principle:**
Solar radiation (0.3 – 2.8 $\mu$m) passes through the domes and is absorbed by the black surface of the thermopile. The hot junctions heat up, while the cold junctions remain at ambient. The temperature difference generates a small DC voltage (Seebeck EMF) **proportional to the irradiance** — typically 5 – 15 $\mu\text{V}$ per $\text{W/m}^2$. A calibration factor (sensitivity constant, in $\mu\text{V}/(\text{W/m}^2)$) is applied to convert the voltage to irradiance. The instrument's cosine response ensures that radiation from oblique angles is weighted correctly.

**Measuring Diffuse Radiation Only:**
To measure only the diffuse component, the direct solar beam must be excluded. This is done by:
- **(i) Shadow-band method:** A circular metal ring of width equal to the Sun's angular diameter (~ $0.5°$) plus the circumsolar scattering angle is mounted around the pyranometer, oriented such that it casts a shadow over the thermopile at all times of the year. The band is adjusted manually or automatically, and a small geometric correction factor (typically 1.05 – 1.20) is applied to compensate for the sky portion blocked by the band.
- **(ii) Sun-tracking disc (shading disc) method:** A small black disc mounted on an arm attached to a solar tracker is positioned to continuously block only the Sun's disc from the pyranometer sensor. This is more accurate and used in research-grade stations.

*(7 marks — 3 for sketch/construction, 2 for principle, 2 for diffuse method.)*

##### Model Solution to (b):

The detailed derivation is given in **§3.1** of these notes. The student is expected to start from $G_{on} = G_{sc}(1 + 0.033\cos(360 n_d/365))$, apply $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$, integrate from $-\omega_s$ to $+\omega_s$, and arrive at:

$$
H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}\, G_{sc}\left(1+0.033\cos\frac{360 n_d}{365}\right)\left(\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right)
$$

with sunset hour angle:
$$
\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)
$$

and declination:
$$
\delta = 23.45 \sin\!\left(\frac{360(284+n_d)}{365}\right)
$$

All variables must be defined (latitude $\phi$ in degrees, declination $\delta$ in degrees, hour angle $\omega$ in degrees, day number $n_d$); final $H_o$ is in $\text{J/m}^2$/day (or $\text{MJ/m}^2$/day after dividing by $10^6$).

*(7 marks — 2 for setup, 3 for integration, 1 for final expression, 1 for variable definitions.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Module 1 Choice B — Alternative]

**(a)** What is **Angstrom's empirical equation** for estimating global solar radiation? Define each term, and explain how the constants $a$ and $b$ are determined experimentally.  *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A station located at latitude $\phi = 23°\text{N}$ records an average sunshine duration of $n = 8.5$ h/day for the month of September ($n_d = 258$). Using Angstrom's equation with $a = 0.28$ and $b = 0.52$, and given that the day length $N = 12.1$ h and the extraterrestrial daily radiation $H_o = 32.4\ \text{MJ/m}^2/\text{day}$, calculate the **average daily global radiation $H$** on a horizontal surface. Comment on the result.  *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution to (a):

**Angstrom's Equation:**
$$
\frac{H}{H_o} = a + b\left(\frac{n}{N}\right)
$$

**Definitions of terms:**
- $H$ — monthly average **daily global solar radiation** on a horizontal surface at the location ($\text{MJ/m}^2$/day).
- $H_o$ — monthly average **daily extraterrestrial radiation** on a horizontal surface at the same latitude and time of year ($\text{MJ/m}^2$/day).
- $n$ — monthly average **daily actual sunshine hours** recorded by a Campbell–Stokes or pyrheliometric sunshine switch.
- $N$ — monthly average **daily maximum possible sunshine hours** (i.e., the day length from sunrise to sunset) computed from solar geometry, $N = (2/15)\omega_s$ in hours.
- $a, b$ — **empirical regression constants** specific to the location.

**Determination of $a$ and $b$:**
1. At the station, collect **at least one year** (ideally several years) of simultaneous measurements: $H$ (from pyranometer), $n$ (from sunshine recorder), and $N$ (computed from latitude and day number).
2. Compute $H_o$ for each day/month from the latitude and declination formula.
3. Plot the **clearness index** $H/H_o$ on the y-axis against the **sunshine fraction** $n/N$ on the x-axis.
4. Fit a **straight line** by least-squares regression. The **y-intercept is $a$** (representing diffuse radiation on a fully overcast day) and the **slope is $b$** (representing the contribution of the variable direct-beam component as sunshine increases).
5. Typical values for tropical Indian stations: $a \in [0.20, 0.35]$, $b \in [0.40, 0.60]$.

*(7 marks — 2 for equation, 2 for terms, 3 for procedure to determine $a, b$.)*

##### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Compute the sunshine fraction.**
$$
\frac{n}{N} = \frac{8.5}{12.1} = 0.7025
$$

**Step 2: Compute the linear combination $a + b(n/N)$.**
$$
a + b\frac{n}{N} = 0.28 + 0.52 \times 0.7025 = 0.28 + 0.3653 = 0.6453
$$

**Step 3: Compute $H$.**
$$
H = H_o \left(a + b\frac{n}{N}\right) = 32.4 \times 0.6453 = 20.91\ \text{MJ/m}^2/\text{day}
$$

**Step 4: Comment.**
The value $H \approx 20.9\ \text{MJ/m}^2/\text{day}$ is in the expected range for a tropical Indian station in September. The clearness index $H/H_o = 0.645$ indicates a **moderately clear sky** — characteristic of post-monsoon conditions. The fraction of possible sunshine achieved is $70.3\%$, consistent with partly cloudy skies typical of the retreating monsoon. For PV system sizing, the **average daily insolation of $20.9\ \text{MJ/m}^2/\text{day}$** corresponds to a peak-sun-hours equivalent of $20.9/3.6 \approx 5.8$ h/day.

*(7 marks — 2 for substitution, 2 for arithmetic, 1 for unit sanity check, 2 for engineering comment.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the cosine factor for a horizontal surface.** Many students write $H_o$ as if the Earth received $G_{sc}$ constantly at the top of the atmosphere. The $86400/\pi$ factor from the time-integration is the single most-skipped step; expect to lose **2 marks** if it is missing.
> 2. **Mixing up $n_d$ (day of year, 1–365) with $N$ (day length in hours).** A very common error.
> 3. **Confusing $G_b$ (beam on a normal surface) with $G_b \cos\theta_z$ (beam on a horizontal surface).** The pyrheliometer measures the former; the pyranometer's direct-beam contribution is the latter.
> 4. **Declination sign errors** — declination is **positive in the Northern hemisphere summer** and **negative in winter**. A sign slip here propagates into $\omega_s$ and ruins the entire $H_o$ calculation.
> 5. **Not converting hour-angle from degrees to radians** (or vice-versa) in the integration. Use $\omega_s$ in **degrees** in the $H_o$ formula as given above (the $\pi/180$ inside the second term takes care of it).
> 6. **Writing $H$ in $\text{kWh/m}^2$ without converting to $\text{MJ/m}^2$** (or the reverse) when using the given $H_o$. Always show units and conversions explicitly.
> 7. **Stating that a pyranometer needs a sun tracker.** It does **not**. The pyrheliometer is the one that needs tracking. This is a frequently-tested 1-mark trap.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Three main instruments, three different physical quantities:**
  - **Pyranometer** → Global irradiance $G$ (W/m²), horizontal, stationary, 2π sr view.
  - **Pyrheliometer** → Direct normal beam $G_b$ (W/m²), perpendicular to sun, needs tracker, ~5.7° view.
  - **Campbell–Stokes** → Sunshine duration $n$ (hours), 120 W/m² threshold, glass sphere burns paper.
- **Solar constant** = $\mathbf{1367\ W/m}^2$; the value $\mathbf{1361}$ is also accepted (latest WMO value is 1361).
- **Pyranometer construction:** thermopile + black absorber + cold junction + two glass domes + cosine response.
- **Pyrheliometer construction:** collimator tube + cavity detector + thermopile + tracker.
- **Cavity absolute pyrheliometer** = primary standard; uses **electrical substitution**.
- **Campbell–Stokes** is the **only instrument without any electrical output** — purely optical/mechanical.
- **Angstrom's equation** $\dfrac{H}{H_o} = a + b\dfrac{n}{N}$ — linear in $n/N$ — is the **most-asked empirical model** in KTU Module 1 numericals.
- **$H_o$ formula** integrates $G_{on}\cos\theta_z$ over a day — derive it once, memorize it, and apply with the correct cosine-correction factor.
- **Declination $\delta$** varies between $-23.45°$ (Dec 21, winter solstice) and $+23.45°$ (Jun 21, summer solstice).
- **Sunset hour angle** $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$ — symmetric about solar noon.
- **Day length** $N = \dfrac{2\omega_s}{15}$ in hours.
- **Air mass** $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$ — at zenith, $AM=1$ (sea-level standard); at horizon, $AM \to \infty$.
- **Calibration** of all instruments is referenced to the **World Radiometric Reference (WRR)**, Davos.
- **Units conversions:** $1\ \text{kWh/m}^2 = 3.6\ \text{MJ/m}^2$; $1\ \text{Langley} = 1\ \text{cal/cm}^2 = 4.186\ \text{J/cm}^2 = 0.04186\ \text{MJ/m}^2$.
- **Engineering takeaway:** A pyranometer is a *watt-meter for sunlight*; a pyrheliometer is a *watt-meter for direct sunlight*; a Campbell–Stokes is a *clock for sunlight*. Knowing the difference is the single biggest differentiator in KTU viva and written exams.

# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Solar Thermal Collectors — Foundational Definition

A **Solar Thermal Collector** is a device designed to absorb incident solar radiation, convert it into thermal energy (heat), and transfer that heat to a working fluid (air, water, oil, or molten salt) for direct use or further conversion. They are the workhorse components of all solar heating and solar thermal power generation systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A solar thermal collector is a heat exchanger that transforms incoming short-wave solar irradiance into useful internal energy of a fluid, characterized by its **absorptance (α)**, **emittance (ε)**, and **overall heat loss coefficient (U_L)**.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Greenhouse Bucket"
Imagine a black-painted bucket of water left in the sun. The black paint (absorber) catches sunlight, the water stores the warmth, and a transparent glass cover (glazing) traps the heat like a greenhouse. A **flat plate collector** is simply an engineered, leak-proof, insulated version of that bucket with tubes to carry the heated water away. A **solar concentrator** is like using a magnifying glass to focus many buckets' worth of sunlight onto a single small pot — making it far hotter.

---

## 1.2 Flat Plate Collector (FPC) — Definition

A **Flat Plate Collector (FPC)** is a non-concentrating, stationary solar thermal device that captures both **beam and diffuse** solar radiation on a flat absorber surface, producing low-to-medium temperature heat (typically **30 °C to 100 °C**, with advanced designs reaching **200 °C**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** FPCs are the most widely deployed solar collectors globally — used in domestic water heating, space heating, and industrial process heat.

### Major Components of an FPC
1. **Glazing Cover(s):** 1–3 sheets of low-iron tempered glass; reduces convective & radiative losses.
2. **Absorber Plate:** Metallic (Cu, Al) plate coated with **selective coating** (α ≈ 0.95, ε ≈ 0.05).
3. **Tubes / Risers:** Carry working fluid through/under the absorber.
4. **Insulation:** Back & side insulation (mineral wool, PUF) to suppress rear losses.
5. **Frame & Casing:** Weather-tight enclosure, typically anodized aluminum.

---

## 1.3 Solar Concentrators — Definition

A **Solar Concentrator** is an optical device that focuses sunlight from a large **aperture area (A_a)** onto a small **receiver/absorber area (A_r)**, raising the **flux intensity** and thus achieving higher working temperatures.

**Concentration Ratio (C)** is the governing metric:

$$C = \frac{A_a}{A_r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Higher concentration ratios yield higher temperatures but require **solar tracking** and can only utilize **direct (beam) radiation**, not diffuse.

### 1.3.1 Tracking Concentrators (Focusing Type)
These require continuous sun-tracking because they image sunlight to a point or line.

| Type | Geometry | Concentration Ratio | Receiver Type | Temperature Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Parabolic Trough (PTC)** | Linear parabolic mirror | 15–45 | Line (tube) | 50–400 °C |
| **Parabolic Dish (PD)** | 3D paraboloid | 600–2000 | Point (Stirling engine) | 250–1500 °C |
| **Solar Tower (Heliostat)** | Field of mirrors → central receiver | 300–1500 | Point (cavity) | 500–2000 °C |
| **Linear Fresnel Reflector (LFR)** | Multiple flat/mildly curved strips | 10–40 | Line (tube, fixed) | 50–300 °C |

### 1.3.2 Non-Tracking (Static) Concentrators
These achieve moderate concentration without moving parts.

| Type | Mechanism | Concentration Ratio | Acceptance Angle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compound Parabolic Concentrator (CPC)** | Two parabolic reflectors | 3–10 | ± 10° to ± 30° |
| **V-Trough** | Two flat mirrors forming V | 2–4 | ± 5° to ± 10° |
| **Fresnel Lens (Stationary)** | Flat lens with concentric grooves | 50–500 (theoretical) | Very narrow |

---

## 1.4 Solar Energy Storage Systems — Definition

**Solar Energy Storage** is the capture of thermal or electrical energy for release when the sun is unavailable (night, cloudy periods). Three physical mechanisms dominate:

1. **Sensible Heat Storage (SHS):** Energy stored by raising temperature of a medium (water, rock, sand, molten salt).
2. **Latent Heat Storage (LHS):** Energy absorbed/released during phase change (e.g., paraffin wax, salt hydrates) at nearly constant temperature.
3. **Thermochemical Storage (TCS):** Energy stored in endothermic/exothermic reversible chemical reactions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Sensible heat storage in **molten salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃)** is the most commercially deployed system in Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plants.

---

## 1.5 Solar Thermal Power Plant — Definition

A **Solar Thermal Power Plant** (also called **Concentrated Solar Power, CSP**) converts solar energy into electricity by first producing high-temperature heat via a solar concentrator, then driving a conventional thermodynamic cycle (Rankine, Brayton, or Stirling).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** CSP ≠ Photovoltaic. CSP uses **heat as an intermediate**, allowing easy integration with thermal storage. PV converts sunlight **directly** to electricity.

---

## 1.6 Solar Pond — Definition

A **Solar Pond** is a large, shallow saline water body engineered to suppress natural convection, allowing the lower brine layers to become hot (70–95 °C) and act as a thermal energy reservoir. It is a unique combination of **collector + storage** in a single body.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Solar pond is the only solar technology that combines **collection and storage in a single physical unit** — no separate collector or storage tank needed.

---

## 1.7 Solar Photovoltaic System — Definition

A **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) System** converts sunlight directly into electricity using the **photovoltaic effect** discovered by Becquerel (1839) and operationalized in semiconductor p–n junctions (Bell Labs, 1954, efficiency 6%).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** Students must clearly differentiate **PV (solid-state, direct)** from **Solar Thermal (fluid-based, indirect)** in the exam.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Solar Coin Sorter"
Think of sunlight as a mixture of energy "coins" of different sizes. A **flat plate collector** is like a wide scoop catching all coins (even small ones = diffuse). A **solar concentrator** is like a funnel — it only accepts the large coins (direct beam) but can deliver many of them at once to a small target. A **PV cell** is an automated coin sorter that converts each accepted coin directly into a different kind of currency (electricity) without any middleman (heat).

### Standard Solar Metrics (KTU 2024)
- **Solar Constant (G_sc): 1367 W/m²** (extraterrestrial, AM 0)
- **AM 1.5 Global Standard: 1000 W/m²** (Standard Test Conditions, STC)
- **1 Sun = 1000 W/m²** (terrestrial reference irradiance)

# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Flat Plate Collector — Operating Principle (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1: Radiation Incidence**
Beam + diffuse solar radiation strikes the **transmissive glazing** (τ). A fraction is reflected, the rest (τ·G_T) passes inward.

**Step 2: Absorption by Plate**
The absorber plate absorbs a fraction (α) of transmitted radiation, gaining internal energy.

**Step 3: Heat Transfer to Fluid**
Absorbed heat is conducted to the **tube/header** and convected to the working fluid:
$$Q_u = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{fo} - T_{fi})$$

**Step 4: Heat Losses**
- **Top loss** (U_t) through glazing by convection + IR radiation.
- **Bottom loss** (U_b) through insulation.
- **Edge loss** (U_e) through side walls.
- **Overall loss coefficient:** $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$ (typical: 3–6 W/m²·K)

**Step 5: Net Useful Heat Gain (Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MOST IMPORTANT FORMULA IN MODULE 2 — KTU 2024**

$$Q_u = A_c \cdot F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$$

where $S = (\tau \alpha)_e \cdot G_T$ is the absorbed radiation per unit area, and $F_R$ is the **heat removal factor** (0.6–0.9 typically).

**Step 6: Collector Efficiency**
$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \cdot G_T} = F_R (\tau \alpha)_e - F_R U_L \frac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_T}$$

This is a **linear equation** in $(T_{fi} - T_a)/G_T$, plotted on the standard **Hottel–Whillier plot** with slope $-F_R U_L$ and intercept $F_R (\tau \alpha)_e$.

---

## 2.2 Concentrating Collectors — Theoretical Framework

**Why concentrate?** To reach temperatures beyond the FPC limit (~100 °C) where heat-to-electricity conversion becomes thermodynamically efficient. **Carnot efficiency** scales with $T_{hot}/T_{cold}$:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$$

### 2.2.1 Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC)
A **linear parabolic mirror** reflects parallel sun rays onto a focal line where a **selectively coated absorber tube** runs. Working fluid: synthetic oil (Therminol VP-1) up to 400 °C, or molten salt up to 565 °C.

### 2.2.2 Parabolic Dish
A **3D paraboloid dish** tracks the sun in two axes, focusing light onto a point receiver coupled to a **Stirling engine**. Highest thermal-to-electrical efficiency of any CSP (~31.25% achieved by SES/Sandia).

### 2.2.3 Solar Tower (Central Receiver)
A field of independently-tracked flat mirrors (**heliostats**) reflects sunlight onto a **central receiver** atop a tower. Working fluid can be water/steam, molten salt, or air.

### 2.2.4 Linear Fresnel Reflector (LFR)
Many long, slightly-curved mirror strips track the sun (single axis), focusing light onto a **fixed linear receiver** above. Cheaper than PTC but slightly lower optical efficiency.

### 2.2.5 Compound Parabolic Concentrator (CPC)
Two **truncated parabolas** facing each other. Theoretical concentration up to $C_{max} = 1/\sin \theta_a$, where $\theta_a$ is the half-acceptance angle. CPCs are **stationary** if designed for a full seasonal sun-path acceptance.

### 2.2.6 V-Trough
Two flat mirrors at angle 2φ form a V. Concentration: $C = 1/\sin\phi$. Small, low-cost, used for cooking (e.g., Scheffler dishes nearby).

---

## 2.3 Solar Energy Storage — Mechanism Analysis

| Storage Type | Energy Equation | Typical Media | Energy Density (kJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensible (water) | $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ | Water, rock, sand, molten salt | 4–85 (water 4–80) |
| Sensible (molten salt) | $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ | 60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃ | ~150 (ΔT = 200 K) |
| Latent (PCM) | $Q = m L$ | Paraffin, salt hydrates | 200–300 |
| Thermochemical | $Q = m \Delta H_{rxn}$ | CaO/Ca(OH)₂, metal hydrides | 800–3000 |

**Sensible Heat — Detailed:**
$$Q_S = m c_p (T_{max} - T_{min})$$

**Latent Heat — Detailed:**
$$Q_L = m L_f \quad \text{(fusion)}$$

**Overall Storage Capacity:**
$$Q_{total} = m c_p \Delta T + m L + m \Delta H_{chem}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-World Utility:** The **Gemasolar Plant (Spain)** — a 24-hour molten-salt tower plant — uses 15 hours of thermal storage, making it the world's first baseload CSP plant.

---

## 2.4 Solar Pond — Theoretical Basis

A salt-gradient solar pond has three zones:
1. **Upper Convective Zone (UCZ)** — fresh/low-salinity water (transparent, ~20 °C).
2. **Non-Convective Gradient Zone (NGZ)** — salt concentration increases with depth, suppressing convection.
3. **Lower Convective Zone (LCZ)** — saturated brine, hot (70–95 °C), acts as both collector and storage.

**Why the gradient suppresses convection:** The density gradient $\partial \rho / \partial z$ from dissolved salt counteracts the buoyancy from heating. Stable if the **Rayleigh number** is below critical.

**Efficiency of Solar Pond (first law):**
$$\eta_{pond} = \frac{\dot{m} c_p (T_{LCZ} - T_{UCZ})}{G_T \cdot A_{pond}}$$

---

## 2.5 Solar Photovoltaic — Equivalent Circuit & Equations

A PV cell is modeled as a **current source in parallel with a diode** with series resistance $R_s$ and shunt resistance $R_{sh}$.

**Ideal diode equation:**
$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q(V + I R_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

where:
- $I_L$ = photogenerated current (A)
- $I_0$ = diode saturation current (A)
- $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann)
- $n$ = ideality factor (1–2)

**Fill Factor:**
$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

**Cell Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{PV} = \frac{P_{max}}{G_T \cdot A_c} = \frac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G_T \cdot A_c}$$

---

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_{sc} = 1367$ | Solar constant | W/m² | Fixed |
| 2 | $Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)]$ | FPC useful heat (Hottel–Whillier) | W | — |

| 9 | $I = I_L - I_0[\exp(\frac{qV}{nkT}) - 1]$ | PV diode eqn. (ideal) | A | — |
