# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation for FPC

### Starting Energy Balance
The absorbed solar flux $S$ on the absorber plate equals useful heat + all top, bottom, and edge losses:

$$S = \frac{Q_u}{A_c F_R} + U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)$$

### Step 1 — Definition of Heat Removal Factor
The heat removal factor $F_R$ accounts for the fact that the fluid temperature rises gradually as it flows through the risers, not reaching the maximum plate temperature.

**Definition:**
$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} c_p (T_{fo} - T_{fi})}{A_c [S - U_L (T_{pm} - T_a)]}$$

where $T_{pm}$ is the mean plate temperature.

**Step 1A — Rearranging for Useful Heat:**
$$Q_u = \dot{m} c_p (T_{fo} - T_{fi})$$

**Step 1B — Defining Maximum Possible Gain** (when fluid = plate temp):
$$Q_{u,max} = A_c F_R [S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)]$$

### Step 2 — Substitution into Energy Balance
The plate loses energy by:
- Conduction into the fluid (useful).
- Convection + radiation upward ($U_t$).
- Conduction downward through insulation ($U_b$).
- Edge loss ($U_e$).

Therefore per unit area:
$$S = \frac{Q_u}{A_c} + U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)$$

### Step 3 — Final Useful Heat Equation
Solving for $Q_u$:

$$Q_u = A_c [S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)]$$

But because the fluid does not perfectly match the plate temperature, we multiply by $F_R$:

$$\boxed{Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)]}$$

### Step 4 — Instantaneous Efficiency

$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c G_T}$$

Substituting:

$$\eta_c = F_R \left[ (\tau \alpha)_e - U_L \frac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_T} \right]$$

This is a **straight line** with:
- **Y-intercept** $= F_R (\tau \alpha)_e$ (maximum efficiency, no losses)
- **Slope** $= -F_R U_L$ (losses increase with temperature rise)
- **X-intercept** (stagnation condition) = $\dfrac{(\tau \alpha)_e}{U_L}$ — the "stagnation temperature rise."

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Concentration Ratio for a 2D CPC

### Geometric Setup
A CPC consists of two parabolic reflectors with their foci at the edges of the absorber, and axes tilted at the half-acceptance angle $\theta_a$.

### Step 1 — Acceptance Angle Theorem
For any static concentrator, the maximum geometric concentration ratio is:

$$C_{max} = \frac{1}{\sin \theta_a}$$

### Step 2 — Derivation from Optical Invariant (Etendue Conservation)

For a 2D system, optical throughput (etendue) is conserved:

$$n_1 A_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 A_2 \sin \theta_2$$

**Step 2A — For Air (n = 1), Sun → Aperture:**
$$A_{sun} \sin \theta_{sun} = A_a \sin \theta_a$$

$$\frac{A_a}{A_{sun}} = \frac{\sin \theta_{sun}}{\sin \theta_a}$$

**Step 2B — Sun Angular Size:**
$\theta_{sun} \approx 0.27°$ (sun's half-angle as seen from Earth).

**Step 2C — Maximum Concentration:**
$$C_{max} = \frac{A_a}{A_r} = \frac{\sin \theta_{sun}}{\sin \theta_a} \cdot \frac{A_{sun}}{A_r}$$

Since all radiation reaching $A_r$ must be re-emitted into $2\pi$ (full hemisphere, sin = 1), $A_{sun}/A_r = 1$.

$$\boxed{C_{max} = \frac{1}{\sin \theta_a}}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Check
For $\theta_a = 10°$:
$$C_{max} = \frac{1}{\sin 10°} = \frac{1}{0.1736} \approx 5.76$$

For $\theta_a = 5°$ (high concentration, narrow acceptance):
$$C_{max} = \frac{1}{\sin 5°} \approx 11.47$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Sensible Heat Storage Capacity in Molten Salt

### Given
- Mass of salt: $m = 1400 \times 10^3$ kg
- $c_p$ (salt) = 1.55 kJ/kg·K
- $T_{min} = 290$ °C (cold tank)
- $T_{max} = 565$ °C (hot tank)

### Step 1 — Apply Sensible Heat Equation

$$Q_S = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{max} - T_{min})$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$Q_S = 1400 \times 10^3 \text{ kg} \times 1.55 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \times (565 - 290) \text{ K}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Step-by-Step

**Step 3A — Temperature Difference:**
$$\Delta T = 565 - 290 = 275 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3B — Product of First Two Terms:**
$$m \cdot c_p = 1.4 \times 10^6 \times 1.55 = 2.17 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 3C — Final Multiplication:**
$$Q_S = 2.17 \times 10^6 \times 275 = 5.9675 \times 10^8 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3D — Convert to MWh:**
$$Q_S = \frac{5.9675 \times 10^8}{3600} = 1.6576 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh} = 165.76 \text{ MWh}$$

### Result
$$\boxed{Q_S \approx 596.75 \text{ GJ} \approx 165.76 \text{ MWh}}$$

> This is the storage capacity of a 1400-tonne molten salt tank — comparable to the Gemasolar/Andasol type CSP plants.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: PV Cell Efficiency from I–V Curve

### Step 1 — Open Circuit Voltage
Setting $I = 0$ in the ideal diode equation:
$$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

Solving for $V_{oc}$ (since $I_L \gg I_0$):
$$V_{oc} \approx \frac{n k T}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Short Circuit Current
At $V = 0$: $I = I_{sc} = I_L$ (shunt losses neglected).

### Step 3 — Maximum Power Point
Differentiate $P = V \cdot I$ and set $dP/dV = 0$. This gives implicit equations for $V_{mp}$ and $I_{mp}$.

### Step 4 — Fill Factor (Empirical Approximation)
$$FF_0 = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

where $v_{oc} = \dfrac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}$ (normalized voltage).

### Step 5 — Final Efficiency

$$\boxed{\eta_{PV} = \frac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G_T \cdot A_c}}$$

For c-Si at STC (AM 1.5, 1000 W/m², 25 °C): $V_{oc} \approx 0.6$ V, $I_{sc} \approx 3.5$ A, $FF \approx 0.78$, $\eta \approx 16$–$20\%$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — FPC Outlet Temperature

**Problem (KTU Typical):** A flat plate collector has $A_c = 2$ m², $F_R = 0.8$, $U_L = 5$ W/m²·K, $(\tau \alpha)_e = 0.8$, $G_T = 800$ W/m², $\dot{m} = 0.02$ kg/s, $c_p = 4186$ J/kg·K, $T_{fi} = 30$ °C, $T_a = 25$ °C. Find $T_{fo}$.

### Step 1 — Absorbed Radiation
$$S = (\tau \alpha)_e \cdot G_T = 0.8 \times 800 = 640 \text{ W/m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Useful Heat
$$Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)]$$
$$Q_u = 2 \times 0.8 \times [640 - 5 \times (30 - 25)]$$
$$Q_u = 1.6 \times [640 - 25] = 1.6 \times 615 = 984 \text{ W}$$

### Step 3 — Outlet Temperature
From $Q_u = \dot{m} c_p (T_{fo} - T_{fi})$:
$$T_{fo} = T_{fi} + \frac{Q_u}{\dot{m} c_p}$$
$$T_{fo} = 30 + \frac{984}{0.02 \times 4186}$$
$$T_{fo} = 30 + \frac{984}{83.72} = 30 + 11.75 = 41.75 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{T_{fo} \approx 41.75 \text{ °C}}$$

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] 
**Define a flat plate collector (FPC). List its main components and state the standard temperature range.**

**Model Answer:**
A flat plate collector (FPC) is a non-concentrating, stationary solar thermal device that absorbs both beam and diffuse solar radiation on a flat absorber surface, transferring the heat to a working fluid.

**Main Components:**
1. Transparent glazing (low-iron tempered glass).
2. Selective-coated absorber plate.
3. Riser tubes and header.
4. Back and side insulation.
5. Weather-tight casing/frame.

**Temperature Range:** 30 °C to 100 °C (standard); advanced FPCs reach up to 200 °C with double glazing and improved selective coatings.

**Marks Distribution (KTU 2024 Pattern):** Definition — 1 mark | Components — 1 mark | Range — 1 mark.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] 
**Differentiate between sensible heat storage and latent heat storage systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sensible Heat Storage | Latent Heat Storage |
|---|---|---|
| Storage mechanism | Temperature rise of medium | Phase change (solid–liquid) at constant T |
| Energy equation | $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ | $Q = m L_f$ |
| Energy density | Low (4–80 kJ/kg for water) | High (200–300 kJ/kg for paraffin) |
| Temperature stability | Variable (depends on T range) | Constant (at melting point) |
| Common media | Water, rock, molten salt | Salt hydrates, paraffin wax |
| Example application | Molten-salt tanks in CSP | Building thermal comfort, PCM walls |

**Marks Distribution:** Three differentiating points × 1 mark each.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Q. Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **flat plate collector (FPC)**. Derive the **Hottel–Whillier equation** for useful heat gain. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Construction (3 marks):**
The FPC consists of:
- **Glazing:** 1–3 layers of low-iron glass (τ ≈ 0.9) to admit solar radiation and trap IR.
- **Absorber Plate:** Copper or aluminum sheet coated with black chrome or cermet (α ≈ 0.95, ε ≈ 0.05) to maximize absorption and minimize emission.
- **Tubes/Risers:** Copper tubes bonded to the plate (ultrasonically welded or roll-bonded) carry the working fluid.
- **Insulation:** Polyurethane foam or mineral wool (k ≈ 0.04 W/m·K) on back and sides.
- **Casing:** Anodized aluminum or galvanized steel housing.

**Working (1 mark):**
Beam + diffuse solar radiation passes through the glazing, is absorbed by the selective surface, conducted through the plate, convected into the fluid in the tubes, and delivered as hot fluid. Heat losses occur upward (re-radiation + convection), downward (conduction), and sideways (edge loss).

**Derivation (3 marks):**

The energy balance on the absorber plate per unit area is:
$$S = \frac{Q_u}{A_c F_R} + U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)$$

Solving for $Q_u$:
$$Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)]$$

where:
- $S = (\tau \alpha)_e \cdot G_T$
- $F_R$ = heat removal factor
- $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$
- $T_{fi}$ = fluid inlet temperature
- $T_a$ = ambient temperature

The **Hottel–Whillier plot** is a straight line: $\eta_c$ vs. $(T_{fi} - T_a)/G_T$ with intercept $F_R(\tau \alpha)_e$ and slope $-F_R U_L$.

---

**(b)** A flat plate collector of area $2.5$ m² receives solar radiation at 800 W/m². The heat removal factor $F_R = 0.82$, effective transmittance-absorptance product $(\tau \alpha)_e = 0.78$, and overall heat loss coefficient $U_L = 5.2$ W/m²·K. Ambient temperature is 28 °C and fluid inlet temperature is 35 °C. Determine: **(i)** the useful heat gain, **(ii)** the collector efficiency, and **(iii)** the rise in stagnation condition. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:**
- $A_c = 2.5$ m²
- $G_T = 800$ W/m²
- $F_R = 0.82$
- $(\tau \alpha)_e = 0.78$
- $U_L = 5.2$ W/m²·K
- $T_a = 28$ °C
- $T_{fi} = 35$ °C

**Step 1 — Absorbed Radiation:**
$$S = (\tau \alpha)_e \cdot G_T = 0.78 \times 800 = 624 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Useful Heat Gain (i):**
$$Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)]$$
$$Q_u = 2.5 \times 0.82 \times [624 - 5.2 \times (35 - 28)]$$
$$Q_u = 2.05 \times [624 - 36.4] = 2.05 \times 587.6 = 1204.6 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Collector Efficiency (ii):**
$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \cdot G_T} = \frac{1204.6}{2.5 \times 800} = \frac{1204.6}{2000} = 0.6023$$

$$\boxed{\eta_c \approx 60.23\%}$$

**Step 4 — Stagnation Temperature Rise (iii):**
Stagnation condition: $\eta_c = 0$, so $S = U_L (T_{stagnation} - T_a)$:
$$T_{stagnation} - T_a = \frac{S}{U_L} = \frac{624}{5.2} = 120 \text{ K}$$
$$T_{stagnation} = 28 + 120 = 148 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{Q_u = 1204.6 \text{ W}, \quad \eta_c = 60.23\%, \quad T_{stag} = 148 \text{ °C}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Q. Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle and operation of a salt-gradient solar pond** with a neat diagram. Discuss its limitations. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Principle (2 marks):**
A solar pond is a saline water body engineered to store solar energy as heat in its bottom layer. The key innovation is the **salinity gradient** that increases with depth, creating a stable density gradient which **suppresses natural convection**. Without this, hot water would rise to the surface and lose heat to the atmosphere.

**Construction & Zones (2 marks):**
1. **Upper Convective Zone (UCZ):** 0.3–0.5 m of fresh or low-salinity water, transparent, near ambient temperature.
2. **Non-Convective Gradient Zone (NGZ):** 1.0–1.5 m with gradually increasing salt concentration (from ~5% to ~25% by weight), suppressing convection.
3. **Lower Convective Zone (LCZ):** 0.5–1.0 m of saturated brine (~25% salt), which heats to 70–95 °C and acts as thermal storage.

**Operation (2 marks):**
- Sunlight penetrates the transparent upper layers and is absorbed in the lower zone (about 20–30% is absorbed in the LCZ itself; the rest heats intermediate layers but the gradient prevents convective loss).
- Hot brine is **withdrawn** from the bottom through an external heat exchanger, then **re-injected** after cooling.
- The extracted heat drives a **low-temperature Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC)** for electricity generation or supplies process heat.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Low thermal-to-electrical efficiency (~15–25% due to low temperature).
- Requires continuous maintenance of salt gradient (salt diffusion must be corrected).
- Susceptible to surface evaporative losses and algae growth.
- Large land area requirement; site-specific (works best in arid regions with low rainfall).
- Slow thermal response time.

---

**(b)** A solar pond has surface area 1000 m², LCZ temperature 80 °C, UCZ temperature 25 °C. Brine flow rate is 0.5 kg/s with $c_p = 3500$ J/kg·K. Average daily solar radiation is 600 W/m² for 8 hours. Calculate: **(i)** thermal energy collected per day, **(ii)** overall pond efficiency, **(iii)** electrical energy if ORC efficiency is 12%. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:**
- $A_{pond} = 1000$ m²
- $T_{LCZ} = 80$ °C, $T_{UCZ} = 25$ °C
- $\dot{m} = 0.5$ kg/s
- $c_p = 3500$ J/kg·K
- $G_T = 600$ W/m², $t = 8 \times 3600$ s

**Step 1 — Heat Extraction Rate (i):**
$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{LCZ} - T_{UCZ})$$
$$Q = 0.5 \times 3500 \times (80 - 25) = 0.5 \times 3500 \times 55 = 96{,}250 \text{ W} = 96.25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Daily Thermal Energy (i):**
$$Q_{day} = Q \times t = 96{,}250 \times (8 \times 3600) = 96{,}250 \times 28{,}800 = 2.772 \times 10^9 \text{ J}$$

Convert to kWh: $Q_{day} = 2.772 \times 10^9 / 3.6 \times 10^6 = 770$ kWh.

**Step 3 — Daily Solar Input:**
$$Q_{in} = G_T \cdot A \cdot t = 600 \times 1000 \times 28{,}800 = 1.728 \times 10^{10} \text{ J} = 4800 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Pond Efficiency (ii):**
$$\eta_{pond} = \frac{Q_{day}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{2.772 \times 10^9}{1.728 \times 10^{10}} = 0.1604$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{pond} \approx 16.04\%}$$

**Step 5 — Electrical Energy Output (iii):**
$$E_{elec} = \eta_{pond} \times \eta_{ORC} \times Q_{in} = 770 \times 0.12 = 92.4 \text{ kWh}$$

or directly: $E_{elec} = 0.1604 \times 0.12 \times 4800 = 92.4$ kWh.

$$\boxed{E_{elec} \approx 92.4 \text{ kWh/day}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $F_R$:** Many students write $Q_u = A_c [S - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)]$ without $F_R$. This is **worth 2 marks lost** in Part B.
> 
> 2. **Mixing $S$ and $G_T$:** $S$ is the **absorbed** radiation $(\tau \alpha)_e \cdot G_T$, **not** the incident radiation. Using $G_T$ instead of $S$ gives a wrong efficiency.
> 
> 3. **Confusing Solar Constant and AM 1.5:** The **solar constant** is **1367 W/m² at AM 0 (extraterrestrial)**. The standard terrestrial test irradiance is **1000 W/m² at AM 1.5**. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if confused.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting unit conversion in storage problems:** $c_p$ in kJ/kg·K vs. J/kg·K — a factor of 1000 error. Always write units explicitly.
> 
> 5. **Wrong efficiency definition for FPC:** $\eta_c = Q_u / (A_c G_T)$, **not** $Q_u / (A_c S)$. The denominator is always incident radiation, not absorbed.
> 
> 6. **Solar Pond NCG vs. CG confusion:** The gradient zone is **non-convective** (stable), and the lower zone is **convective** (mixed). Drawing it reversed loses 1 mark.
> 
> 7. **PV $V_{oc}$ vs. $V_{mp}$:** $V_{oc}$ is open-circuit; $V_{mp}$ is at maximum power. Power = $V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$, **not** $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### Solar Thermal Collectors
- **FPC** = non-concentrating, flat, fixed, uses beam + diffuse; 30–100 °C.
- **Hottel–Whillier equation** $Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)]$ is the cornerstone of Module 2.
- **Efficiency plot** is a straight line: $\eta_c$ vs. reduced temperature $(T_{fi} - T_a)/G_T$.
- **Stagnation** occurs when $\eta_c = 0$, giving max $T_{fi} = T_a + S/U_L$.

### Solar Concentrators
- **Tracking** = PTC, Dish, Tower, LFR (one or two axes).
- **Non-tracking** = CPC, V-Trough, Stationary Fresnel Lens.
- **Max CPC concentration:** $C_{max} = 1/\sin\theta_a$.
- **Concentration ratio** $C = A_a/A_r$ — higher C → higher T → higher $\eta_{Carnot}$.

### Solar Energy Storage
- **Sensible:** $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ — water, rock, molten salt.
- **Latent:** $Q = m L_f$ — PCMs (paraffin, salt hydrates).
- **Thermochemical:** $Q = m \Delta H_{rxn}$ — Ca(OH)₂, metal hydrides (highest density).
- **Molten salt** ($60\%$ NaNO₃ + $40\%$ KNO₃) is industry standard for CSP storage.

### Solar Thermal Power Plant (CSP)
- **PTC + Rankine cycle** → SEGS California, Andasol Spain.
- **Tower + molten salt** → Gemasolar (24-hr storage).
- **Dish-Stirling** → highest single-unit efficiency (~31%).
- **LFR** → cheaper alternative to PTC.

### Solar Pond
- Three zones: UCZ (transparent), NCG (gradient, non-convective), LCZ (hot, saturated).
- **Combines collection + storage** in one body.
- Efficiency 15–25%; uses ORC for electricity.

### Solar Photovoltaic
- **Photovoltaic effect** in p–n junction.
- **Equivalent circuit:** current source + diode + $R_s$ + $R_{sh}$.
- **Fill Factor** $FF = V_{mp} I_{mp} / (V_{oc} I_{sc}) \approx 0.7$–$0.85$.
- **Efficiency** $\eta_{PV} = P_{max} / (G_T A_c) \approx 15$–$22\%$ (c-Si).
- **STC:** AM 1.5, 1000 W/m², 25 °C.

### Cross-Cutting KTU 2024 High-Yield Formulas
1. $Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)]$
2. $\eta_c = F_R(\tau \alpha)_e - F_R U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)/G_T$
3. $C = A_a/A_r$, $C_{max} = 1/\sin\theta_a$
4. $Q_S = m c_p \Delta T$, $Q_L = m L_f$
5. $FF = V_{mp} I_{mp} / (V_{oc} I_{sc})$
6. $\eta_{PV} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} / (G_T A_c)$

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Strategy:** For 14-mark Part B questions, always draw a **labelled block diagram (1 mark)**, write the **governing equation(s) (2 marks)**, perform **step-by-step numerical substitution (3 marks)**, and present the **final boxed answer with units (1 mark)**. The remaining 7 marks come from the question's other sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Water Heating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET754 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Water Heating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Solar Water Heating (SWH) System** is a solar thermal engineering arrangement that converts incident solar radiation into usable thermal energy stored in a working fluid (typically water or a heat-transfer fluid) for domestic, commercial, or industrial hot-water applications. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, SWH is classified as the most mature and commercially deployed solar thermal technology, forming a sub-topic of *Active and Passive Solar Thermal Systems* in Module 2.

A solar water heating system fundamentally consists of three engineering subsystems:
1. **Solar Collector** — absorbs solar radiation and converts it to heat.
2. **Heat Transfer & Transport Loop** — carries the heated fluid from the collector to storage.
3. **Thermal Storage Unit** — stores the heated water for later use, often with auxiliary backup.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine placing a black garden hose on a sunny rooftop with water flowing inside. After a few hours, the water becomes warm — even hot. A Solar Water Heating System is the *engineered, optimized, and instrumented* version of this phenomenon. The **black surface** acts as the absorber, the **transparent cover (glass)** acts like sunglasses that trap heat (greenhouse effect), the **insulation** acts like a woolen sweater preventing heat escape, and the **storage tank** acts as a thermos flask preserving the heat for evening use.

The fundamental physics exploited is the **greenhouse effect** combined with the **selective absorption** of solar radiation. A typical SWH system can supply **60 °C to 90 °C** hot water, sufficient for bathing, laundry, and industrial pre-heating.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Parameters (KTU Board Standard Values):**
> - **Solar constant (I_sc)** = **1367 W/m²** (AM 1.5 extraterrestrial irradiance)
> - **Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** ≈ **5 to 6 hrs/day** (Kerala geographical average)
> - **Useful temperature range** for domestic SWH: **40 °C – 60 °C**
> - **Stagnation temperature** of flat-plate collector: **150 °C – 200 °C**
> - **Standard test condition irradiance (G_T)** = **1000 W/m²** (for rating)
> - **Ambient reference temperature (T_a)** = **25 °C** (ISO 9806 test condition)

### 1.4 Classification Overview

| **Classification Basis** | **Categories** | **Brief Description** |
|---|---|---|
| By Circulation | Active (forced) / Passive (thermosiphon) | Active uses pumps; passive relies on natural density difference |
| By Loop Type | Direct (open) / Indirect (closed) | Direct heats water in collector; indirect uses heat exchanger |
| By Collector Type | Flat Plate / Evacuated Tube / Concentrating | FPC and ETC are non-concentrating; CPC/CPC-ETC are concentrating |
| By End Use | Domestic / Commercial / Industrial | LMTD and capacity vary widely |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does a Solar Water Heater work?**
> The engineering reasoning rests on three pillars: **(1) High solar absorptance**, **(2) Low thermal emittance**, and **(3) Heat retention**.

**Step 1 — Absorption:** Solar radiation (wavelength 0.3 – 2.5 μm) strikes the **selective absorber coating** (e.g., black nickel, black chrome, or cermet). The coating has **α > 0.95** (absorptance) and **ε < 0.10** (emittance) — meaning it absorbs almost all incoming radiation but emits very little infrared re-radiation.

**Step 2 — Heat Transfer to Fluid:** The absorbed heat is conducted through the **absorber plate** (usually copper or aluminum) to the **risers/tubes** bonded to it. Water or heat-transfer fluid circulates through these tubes, gaining sensible heat.

**Step 3 — Minimizing Heat Loss:** A **translucent glass cover** (low-iron tempered glass) reduces convective and radiative losses from the top. **Insulation** at the back and sides reduces conductive losses. The glass also transmits ~90% of solar radiation while being opaque to long-wave IR emitted by the hot absorber.

**Step 4 — Transport to Storage:** Heated fluid rises naturally (passive) or is pumped (active) to the insulated **storage tank**, where it is held until demanded.

**Step 5 — Auxiliary Backup:** In cloudy weather, an **electrical heating element** or backup coil maintains desired temperature, ensuring year-round supply.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Board Examinations** (KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2)

| **Equation / Parameter** | **Mathematical Form** | **Variables & Units** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Useful Heat Gain (Hottel-Whillier) | $Q_u = A_c \, F_R \, \left[ S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$ | $A_c$: collector area (m²); $F_R$: heat removal factor (–); $S$: absorbed radiation per unit area (W/m²); $U_L$: overall loss coefficient (W/m²·K); $T_{in}$: fluid inlet temp (°C); $T_a$: ambient temp (°C) | Net thermal energy delivered to fluid per unit time |
| Instantaneous Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_i = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c \, G_T} = F_R \left[ (\tau \alpha) - \dfrac{U_L (T_{in} - T_a)}{G_T} \right]$ | $G_T$: total incident solar irradiance (W/m²); $(\tau \alpha)$: transmittance-absorptance product (–) | Fraction of incident solar energy converted to useful heat |
| Overall Loss Coefficient | $U_L = U_t + U_{e,b} + U_{edge}$ | $U_t$: top loss; $U_{e,b}$: bottom loss; $U_{edge}$: edge loss (all W/m²·K) | Aggregate thermal resistance of the collector |
| Absorbed Radiation | $S = G_T \, (\tau \alpha)_n \, K_{\tau \alpha}$ | $(\tau \alpha)_n$: normal incidence value; $K_{\tau \alpha}$: incident angle modifier (–) | Solar energy actually retained by absorber |
| Heat Removal Factor | $F_R = \dfrac{\dot{m} \, c_p}{A_c \, U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\left( -\dfrac{A_c \, U_L \, F'}{\dot{m} \, c_p} \right) \right]$ | $\dot{m}$: mass flow rate (kg/s); $c_p$: specific heat (J/kg·K); $F'$: collector efficiency factor | Effectiveness of heat extraction from absorber |
| Storage Tank Energy Balance | $M \, c_p \, \dfrac{dT_s}{dt} = Q_u - Q_L - Q_{load}$ | $M$: tank water mass; $T_s$: storage temp; $Q_L$: tank loss; $Q_{load}$: demand | Rate of change of stored thermal energy |
| Daily Useful Heat | $Q_{day} = \eta_d \cdot A_c \cdot \overline{G_T} \cdot 3600 \cdot 24$ | $\eta_d$: daily efficiency; $\overline{G_T}$: daily mean irradiance (W/m²) | Total energy delivered per day (J) |
| Heat Exchanger (Indirect) | $Q_{HX} = (UA)_{HX} \, \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | $(UA)_{HX}$: HX conductance; $\Delta T_{LMTD}$: log-mean temperature difference | Heat transferred in closed-loop systems |
| FPC Top Loss (Klein Equation) | $U_t = \left[ \dfrac{N}{\dfrac{C}{T_p} \left( \dfrac{T_p - T_a}{N + f} \right)^{e}} + \dfrac{1}{h_w} \right]^{-1} + \dfrac{\sigma (T_p^2 + T_a^2)(T_p + T_a)}{\varepsilon_p^{-1} + \varepsilon_c^{-1} - 1}$ | $N$: number of glass covers; $C, e, f$: empirical wind constants; $h_w$: wind coefficient; $\varepsilon_p, \varepsilon_c$: emissivities | Top heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K) |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Applications of SWH Systems:**
> - **Domestic Sector:** 100–500 LPD (Litres Per Day) systems for households — the **MNRE** mandates SWH for hospitals, hotels, and hostels in Kerala.
> - **Industrial Process Heat:** Dairy plants, textile dyeing, food processing — preheating boiler feed water reduces fossil-fuel consumption by 20–40%.
> - **Swimming Pool Heating:** Low-temperature (~28 °C) unglazed collectors are cost-effective.
> - **Space Heating Integration:** In cold climates, SWH feeds underfloor radiant loops.
> - **Solar Desalination:** Indirect SWH drives multi-effect distillation for potable water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Economic Significance:** A 100 LPD system in Kerala (≈ 2 m² collector) saves approximately **1250 units (kWh) of electricity per year**, translating to **₹10,000+ savings** and a **CO₂ reduction of ~1 tonne/year**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hottel-Whillier-Bliss (HWB) Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Derive the fundamental instantaneous efficiency equation of a flat-plate solar collector, which is the most-tested derivation in KTU board papers.

**Starting Point — Energy Balance on Absorber Plate:**

For a unit area of absorber plate at steady state, the absorbed solar radiation equals the sum of useful heat gain and heat losses:

$$S = Q_u + Q_{loss}$$

**Step 1 — Express the Loss Term:**

The total heat loss per unit area is proportional to the temperature difference between absorber and ambient, governed by overall loss coefficient $U_L$:

$$Q_{loss} = U_L \, (T_p - T_a)$$

where $T_p$ is the local absorber plate temperature.

**Step 2 — Introduce the Useful Heat Transfer to Fluid:**

The rate at which heat is removed by the fluid depends on the difference between plate temperature and fluid temperature. Define the **collector efficiency factor** $F'$ such that:

$$Q_u = F' \, A_c \, U_L \, (T_p - T_{in})$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate Plate Temperature $T_p$:**

From the energy balance:

$$T_p - T_a = \dfrac{S - Q_u / A_c}{U_L} = \dfrac{S - \eta \, G_T}{U_L}$$

Substitute into Step 2:

$$Q_u = F' \, A_c \, U_L \left[ \dfrac{S - Q_u/A_c}{U_L} - (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to Isolate $Q_u$:**

$$Q_u = F' \left[ A_c \, S - Q_u - A_c \, U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$$

$$Q_u (1 + F') = F' \left[ A_c \, S - A_c \, U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$$

$$\dfrac{Q_u}{A_c} = \dfrac{F'}{1 + F'} \left[ S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Define the Heat Removal Factor $F_R$:**

When the fluid moves through the collector, the average plate-fluid temperature difference is less than the local one. The **heat removal factor** is defined as:

$$F_R = \dfrac{\dot{m} \, c_p}{A_c \, U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\left( -\dfrac{A_c \, U_L \, F'}{\dot{m} \, c_p} \right) \right]$$

This is derived by integrating the differential energy balance over the length of a single riser tube. Replacing $F'/(1+F')$ with $F_R$:

$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$$

**Step 6 — Express in Terms of Incidence Quantities:**

Substituting $S = G_T (\tau \alpha)$ and dividing by $A_c G_T$:

$$\eta_i = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c G_T} = F_R (\tau \alpha) - F_R \dfrac{U_L (T_{in} - T_a)}{G_T}$$

This is the **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation**, the cornerstone of all solar thermal engineering.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Collector Efficiency Factor $F'$

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the expression for $F'$, which quantifies how effectively the fluid extracts heat from the plate through the tube-bond interface.

**Step 1 — Thermal Resistance Network:**

The total resistance between fluid and ambient is the **sum of two parallel paths**: (a) tube-fluid fin resistance, and (b) plate-to-tube conduction resistance. For a single tube with spacing $W$ and tube diameter $D$:

$$F' = \dfrac{1 / U_L}{W \left[ \dfrac{1}{U_L (D + (W - D) F)} + \dfrac{1}{C_b + h_{f,i} \pi D} \right]}$$

**Step 2 — Fin Efficiency $F$:**

The plate between tubes acts as a fin of length $(W - D)/2$:

$$F = \dfrac{\tanh\left( m (W - D)/2 \right)}{m (W - D)/2}, \quad m = \sqrt{\dfrac{U_L}{k \delta}}$$

where $k$ is plate thermal conductivity and $\delta$ is plate thickness.

**Step 3 — Bond Conductance $C_b$:**

The thermal resistance of the adhesive (solder/epoxy) between tube and plate is modeled as:

$$C_b = \dfrac{k_b}{t_b} \cdot \pi D$$

where $k_b$ is bond thermal conductivity and $t_b$ is bond thickness.

**Step 4 — Internal Film Coefficient $h_{f,i}$:**

Using Dittus-Boelter for turbulent flow in tubes:

$$h_{f,i} = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{0.4} \cdot \dfrac{k_f}{D}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Storage Tank Energy Balance

**Step 1 — Lumped Capacitance Model:**

Assuming the tank water is at uniform temperature $T_s(t)$ (valid for small tanks with mixing):

$$M \, c_p \, \dfrac{dT_s}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{Q}_{out,loss} - \dot{Q}_{load}$$

**Step 2 — Heat Loss from Tank:**

$$\dot{Q}_{out,loss} = (UA)_{tank} (T_s - T_{room})$$

**Step 3 — Coupled Collector Equation:**

The collector delivers:

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = A_c \, F_R \left[ G_T (\tau \alpha) - U_L \left( T_{in,coll} - T_a \right) \right]$$

For direct systems, $T_{in,coll} \approx T_s$. Substituting:

$$M \, c_p \, \dfrac{dT_s}{dt} = A_c F_R G_T (\tau \alpha) - A_c F_R U_L (T_s - T_a) - (UA)_{tank} (T_s - T_{room}) - \dot{Q}_{load}$$

**Step 4 — Steady-State Solution:**

Setting $dT_s/dt = 0$ and solving for the equilibrium temperature:

$$T_{s,eq} = \dfrac{A_c F_R G_T (\tau \alpha) + \dot{Q}_{load} + A_c F_R U_L T_a + (UA)_{tank} T_{room}}{A_c F_R U_L + (UA)_{tank}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** The Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation is **linear** in $\left( T_{in} - T_a \right) / G_T$. Plotting $\eta_i$ vs. $\left( T_{in} - T_a \right) / G_T$ yields a **straight line** with intercept $F_R(\tau \alpha)$ and slope $-F_R U_L$. This is a high-yield numerical question.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A flat-plate solar water heater has the following specifications: $A_c = 2.5$ m², $F_R (\tau \alpha) = 0.78$, $F_R U_L = 4.5$ W/m²·K, $G_T = 850$ W/m², $T_{in} = 30$ °C, $T_a = 25$ °C. Calculate (a) instantaneous efficiency, (b) useful heat gain rate, and (c) hot water output if temperature rise is 35 °C.

**Solution:**

**(a) Instantaneous Efficiency:**

$$\eta_i = F_R (\tau \alpha) - F_R U_L \cdot \dfrac{T_{in} - T_a}{G_T}$$

$$\eta_i = 0.78 - 4.5 \times \dfrac{30 - 25}{850}$$

$$\eta_i = 0.78 - 4.5 \times \dfrac{5}{850} = 0.78 - 0.0265 = 0.7535$$

$$\boxed{\eta_i \approx 75.35\%}$$

**(b) Useful Heat Gain:**

$$Q_u = \eta_i \times A_c \times G_T = 0.7535 \times 2.5 \times 850$$

$$\boxed{Q_u \approx 1601.2 \text{ W} \approx 1.60 \text{ kW}}$$

**(c) Hot Water Output:**

Using $Q_u = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ with $c_p = 4186$ J/kg·K and $\Delta T = 35$ K:

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{1601.2}{4186 \times 35} = \dfrac{1601.2}{146510} = 0.01093 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{0.01093}{1000} \times 3600 = 0.0394 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} \approx 39.4 \text{ L/hr}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Hot water delivery} \approx 39.4 \text{ L/hr}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Active Closed-Loop (Indirect) Solar Water Heating System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SUN Solar Radiation G_T] --> B[SOLAR COLLECTOR FPC or ETC]
    B --> C[PRIMARY LOOP Heated Glycol]
    C --> D[PUMP Circulation Pump]
    D --> E[HEAT EXCHANGER Coil in Tank]
    E --> F[STORAGE TANK Insulated]
    F --> G[SECONDARY LOOP Domestic Hot Water]
    F --> H[TANK LOSS UA_tank T_room]
    F --> I[BACKUP HEATER Electrical Coil]
    G --> J[END USER Taps and Appliances]
    K[CONTROLLER Differential Thermostat] -.-> D
    K -.-> I
    B -.-> L[HEAT LOSS U_L T_a]
    L --> M[AMBIENT ENVIRONMENT]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows represent the **primary energy and fluid flow**.
- Dashed arrows represent **control signals** and **heat loss paths**.
- The Controller monitors $T_{collector} - T_{tank}$ and actuates the pump only when collector is hotter than tank (avoids reverse heat flow at night).

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Heat Transfer and Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Incidence G_T] --> R2[Glazing Transmission tau]
    R2 --> R3[Absorber Plate alpha]
    R3 --> R4[Useful Heat Q_u to Fluid]
    R3 --> R5[Top Loss U_t]
    R3 --> R6[Bottom Loss U_b]
    R3 --> R7[Edge Loss U_e]
    R5 --> R8[Ambient T_a]
    R6 --> R8
    R7 --> R8
    R4 --> R9[Storage Tank M c_p dT_s/dt]
    R9 --> R10[Demand Q_load]
    R9 --> R11[Tank Loss to Room]
```

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- Total absorbed radiation $S$ splits into **one useful stream** ($Q_u$) and **three loss streams** ($U_t, U_b, U_e$).
- The storage tank acts as a **buffer**, integrating intermittent collector output against steady demand.

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Passive Thermosiphon System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Cold Water Inlet] --> P2[STORAGE TANK Mounted Above Collector]
    P2 --> P3[Downcomer Pipe]
    P3 --> P4[FLAT PLATE COLLECTOR]
    P4 --> P5[Hot Water Rises Naturally Density Difference]
    P5 --> P2
    P4 --> P6[Heat Loss to Ambient]
    P2 --> P7[Hot Water Tap to User]
    P2 --> P8[Backup Electrical Heater Optional]
```

**Key Engineering Principle:** No pump is required because hot water (lighter) rises from the collector to the tank mounted at a higher elevation. This is the simplest, most reliable, and most common residential SWH in Kerala.

### 4.4 Collector Type Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[SOLAR COLLECTOR TYPES] --> R1[FLAT PLATE COLLECTOR FPC]
    Q --> R2[EVACUATED TUBE COLLECTOR ETC]
    Q --> R3[CONCENTRATING COLLECTOR]
    R1 --> S1[Temp 40 to 80 deg C]
    R1 --> S2[Glazed Insulated]
    R1 --> S3[Selective Coating]
    R2 --> T1[Temp 50 to 120 deg C]
    R2 --> T2[Vacuum Insulation]
    R2 --> T3[Glass Tubes with Absorber]
    R3 --> U1[Temp Above 100 deg C]
    R3 --> U2[Tracking Required]
    R3 --> U3[CPC or Parabolic]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between active and passive solar water heating systems. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Active System** | **Passive System** |
|---|---|---|
| Circulation | Forced using electrical pump | Natural thermosiphon effect |
| Installation complexity | Higher; needs controls and power backup | Simple, minimal components |
| Cost | Higher initial cost | Lower initial cost |
| Reliability | Depends on electricity/controls | Highly reliable, no electricity needed |
| Best suited for | Large commercial/industrial loads | Domestic rooftop installations |

**Advantage — Active:** Faster response and ability to handle large flow rates.

**Advantage — Passive:** Zero operating cost and no pump failure risk.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for comparison, 1 mark for advantage)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation? Explain each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation expresses the instantaneous thermal efficiency of a flat-plate solar collector:

$$\eta_i = F_R (\tau \alpha) - F_R U_L \left( \dfrac{T_{in} - T_a}{G_T} \right)$$

**Term definitions (1.5 marks for equation, 1.5 marks for terms):**
- **$F_R$** — Heat removal factor (dimensionless), quantifies effectiveness of heat transfer from plate to fluid.
- **$(\tau \alpha)$** — Transmittance-absorptance product; fraction of solar radiation that is retained by absorber.
- **$U_L$** — Overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K).
- **$T_{in}$** — Fluid inlet temperature to collector (°C).
- **$T_a$** — Ambient air temperature (°C).
- **$G_T$** — Total solar irradiance incident on collector plane (W/m²).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **forget to convert units** (e.g., W to kW, minutes to seconds) and lose 2 marks.
> - Forgetting the **$(\tau \alpha)$ value** and using $G_T$ directly instead of $S$ loses 1 mark.
> - In numerical problems, **not stating the Hottel-Whillier equation first** before plugging values loses 1 mark.
> - For indirect systems, students often **miss the heat exchanger effectiveness**, losing 2–3 marks.
> - Drawing block diagrams without arrows or proper labeling results in **partial marking**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**A flat-plate solar water heater has the following specifications under standard test conditions:**

- Collector area: $A_c = 3.0$ m²
- Heat removal factor: $F_R = 0.85$
- Transmittance-absorptance product: $(\tau \alpha) = 0.78$
- Overall loss coefficient: $U_L = 5.2$ W/m²·K
- Ambient temperature: $T_a = 30$ °C
- Fluid inlet temperature: $T_{in} = 45$ °C
- Solar irradiance: $G_T = 900$ W/m²

#### **(a) Calculate the instantaneous thermal efficiency and useful heat gain of the collector.** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State HWB Equation (1 mark):**

$$\eta_i = F_R (\tau \alpha) - F_R U_L \cdot \dfrac{T_{in} - T_a}{G_T}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values (2 marks):**

$$\eta_i = 0.85 \times 0.78 - 0.85 \times 5.2 \times \dfrac{45 - 30}{900}$$

$$\eta_i = 0.663 - 4.42 \times \dfrac{15}{900}$$

$$\eta_i = 0.663 - 4.42 \times 0.01667$$

$$\eta_i = 0.663 - 0.0737 = 0.5893$$

**Step 3 — Instantaneous Efficiency (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\eta_i \approx 58.93\%}$$

**Step 4 — Useful Heat Gain (3 marks):**

$$Q_u = \eta_i \times A_c \times G_T$$

$$Q_u = 0.5893 \times 3.0 \times 900$$

$$\boxed{Q_u = 1591.1 \text{ W} \approx 1.591 \text{ kW}}$$

---

#### **(b) If the system operates for 6 effective sun hours per day, estimate the daily useful heat delivered, the equivalent electrical energy saved, and the daily hot water output assuming an average temperature rise of 30 °C.** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Daily Energy Delivered (2 marks):**

$$Q_{day} = \eta_i \times A_c \times G_T \times t$$

$$Q_{day} = 1591.1 \times 6 \times 3600 \text{ J}$$

$$Q_{day} = 34,367,760 \text{ J} \approx 34.37 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent Electrical Energy (2 marks):**

$$E_{elec} = \dfrac{Q_{day}}{3.6 \times 10^6} \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{elec} = \dfrac{34.37}{3.6} = 9.547 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{elec} \approx 9.55 \text{ units of electricity saved per day}}$$

**Step 3 — Daily Hot Water Output (3 marks):**

Using $Q_{day} = m c_p \Delta T$ with $c_p = 4186$ J/kg·K and $\Delta T = 30$ K:

$$m = \dfrac{34,367,760}{4186 \times 30} = \dfrac{34,367,760}{125,580} = 273.7 \text{ kg}$$

$$V = \dfrac{273.7}{1000} = 0.2737 \text{ m}^3 = 273.7 \text{ litres}$$

$$\boxed{V_{hot} \approx 273.7 \text{ L/day}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### **(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an active closed-loop (indirect) solar water heating system. List its major components.** (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Sun] → [Flat-Plate Collector] → [Pump] → [Heat Exchanger in Tank]
                                       ↑                     ↓
                                    [Controller]   [Storage Tank]
                                                         ↓
                                                [Hot Water Outlet]
                                                [Auxiliary Heater]
```

*(Full block diagram similar to Section 4.1 should be drawn for full marks)*

**Working Description (2 marks):**

In an **active closed-loop system**, the heat-transfer fluid (typically a glycol-water mixture to prevent freezing) circulates through the collector array driven by an **electrical pump**. The heated fluid passes through a **heat exchanger coil** immersed in the storage tank, transferring its heat to the domestic water. The **differential thermostat controller** activates the pump only when the collector temperature exceeds the tank temperature by a set value (typically 5–10 °C), preventing reverse heat flow at night. The **storage tank** is well-insulated to minimize standby loss. An **auxiliary electrical heater** ensures hot water supply during cloudy days.

**Major Components (2 marks):**
1. **Solar collector** (FPC or ETC)
2. **Circulation pump** with electrical connection
3. **Differential thermostat controller** with sensors
4. **Heat exchanger** (coil or wrap-around type)
5. **Insulated storage tank** with sacrificial anode
6. **Piping, valves, expansion tank, and glycol fluid**
7. **Auxiliary backup heater**

---

#### **(b) An evacuated tube collector (ETC) system has a gross area of 4 m². The manufacturer reports an efficiency equation: $\eta = 0.68 - 1.8 \left( T_{in} - T_a \right) / G_T$. For $T_{in} = 50$ °C, $T_a = 28$ °C, and $G_T = 800$ W/m², determine: (i) instantaneous efficiency, (ii) useful heat gain, and (iii) the reduction in efficiency compared to a flat-plate collector with $\eta_{FPC} = 0.55$ under the same conditions.** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the Reduced Temperature Parameter (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{T_{in} - T_a}{G_T} = \dfrac{50 - 28}{800} = \dfrac{22}{800} = 0.0275 \text{ K·m}^2/\text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Instantaneous Efficiency of ETC (1 mark):**

$$\eta_{ETC} = 0.68 - 1.8 \times 0.0275$$

$$\eta_{ETC} = 0.68 - 0.0495 = 0.6305$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{ETC} \approx 63.05\%}$$

**Step 3 — Useful Heat Gain (2 marks):**

$$Q_u = \eta_{ETC} \times A_c \times G_T = 0.6305 \times 4 \times 800$$

$$\boxed{Q_u = 2017.6 \text{ W} \approx 2.018 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with FPC (3 marks):**

$$Q_{u,FPC} = 0.55 \times 4 \times 800 = 1760 \text{ W}$$

$$\Delta Q_u = 2017.6 - 1760 = 257.6 \text{ W}$$

$$\Delta \eta = \eta_{ETC} - \eta_{FPC} = 0.6305 - 0.55 = 0.0805$$

$$\boxed{\Delta \eta = 8.05\% \text{ (ETC delivers } 14.6\% \text{ more useful heat)}}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- **Solar Water Heating (SWH)** converts solar radiation into thermal energy for heating water; classified under **solar thermal passive/active systems**.

- **Three main subsystems:** Collector + Transport loop + Storage tank.

- **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss (HWB) Equation — MUST MEMORIZE:**
  $$\eta_i = F_R (\tau \alpha) - F_R U_L \left( \dfrac{T_{in} - T_a}{G_T} \right)$$
  - Plot of $\eta_i$ vs. $(T_{in} - T_a)/G_T$ is a **straight line** — intercept = $F_R(\tau \alpha)$, slope = $-F_R U_L$.

- **Heat removal factor $F_R \leq 1$**, depends on flow rate; increasing $\dot{m}$ increases $F_R$ until it approaches $F'$.

- **Two main collector types:**
  - **Flat Plate Collector (FPC):** $40$–$80$ °C, simple, low cost.
  - **Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC):** $50$–$120$ °C, vacuum insulation, higher efficiency at high temperatures.

- **Active vs. Passive:**
  - **Passive (Thermosiphon):** Tank above collector, no pump, density-driven flow.
  - **Active:** Pump + Controller, more flexible placement.

- **Direct vs. Indirect:**
  - **Direct (Open-loop):** Water in collector — risk of freezing and scaling.
  - **Indirect (Closed-loop):** Glycol + Heat Exchanger — required in cold/hard-water regions.

- **Key Constants for Numerical Problems:**
  - $c_{p,water} = 4186$ J/kg·K
  - $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³
  - $1$ kWh $= 3.6 \times 10^6$ J
  - $1$ tonne of refrigeration $= 3.517$ kW

- **Efficiency range:** FPC ≈ 50–65%, ETC ≈ 60–75% at standard test conditions.

- **MNRE Specification (India):** 100 LPD system ≈ 2 m² collector, delivers 50–60 °C water.

- **Common Pitfalls:** Unit conversion errors, forgetting $\eta$ is **dimensionless**, misidentifying $T_{in}$ (collector inlet, not tank outlet), and using $G_T$ without considering the tilt factor.

- **Storage tank design rule of thumb:** Tank capacity (L) ≈ $50 \times A_c$ (m²); for 2 m² collector → 100 L tank.

- **Economic note:** SWH payback period in Kerala = **3 to 5 years** with current electricity tariffs (~₹8/unit).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Air Heating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET754 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Air Heating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Air Heating** is a solar thermal conversion technology in which incident solar radiation is absorbed by a specially designed collector plate, and the resulting thermal energy is transferred to a working fluid — **air** — that flows over, under, or through the absorber. The heated air is then ducted to a space or process for space heating, drying, or industrial process-heat applications.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, Solar Air Heating is positioned as the lowest-temperature, simplest, and most economical subset of solar thermal engineering. The standard reference collector is the **Flat Plate Solar Air Heater (FPSAH)**, consisting of:

- A **transparent cover** (single or double glazing) to reduce convective and radiative top losses.
- An **absorber plate** — flat, corrugated, V-grooved, finned, or matrix type — that captures solar radiation.
- **Side and bottom insulation** to suppress rear and edge losses.
- A **duct / plenum** through which the air is forced (forced circulation) or drawn (natural circulation) by natural buoyancy or by a fan/blower.

The fundamental governing principle is energy balance on the absorber plate:

$$Q_{abs} = Q_{u} + Q_{losses}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The conventional figure of merit is the **thermal efficiency** $\eta = Q_u / (A_c G_T)$, where $A_c$ is the collector aperture area (m²) and $G_T$ is the total (beam + diffuse) in-plane solar irradiance on the tilted collector (W/m²). Typical operating efficiency of an FPSAH ranges from **40 % to 65 %**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Greenhouse on Your Roof"

Imagine stepping into a parked car on a sunny afternoon — the cabin becomes unbearably hot because shortwave solar radiation passes through the glass, is absorbed by the dark interior, and is re-emitted as longwave radiation that the glass traps. A **solar air heater is essentially that greenhouse, except the "trapped heat" is deliberately captured by passing a stream of air through it**.

A better analogy: **drying wet clothes on a clothesline on a sunny, breezy day**. The dark fabric absorbs sunlight, warms up, and the breeze continuously carries that warmth away (the "useful heat" pickup). If you simply placed the fabric in still air, the air around the fabric would saturate thermally and the cloth would not get hotter. This is precisely why forced or induced airflow is critical in a solar air heater.

> [!TIP]
> **Why air, not water?** Air has a very low volumetric heat capacity ( $\rho c_p \approx 1200\ \text{J/(m}^3\cdot\text{K)}$ vs. water's $\approx 4.18 \times 10^6$ J/(m³·K) — roughly **3500 times lower**). This means air heaters must move **larger volumetric flow rates** to deliver the same amount of heat, but in return they offer: no freezing risk, no corrosion, no scaling, and zero leak hazard — making them ideal for low-to-medium temperature applications (40–80 °C).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Solar Air Heaters

Solar air heater designs are classified along **two independent axes** — the **flow path geometry** and the **absorber geometry**.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Flow Path (Pass Configuration)

| Type | Construction | Air Trajectory | Typical ΔT (K) | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Pass** (above/below) | Air flows in one channel, either over the absorber top or beneath it | One direction | 15–30 | Simplest, lowest cost |
| **Double Pass** | Two channels; air crosses the absorber twice | Serpentine / counter-flow | 25–45 | Higher efficiency |
| **Multi-Pass** | Three or more passes (rare) | Folding path | 30–50 | Compact, higher pumping power |

### 2.1.2 Classification by Absorber Geometry (Performance Enhancement)

This is the most **KTU-favoured** classification, as it directly drives thermal performance.

- **Flat Plate Absorber** — Reference baseline. $\eta \approx 40\text{–}55\%$.
- **V-Corrugated Absorber** — Corrugations aligned with flow; they increase the surface area, induce turbulence (higher Nusselt number), and act as **fins** that suppress plate-top temperature gradients.
- **Finned Absorber** — Discrete fins welded/transverse to the plate. Effective when $\text{fin\ efficiency} > 0.7$.
- **Matrix / Porous Absorber** — A packed bed of metal shavings, wire mesh, or ceramic pellets. Extremely high heat transfer area per unit volume, but high pressure drop.
- **Transpired Plate Collector (Recuperator)** — A perforated plate with many small holes; air is sucked through the holes, giving near-uniform absorber temperature and very high efficiency (70–80 %).
- **V-Groove / Honeycomb absorber** — Grooves reflect radiation back onto the plate repeatedly, boosting absorptivity.

## 2.2 Energy Balance on the Absorber Plate (Hottel–Whillier Framework)

The classic **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss** equation, originally for liquid flat-plate collectors, is adapted to air heaters. For a solar air heater, the **instantaneous thermal efficiency** is:

$$\eta_i = F_R (\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{T_{fi} - T_a}{G_T}$$

where:

- $F_R$ = **heat removal factor** (depends on $U_L$, m-dot, $c_p$, and $A_c$).
- $(\tau\alpha)$ = effective transmittance–absorptance product of cover + absorber system.
- $U_L$ = **overall heat loss coefficient** (W/m²·K), comprising top, bottom, and edge losses.
- $T_{fi}$ = fluid (air) inlet temperature, $T_a$ = ambient temperature, $G_T$ = in-plane irradiance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** The student is expected to define, derive, and physically interpret $F_R$, $U_L$, and $(\tau\alpha)$. Marks are awarded for clearly stating the boundary conditions: $G_T = 0$ implies $\eta = -F_R U_L (T_{fi}-T_a)/0$ → mathematically singular, physically the collector loses heat (intercept at $\eta=0$ defines the **stagnation temperature**).

## 2.3 Heat Losses in a Flat Plate Solar Air Heater

The **top loss coefficient** $U_t$ is the dominant loss in a horizontal collector and is given by:

$$U_t = \left[\frac{N}{(K_c / D_c) + (1 / h_{w,i})}\right]^{-1} + \frac{\sigma(T_{pm}+T_a)(T_{pm}^2 + T_a^2)}{1 / \varepsilon_p + (1-\varepsilon_g)/\varepsilon_g \cdot N}$$

where $h_{w,i}$ is the **wind-convection coefficient at the inner glass surface**, $\varepsilon_p$ and $\varepsilon_g$ are emissivities, and $N$ is the number of glass covers.

The **bottom loss** is purely conductive:

$$U_b = \frac{k_{ins}}{L_{ins}}$$

The **edge loss** $U_e$ is usually neglected for large $A_c$ and may be approximated as:

$$U_e = \frac{(U_b + U_t) A_e}{A_c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design thumb rule:** Doubling the number of glass covers from 1 to 2 reduces $U_t$ by approximately **35–50 %** but increases the cover–plate temperature differential losses. Beyond 2 covers, the marginal benefit is small for an air heater.

## 2.4 Useful Heat Delivered to the Air Stream

By energy balance on the air stream:

$$Q_u = \dot{m}\, c_{p,a}\, (T_{fo} - T_{fi})$$

with

$$\dot{m} = \rho_a \cdot \dot{V} = \rho_a \cdot v \cdot A_{duct}$$

The **temperature rise** of the air across the collector is:

$$\Delta T = T_{fo} - T_{fi} = \frac{\eta A_c G_T}{\dot{m} c_{p,a}}$$

For a single-pass, single-glazed FPSAH with $\dot{m} = 0.01\ \text{kg/s}$, $A_c = 1\ \text{m}^2$, $G_T = 800\ \text{W/m}^2$, $\eta = 0.5$:

$$\Delta T = \frac{0.5 \times 1 \times 800}{0.01 \times 1005} \approx 39.8\ \text{K}$$

## 2.5 Pressure Drop in the Duct (Friction Loss)

A critical KTU topic because it dictates **fan/blower sizing and parasitic power**:

$$\Delta P = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot \rho_a v^2}{2 D_h}$$

where $D_h$ is the hydraulic diameter:

$$D_h = \frac{4 A_c}{P_w} = \frac{2 w h}{w+h}$$

for a rectangular duct of width $w$ and height $h$. The Darcy friction factor $f$ for turbulent flow in smooth ducts is given by the Blasius equation:

$$f = 0.079\, Re^{-0.25}, \quad 4000 < Re < 10^5$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering trade-off:** Higher mass flow rate → larger $Q_u$ but also larger $\Delta P$ and fan power $W_f = \dot{V}\cdot \Delta P / \eta_{fan}$. There is an **optimum $\dot{m}$** that maximizes **net useful heat** $Q_{u,net} = Q_u - W_f$. KTU problems often ask to plot $\eta$ vs. $G_T$ at different $\dot{m}$ and identify this optimum.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** a KTU 2024 Scheme student is expected to recognize for the Solar Air Heating topic. Memorize units as well as symbols — unit-mismatch errors are the most common cause of partial-mark deductions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_i = Q_u / (A_c G_T)$ | Instantaneous thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 2 | $Q_u = \dot{m}\, c_p\, (T_{fo} - T_{fi})$ | Useful heat gain | W |
| 3 | $\eta_i = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \dfrac{T_{fi}-T_a}{G_T}$ | Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation | dimensionless |
| 4 | $F_R = \dfrac{\dot{m} c_p}{A_c U_L}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} c_p}\right)\right]$ | Heat removal factor | dimensionless |
| 5 | $F' = \dfrac{1/U_L}{W \left[\dfrac{1}{h_{p,a}} + \dfrac{W}{k_{pl}}\right]}$ (for finned plate) | Collector efficiency factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $U_t^{-1} = \dfrac{N}{h_{w,i} + K_c/D_c} + \dfrac{1}{h_{r,p-g}}$ | Top loss resistance | m²·K/W |
| 7 | $U_b = k_{ins} / L_{ins}$ | Bottom loss coefficient | W/m²·K |
| 8 | $h_{w,i} = 5.7 + 3.8 v_w$ | Wind convection inside (McAdams) | W/m²·K |
| 9 | $Q_r = \varepsilon_p \sigma (T_p^4 - T_g^4) / [1/\varepsilon_p + (1-\varepsilon_g)/\varepsilon_g]$ | Radiative heat exchange | W/m² |
| 10 | $\Delta T = \eta A_c G_T / (\dot{m} c_{p,a})$ | Air temperature rise | K |
| 11 | $\dot{m} = \rho_a \dot{V} = \rho_a v A_{duct}$ | Mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 12 | $\Delta P = f L \rho_a v^2 / (2 D_h)$ | Duct friction pressure drop | Pa |
| 13 | $D_h = 4 A_{cs} / P_w$ | Hydraulic diameter | m |
| 14 | $Re = \rho_a v D_h / \mu_a$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 15 | $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ (Dittus–Boelter, smooth duct) | Convective Nusselt number | dimensionless |
| 16 | $Q_{loss} = A_c U_L (T_p - T_a)$ | Total heat loss | W |
| 17 | $T_{stag} = T_a + (\tau\alpha) G_T / U_L$ | Stagnation temperature | K |
| 18 | $P_{fan} = \dot{V}\, \Delta P / \eta_{fan}$ | Parasitic fan power | W |
| 19 | $\eta_{net} = (Q_u - P_{fan}) / (A_c G_T)$ | Net system efficiency | dimensionless |
| 20 | $h_c = 1.32 (\Delta T / D)^{0.25}$ (tilted plate, natural conv.) | Free convection coefficient | W/m²·K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard physical constants** required: Stefan–Boltzmann $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴; $c_{p,a} = 1005$ J/kg·K; $\rho_{a,20°C} = 1.20$ kg/m³; $\mu_a = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s; solar constant $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m².

## 2.7 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

| Sector | Use Case | Typical Operating T (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| Agriculture | Crop drying, greenhouse heating | 40–60 |
| Domestic | Space heating (passive/active), ventilation preheat | 30–50 |
| Industrial | Process heat, paint drying, wood seasoning | 50–90 |
| Desalination | Humidification–dehumidification (HDH) | 50–80 |
| Power | Solar chimneys (turbine air) | ambient to 60 |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Instantaneous Thermal Efficiency of a Solar Air Heater

**Statement:** Starting from first principles, derive the instantaneous thermal efficiency of a flat plate solar air heater in the form
$$\eta_i = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{T_{fi} - T_a}{G_T}$$

### Step 1 — Energy balance on the absorber plate (steady state)

The absorber plate receives $S = G_T (\tau\alpha)$ W/m² of useful absorbed radiation. A fraction of this is transferred to the air stream (the useful gain) and the remainder is lost to surroundings through top, bottom, and edge losses:

$$G_T (\tau\alpha) = Q_u / A_c + U_L (T_p - T_a)$$

where $T_p$ is the mean plate temperature.

### Step 2 — Express useful gain in terms of air temperature

The air absorbs heat from the plate with overall plate-to-air conductance $h_{p,a}$:

$$\frac{Q_u}{A_c} = h_{p,a} (T_p - T_f)$$

where $T_f$ is the **mean air temperature** $\approx (T_{fi} + T_{fo})/2$. Solving for $T_p - T_f$:

$$T_p - T_f = \frac{Q_u}{A_c h_{p,a}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into energy balance

$$G_T (\tau\alpha) = \frac{Q_u}{A_c} + U_L\!\left(T_f - T_a + \frac{Q_u}{A_c h_{p,a}}\right)$$

Grouping the $Q_u$ terms:

$$G_T (\tau\alpha) = U_L (T_f - T_a) + Q_u \left[\frac{1}{A_c} + \frac{U_L}{A_c h_{p,a}}\right]$$

Factor out $1/A_c$:

$$G_T (\tau\alpha) = U_L (T_f - T_a) + \frac{Q_u}{A_c}\left[1 + \frac{U_L}{h_{p,a}}\right]$$

### Step 4 — Define the collector efficiency factor $F'$

$$\frac{1}{F'} = 1 + \frac{U_L}{h_{p,a}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad F' = \frac{h_{p,a}}{h_{p,a} + U_L}$$

Hence:

$$\frac{Q_u}{A_c} = F'\left[G_T(\tau\alpha) - U_L(T_f - T_a)\right]$$

### Step 5 — Replace $T_f$ with $T_{fi}$ using the heat removal factor $F_R$

A mean fluid temperature $T_f$ is **not** directly measurable at a single point. We replace it with the **fluid inlet** temperature $T_{fi}$ by introducing the **heat removal factor** $F_R$, defined such that the actual $Q_u$ equals the $Q_u$ that would result if the entire absorber were at $T_{fi}$:

$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} c_p}{A_c U_L}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} c_p}\right)\right]$$

Algebraically (substituting $T_f = T_{fi} + Q_u/(2 \dot{m}c_p)$ from a linear temperature profile along the flow and simplifying):

$$\frac{Q_u}{A_c} = F_R\left[G_T(\tau\alpha) - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)\right]$$

### Step 6 — Final efficiency equation

Dividing by $G_T$:

$$\boxed{\eta_i = \frac{Q_u}{A_c G_T} = F_R (\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{T_{fi} - T_a}{G_T}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention check:** $T_{fi} > T_a$ ⇒ loss term is positive but is **subtracted**; if the air is colder than ambient, the loss term becomes negative and the efficiency can theoretically exceed $F_R(\tau\alpha)$ — but in practice this is bounded by the Second Law.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Air Temperature Rise in a Single-Pass Heater

**Given:** A single-pass FPSAH has $A_c = 2\ \text{m}^2$, $G_T = 900\ \text{W/m}^2$, $\eta_i = 0.55$, $\dot{m} = 0.015$ kg/s, $c_p = 1005$ J/kg·K, $T_{fi} = 30\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_a = 28\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Find $T_{fo}$.

### Step 1 — Compute useful heat

$$Q_u = \eta_i\, A_c\, G_T = 0.55 \times 2 \times 900 = 990\ \text{W}$$

### Step 2 — Apply air energy balance

$$Q_u = \dot{m} c_p (T_{fo} - T_{fi})$$

$$T_{fo} - T_{fi} = \frac{Q_u}{\dot{m} c_p} = \frac{990}{0.015 \times 1005} = \frac{990}{15.075} \approx 65.67\ \text{K}$$

### Step 3 — Final outlet temperature

$$T_{fo} = T_{fi} + 65.67 = 30 + 65.67 \approx 95.7\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is unrealistically high for a single-pass FPSAH; the assumed $\eta = 0.55$ at $G_T = 900$ W/m² with $T_{fi} - T_a = 2$ K is only consistent with very high $F_R$ and very low $U_L$. The point of the calculation is to expose students to **order-of-magnitude checks** — a frequent KTU valuation comment is *"candidate did not check the physical plausibility of the result"*.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Drop and Fan Power Sizing

**Given:** Rectangular duct, $w = 1.0$ m, $h = 0.05$ m, $L = 2.0$ m, $v = 2.0$ m/s, $T_{air} = 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $P_{atm} = 101.3$ kPa.

### Step 1 — Air properties at 50 °C

$\rho_a = P/(R_a T) = 101300 / (287 \times 323) = 1.092$ kg/m³  
$\mu_a \approx 1.96 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s

### Step 2 — Hydraulic diameter

$$D_h = \frac{4 (w h)}{2(w + h)} = \frac{4(0.05)}{2(1.05)} = \frac{0.20}{2.10} \approx 0.0952\ \text{m}$$

### Step 3 — Reynolds number

$$Re = \frac{\rho_a v D_h}{\mu_a} = \frac{1.092 \times 2.0 \times 0.0952}{1.96 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 10{,}608$$

Turbulent flow confirmed ($Re > 4000$).

### Step 4 — Blasius friction factor

$$f = 0.079\, Re^{-0.25} = 0.079 \times (10608)^{-0.25} = 0.079 \times 0.0983 \approx 0.00777$$

### Step 5 — Pressure drop

$$\Delta P = \frac{f L \rho_a v^2}{2 D_h} = \frac{0.00777 \times 2.0 \times 1.092 \times 4}{2 \times 0.0952}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{0.0679}{0.1904} \approx 0.357\ \text{Pa}$$

### Step 6 — Fan power (assume 60 % fan efficiency)

$$\dot{V} = w h v = 1.0 \times 0.05 \times 2.0 = 0.10\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$P_{fan} = \frac{\dot{V} \Delta P}{\eta_{fan}} = \frac{0.10 \times 0.357}{0.60} \approx 0.0595\ \text{W} \approx 60\ \text{mW}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU pitfall:** Many students forget to add **dynamic head** ($0.5 \rho v^2$) for the entry/exit losses, and **fittings losses** (each elbow ≈ 0.5–1.5 $D_h$ equivalent length). For an exam, mention "neglecting minor losses" explicitly if the question does not specify.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

> [!NOTE]
> The following **decision matrix** is a high-yield KTU tool: it links the typical problem statement to the governing equation.

| If the problem gives you … | Use this equation … | Compute … |
|---|---|---|
| $G_T$, $A_c$, $\eta_i$ | $Q_u = \eta_i A_c G_T$ | Useful heat |
| $Q_u$, $\dot{m}$, $c_p$, $T_{fi}$ | $T_{fo} = T_{fi} + Q_u/(\dot{m}c_p)$ | Outlet temperature |
| $\dot{m}$, $\Delta T$, $A_c$, $G_T$ | $\eta = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T / (A_c G_T)$ | Efficiency |
| $(\tau\alpha)$, $U_L$, $F_R$, $T_{fi}$, $T_a$, $G_T$ | $\eta_i = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)/G_T$ | Efficiency from first principles |
| $f$, $L$, $\rho$, $v$, $D_h$ | $\Delta P = f L \rho v^2 / (2 D_h)$ | Duct friction |
| $k_{ins}$, $L_{ins}$ | $U_b = k_{ins}/L_{ins}$ | Bottom loss |
| $\dot{V}$, $\Delta P$, $\eta_{fan}$ | $P_{fan} = \dot{V}\Delta P / \eta_{fan}$ | Fan power |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Single-Pass FPSAH

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SUN -- In-plane irradiance G_T] --> B[GLAZING -- tau]
    B --> C[ABSORBER PLATE -- alpha]
    C --> D[Useful Heat Q_u to air]
    C --> E[Top Loss U_t via cover]
    C --> F[Bottom Loss U_b via insulation]
    C --> G[Edge Loss U_e]
    D --> H[HEATED AIR STREAM]
    E --> I[SKY + AMBIENT]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> J[FAN/BLOWER -- Delta P]
    J --> K[DUCT to LOAD]
    K --> L[APPLICATION: Drying / Space Heating / Industrial]
```

**Reading the diagram:** solar energy enters from the left, branches at the absorber into useful heat (the desired output) and three loss channels (top, bottom, edge), and the heated air is finally delivered to the application. The fan/ blower block is a **parasitic load** that should be subtracted to compute *net* system efficiency.

## 4.2 Block-Level Comparison: Single-Pass vs Double-Pass FPSAH

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP[Single-Pass FPSAH]
        S1[Inlet] --> S2[Channel over absorber]
        S2 --> S3[Outlet]
    end
    subgraph DP[Double-Pass FPSAH]
        D1[Inlet] --> D2[Lower channel under absorber]
        D2 --> D3[Turnaround / U-bend]
        D3 --> D4[Upper channel over absorber]
        D4 --> D5[Outlet]
    end
```

**Key takeaway:** the double-pass configuration forces the air to extract heat from **both surfaces** of the absorber, raising outlet temperature by 30–50 % for the same $A_c$ and $\dot{m}$, at the cost of a slightly higher $\Delta P$ and a more complex plenum.

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of a V-Corrugated / Finned Absorber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sunlight G_T] --> B[Glazing tau]
    B --> C[V-Corrugated Absorber alpha_eff]
    C --> D[Fin surfaces: enhanced h_pa]
    C --> E[Corrugation valleys: trapped air increases residence time]
    D --> F[Air stream with Nu_increased]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Heated air to load]
```

**Interpretation:** V-corrugations act as **integrated longitudinal fins** and as **mini-ducts** that delay flow separation, increasing the convective heat transfer coefficient $h_{p,a}$ by 25–60 % over a flat plate.

## 4.4 End-to-End System Flow: Solar Air Heater + Crop Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Solar Collector Array] --> B[Hot Air Duct]
    B --> C[Reversible Fan with Inverter]
    C --> D[Drying Chamber with Perforated Floor]
    D --> E[Moisture-laden Exhaust]
    D --> F[Dried Product Output]
    G[Backup Heater -- biomass or electric] -.auxiliary.-> D
    C --> H[Photovoltaic Power Supply -- optional]
```

This is a **typical KTU 14-mark descriptive question** topic. The student should be able to identify the energy and mass flow at every node, and explain why solar-only drying is supplemented by a backup heater (intermittent solar radiation, night-time operation).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Solar Air Heater Problems**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** $c_p$ for air is 1005 J/kg·K (constant pressure) but some textbooks tabulate 1006 or 1010 — use 1005 unless a different value is given. Mixing per-kg and per-m³ units is the single biggest source of zero-mark answers.
> 2. **Forgetting $(\tau\alpha)$ vs $\tau\alpha$:** The bracket denotes the **product** of cover transmittance and plate absorptance, *not* their sum. Numerically $\tau\alpha \in [0.7, 0.85]$ for black-painted steel under one glass cover.
> 3. **Sign of loss term:** In $\eta_i = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)/G_T$, the loss term is **subtracted**. If a student writes "$+$" the answer is dimensionally fine but physically wrong.
> 4. **Confusing $F'$ and $F_R$:** $F'$ is a property of the *plate*; $F_R$ is a property of the *collector* (plate + flow). For an infinitely large $\dot{m}$, $F_R \to F'$.
> 5. **No stagnation check:** When $G_T = 0$ at noon on a winter day, $\eta_i$ can become negative. The "stagnation" temperature $T_{stag} = T_a + (\tau\alpha)G_T/U_L$ is what would occur if the fluid were stationary.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Heat Removal Factor" $F_R$ in a solar air heater. Write its expression and explain the limiting cases.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The heat removal factor $F_R$ is defined as the ratio of the *actual* useful heat gain of the collector to the useful heat gain that *would be obtained* if the entire absorber plate were at the fluid inlet temperature $T_{fi}$.
>
> $$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} c_p}{A_c U_L}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} c_p}\right)\right]$$
>
> **Limiting cases:**
> - As $\dot{m} \to \infty$ (very high flow), $F_R \to F'$ (the collector efficiency factor).
> - As $\dot{m} \to 0$ (no flow, stagnation), $F_R \to 0$.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any four advantages of using air as the working fluid in a solar collector over water.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **No freezing risk** — air does not freeze in sub-zero ambient conditions, eliminating the need for antifreeze loops.
2. **No corrosion / scaling** — air is non-corrosive; water causes scaling in pipes and absorber tubes.
3. **No leakage hazard** — air at low pressure is benign; water leaks damage interiors and insulation.
4. **Lower cost and simpler construction** — air ducts are cheaper than water piping and pumps; no expansion tank required.
5. *(Bonus)* — direct use possible: heated air can be used for drying without an extra heat exchanger.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks each)

### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of a **double-pass flat plate solar air heater (FPSAH)**. Compare its performance with a single-pass FPSAH. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-pass FPSAH has an aperture area of 2.5 m², $(\tau\alpha) = 0.78$, $F_R = 0.78$, and $U_L = 5.8$ W/m²·K. The hourly average in-plane irradiance is 780 W/m², ambient temperature is 30 °C, and air inlet temperature is 35 °C. Calculate:
  (i) the instantaneous thermal efficiency,
  (ii) the stagnation temperature, and
  (iii) the useful heat gain in watts. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Schematic** (describe in words; a labelled sketch in the answer booklet fetches an extra mark):

The double-pass FPSAH consists of a top glazing (single or double), an absorber plate with a **rectangular channel beneath it**, and a **second air channel** above the absorber. Air is admitted through an inlet at the bottom, travels along the lower channel (pass 1), turns 180° at the end-plenum, and returns along the upper channel above the absorber (pass 2) to the outlet.

**Working:** In pass 1 the air picks up heat by convection from the *underside* of the absorber; in pass 2 it picks up heat from the *top* of the absorber as well as gains from any re-radiated longwave energy. The doubling of the heat-transfer area effectively increases the **plate-to-air conductance** $h_{p,a}$, raising $F_R$ and overall efficiency.

**Performance comparison (1 mark tabulated):**

| Parameter | Single-Pass | Double-Pass |
|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer area per unit $A_c$ | 1 | 2 |
| Typical $\eta$ at $G_T = 800$ W/m² | 45–55 % | 55–70 % |
| Pressure drop $\Delta P$ | Low | ~1.5–2× higher |
| Pumping power | Low | Moderate |
| Construction complexity | Simple | Moderate (plenum) |
| Best application | Low-temp drying | Space heating, process heat |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The double-pass FPSAH offers 10–15 % absolute efficiency gain at the cost of higher pressure drop and is preferred when outlet temperature above 50 °C is required.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Given:** $A_c = 2.5\ \text{m}^2$, $(\tau\alpha) = 0.78$, $F_R = 0.78$, $U_L = 5.8$ W/m²·K, $G_T = 780$ W/m², $T_a = 30\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{fi} = 35\ ^\circ\text{C}$.

**(i) Instantaneous thermal efficiency (3 marks)**

$$\eta_i = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{T_{fi} - T_a}{G_T}$$

$$\eta_i = 0.78 \times 0.78 - 0.78 \times 5.8 \times \frac{35 - 30}{780}$$

$$\eta_i = 0.6084 - 0.78 \times 5.8 \times 0.00641$$

$$\eta_i = 0.6084 - 0.0290 = 0.5794 \approx \mathbf{57.94\ \%}$$

**(ii) Stagnation temperature (2 marks)**

$$T_{stag} = T_a + \frac{(\tau\alpha) G_T}{U_L} = 30 + \frac{0.78 \times 780}{5.8}$$

$$T_{stag} = 30 + \frac{608.4}{5.8} = 30 + 104.9 = \mathbf{134.9\ ^\circ\text{C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Stagnation temperature is what the absorber reaches when the fluid is *stationary* (no flow). It is the *upper* bound of operating temperature.

**(iii) Useful heat gain (2 marks)**

$$Q_u = \eta_i \cdot A_c \cdot G_T = 0.5794 \times 2.5 \times 780$$

$$Q_u = 0.5794 \times 1950 = \mathbf{1129.8\ W \approx 1.13\ kW}$$

**Final Answer:** $\eta_i \approx 57.9\%$, $T_{stag} \approx 134.9\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $Q_u \approx 1130$ W.

---

### **Question B [Alternative Choice – Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss efficiency equation** for a flat plate solar air heater, clearly defining each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with diagrams any **three types of solar air heater absorber geometries** (e.g., V-corrugated, finned, matrix). How does each enhance the heat transfer coefficient $h_{p,a}$? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

> See **Section 3.1 (Derivation 1)** of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation. The answer must include:
> 1. Energy balance on absorber plate (1 mark).
> 2. Introduction of $F' = h_{p,a}/(h_{p,a} + U_L)$ (2 marks).
> 3. Substitution of mean fluid temperature $T_f$ with inlet $T_{fi}$ via $F_R$ (2 marks).
> 4. Final boxed equation $\eta_i = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)/G_T$ (1 mark).
> 5. Definition of each term in the final equation (1 mark).

**Key definitions to mention:**
- $(\tau\alpha)$ — effective transmittance–absorptance product.
- $F_R$ — heat removal factor (function of $\dot{m}$, $c_p$, $A_c$, $U_L$, $F'$).
- $U_L$ — overall heat loss coefficient (top + bottom + edge).
- $T_{fi}, T_a$ — fluid inlet, ambient air temperatures.
- $G_T$ — total in-plane solar irradiance on the tilted collector.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**1. V-Corrugated Absorber (2 marks)**
The absorber plate is folded into a series of V-shaped corrugations aligned parallel to the airflow. The corrugations:
- Increase the surface area exposed to the air (geometric enhancement).
- Act as **longitudinal fins** that conduct heat from the corrugated crest to the valleys.
- Induce turbulence and boundary-layer reattachment, raising $h_{p,a}$ by **25–60 %** over a flat plate.

**2. Finned Absorber (2 marks)**
Discrete metal fins (rectangular, pin, or louvered) are welded to the underside of a flat absorber. The fin efficiency $\eta_f$ should be > 0.7 for the fins to be effective:

$$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(m H_f)}{m H_f}, \quad m = \sqrt{\frac{h_{p,a} P_f}{k_f A_{cs,f}}}$$

Effective heat transfer area $A_{eff} = A_p + \eta_f A_f$, giving a 30–80 % enhancement in $h_{p,a,eff}$.

**3. Matrix / Porous Absorber (2 marks)**
A packed bed of metal shavings, wire mesh, or ceramic pellets sits in the air channel. Air percolates through the matrix, experiencing:
- Extreme surface area per unit volume (often > 1000 m²/m³).
- **Recirculation and eddies** that disrupt the thermal boundary layer.
- $h_{p,a}$ enhancement of 200–500 % over a flat plate.
- *Disadvantage:* very high $\Delta P$ → requires a high-power blower.

**Comparative Summary (1 mark):**

| Geometry | $h_{p,a}$ enhancement | $\Delta P$ penalty | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flat | 1× | 1× | Lowest |
| V-corrugated | 1.3–1.6× | 1.2–1.5× | Moderate |
| Finned | 1.3–1.8× | 1.1–1.4× | Moderate |
| Matrix | 2.0–5.0× | 3–10× | Highest |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist in the last 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Solar air heater** = solar collector with **air** as the working fluid; ideal for low–medium temperature (40–80 °C) applications.
- [ ] **Components**: glazing + absorber + insulation + duct + (optional) fan.
- [ ] **Useful heat:** $Q_u = \dot{m} c_p (T_{fo} - T_{fi})$.
- [ ] **Efficiency:** $\eta_i = Q_u / (A_c G_T)$.
- [ ] **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss:** $\eta_i = F_R (\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)/G_T$.
- [ ] **$F_R$** ranges from 0 (no flow) to $F'$ (very high flow); for air heaters typically $F_R \in [0.65, 0.85]$.
- [ ] **$(\tau\alpha)$** for a single-glazed, black-painted plate ≈ **0.78** (board-exam default value).
- [ ] **$U_L$** typical values: 5–7 W/m²·K (one cover, well-insulated), 3–5 W/m²·K (two covers).
- [ ] **Stagnation temperature** $T_{stag} = T_a + (\tau\alpha)G_T/U_L$ — the maximum reachable plate temperature with **no** flow.
- [ ] **Bottom loss:** $U_b = k_{ins}/L_{ins}$; typical $k_{ins} = 0.04$ W/m·K (glass wool).
- [ ] **Wind convection (McAdams):** $h_{w,o} = 5.7 + 3.8 v_w$ (W/m²·K), where $v_w$ is in m/s.
- [ ] **Pressure drop:** $\Delta P = f L \rho v^2 / (2 D_h)$; Blasius $f = 0.079 Re^{-0.25}$.
- [ ] **Hydraulic diameter** for rectangular duct: $D_h = 2 w h / (w + h)$.
- [ ] **Optimum $\dot{m}$** maximizes *net* efficiency $\eta_{net} = (Q_u - P_{fan})/(A_c G_T)$.
- [ ] **Why air?:** No freeze, no corrosion, no scale, no leaks — but low $\rho c_p$ means high volumetric flow.
- [ ] **Why double-pass?** Heat transfer area doubled; +10–15 % absolute efficiency.
- [ ] **Why V-corrugated?** Higher $h_{p,a}$ via turbulence + fin effect; +25–60 %.
- [ ] **Why matrix?** Highest $h_{p,a}$ enhancement (2–5×); high $\Delta P$ penalty.
- [ ] **KTU exam defaults to remember:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴, $c_{p,air} = 1005$ J/kg·K, $\rho_{air} \approx 1.2$ kg/m³.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Crop Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET754 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Crop Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Solar Crop Drying** is the process of utilizing solar thermal energy to remove moisture from agricultural produce (grains, fruits, vegetables, herbs, and spices) by simultaneous heat and mass transfer, in order to reduce the moisture content to a safe storage level (typically **10–14% wet basis for grains** and **15–25% wet basis for fruits/vegetables**), thereby preventing microbial spoilage, insect infestation, and biochemical degradation during post-harvest storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Solar drying is defined as the controlled hygrothermal dehydration of agricultural products in an enclosure that traps incident solar radiation, elevates the drying-air temperature by **10–30 °C** above ambient, lowers the relative humidity, and enhances the moisture-removal rate by 1.5 to 4 times compared to traditional Open Sun Drying (OSD).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two identical wet cotton shirts hung out to dry on a Kerala monsoon morning:

- **Shirt A** is hung openly on a clothesline (this is *Open Sun Drying*). It depends entirely on the weather. If the sun goes behind a cloud, the shirt stays damp. Dust, birds, rain, and insects can damage or contaminate it. It takes a long time.

- **Shirt B** is placed inside a transparent plastic cabinet with a small chimney on top and a black tray at the bottom (this is a *Solar Cabinet Dryer*).
