<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]

**Define the term "Khal" in the context of wind energy land selection. Why is it considered a critical siting constraint in desert regions of India?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Khal** is a natural ephemeral drainage channel or seasonal watercourse found predominantly in the Thar Desert region of Rajasthan (Jaisalmer, Barmer, Bikaner districts). It remains dry for most of the year (8–10 months) but carries high-velocity flash-flood discharges during the southwest monsoon (July–September). **(1 mark)**

In wind farm siting, Khals act as critical exclusion constraints because: **(2 marks)**
- Wind turbine foundations located in or near a Khal face **scour and undermining** during flash floods.
- The seasonal water flow causes **lateral bank erosion**, threatening the structural integrity of the tower base.
- Floating debris (logs, boulders) carried by the flood can cause **direct impact damage**.
- A minimum **setback distance of 1.5 × D** (D = rotor diameter) from the Khal centerline is mandated to mitigate these risks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** A 1-mark answer for "definition only" is a partial response. To score full 3 marks, students MUST include the engineering implication (setback, scour, foundation risk).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]

**Differentiate between "Region" and "Area" as hierarchical levels of land classification for wind energy projects. Give one example of each from India.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Region | Area |
|---|---|---|
| Scale | Macro (100s of km²) | Meso (10s of km²) |
| Wind Data Source | Reanalysis / Atlas | Measured (Met-mast, LIDAR) |
| Example | Thar Desert (Rajasthan) | Jaisalmer wind pocket |

- **Region** is a broad macro-climatic zone with consistently high wind resource across decades, identified via wind atlases (e.g., NIWE Wind Atlas, Global Wind Atlas by DTU). Example: **Tamil Nadu's Southern Pass Region** (Aralvaimozhi–Muppandal corridor) or the **Rajasthan Desert Region**. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Area** is a quantified wind pocket within a Region, selected after on-site measurement of at least one year of validated wind data. Example: **Kayathar Area in Tamil Nadu** or **Jaisalmer–Pokhran Area in Rajasthan**. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided between two question sets.

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3, 14-Mark Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the hierarchical land-classification methodology for wind energy projects, from the **Region** level down to the **Khal** constraint level. Use a suitable diagram and the Indian Thar Desert as a case study. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A proposed wind farm in the Jaisalmer Area has the following specifications:
- Rotor diameter: $D = 130$ m
- Hub height: $h = 100$ m
- Mean wind speed at hub: $V = 7.2$ m/s
- Air density at site: $\rho = 1.08$ kg/m³
- Power coefficient: $C_p = 0.42$
- Combined mechanical + generator efficiency: $\eta_{m} \eta_g = 0.92$

Calculate: (i) Swept area, (ii) Total available wind power, (iii) Actual power output, (iv) Khal setback distance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:

1. **Region (Macro level):** A geographical zone with favorable macro-climatic wind regime. For Thar Desert, mean annual wind speed at 80 m exceeds 5.8 m/s, and the WPD exceeds 250 W/m² in most districts. Selection is based on the NIWE wind atlas and reanalysis data. **(2 marks)**

2. **Area (Meso level):** Within the Thar Region, specific Areas like Jaisalmer, Barmer, and Pokhran have been identified with measured wind speeds of 6.5–8.0 m/s. This is confirmed by met-mast installations of NIWE and private developers (Suzlon, Vestas, ReGen Power). **(2 marks)**

3. **Khal (Micro level):** The Thar Region is crisscrossed by ephemeral drainage channels (khals) that flow during July–September monsoons. Each turbine must maintain a setback of $\geq 1.5 D$ from any active khal centerline. Site geotechnical surveys map the khal network before finalizing micro-siting. **(2 marks)**

4. **Diagram (as in SECTION 4.1 above) carries 1 mark** for clear Region → Area → Khal flow.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:

**(i) Swept Area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (130)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 16900}{4} \approx 13273.23 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**(1 mark)**

**(ii) Total Available Wind Power:**

$$P_{avail} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,V^{3} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.08 \times 13273.23 \times (7.2)^{3}$$

$$= 0.54 \times 13273.23 \times 373.248$$

$$= 0.54 \times 13273.23 \times 373.248 \approx 2{,}675{,}232 \text{ W} \approx 2.675 \text{ MW}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Actual Power Output:**

$$P_{out} = C_p \cdot \eta_{mg} \cdot P_{avail} = 0.42 \times 0.92 \times 2.675$$

$$= 0.3864 \times 2.675 \approx 1.034 \text{ MW}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iv) Khal Setback Distance:**

$$d_{khal} = 1.5 \times D = 1.5 \times 130 = 195 \text{ m}$$

**(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write the **Betz-limited power** as the "available" power. The question asks for the **raw aerodynamic power** $\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ as "available," and then separately the **actual output** $C_p \eta_{mg} P_{avail}$. Mixing the two leads to loss of 1–2 marks.

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Describe the role of **NIWE (National Institute of Wind Energy)** in land resource assessment for wind energy in India. List the wind-rich Regions and Areas identified by NIWE, and explain how the institute differentiates a viable Area from a non-viable one. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the **Thar Desert Region**, explain in detail:
(i) The geomorphological origin and seasonal hydrology of Khals.
(ii) Three engineering risks that Khals pose to a wind turbine foundation.
(iii) The standard mitigation methodology (setback + bund design) recommended for Khal-adjacent turbines. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:

**Role of NIWE (formerly C-WET):**
- Apex body under the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE), headquartered in Chennai.
- Operates the national **Wind Resource Assessment (WRA)** program with 800+ monitored stations across India.
- Publishes the **Indian Wind Atlas** and validates developer-submitted wind data. **(2 marks)**

**Major wind-rich Regions and Areas identified:**
- **Region 1 — Tamil Nadu (Southern Pass Region):** Muppandal Area, Kayathar Area, Aralvaimozhi Area.
- **Region 2 — Rajasthan (Thar Desert Region):** Jaisalmer Area, Barmer Area, Bikaner Area.
- **Region 3 — Gujarat (Coastal + Kutch Region):** Kutch Area, Jamnagar Area.
- **Region 4 — Karnataka–AP Plateau:** Chitradurga Area, Anantapur Area. **(3 marks)**

**Viability differentiation criteria (NIWE):**
- An Area is **viable** if measured WPD at 80 m $\geq 200$ W/m² AND measured mean wind speed $\geq 5.8$ m/s, with at least 1 year of validated data.
- An Area is **non-viable** if any of the above thresholds fail, OR if grid evacuation distance exceeds 50 km, OR if land-acquisition (including Khal setbacks) leaves insufficient contiguous area. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:

**(i) Geomorphological origin and seasonal hydrology of Khals (2 marks):**
Khals are paleo-drainage channels carved into the Thar Desert's alluvial plains by episodic, high-energy monsoon flows over geological time. They have shallow, wide, sand-bed profiles with gently sloping banks. Hydrologically, they remain **dry for 8–10 months** but experience **2–4 flash-flood events per monsoon season**, with peak discharges that can reach several hundred cumecs within hours.

**(ii) Three engineering risks to wind turbine foundations (3 marks):**
1. **Scour of foundation soil** — rapid flowing water removes supporting soil around the pile cap, reducing bearing capacity.
2. **Lateral bank erosion** — meander migration of the khal can undercut a turbine pad located too close to the bank.
3. **Debris impact and buoyancy** — floating tree trunks, boulders, and trash carried by floodwaters can collide with the tower base; saturated soil loses shear strength.

**(iii) Standard mitigation methodology (2 marks):**
- **Setback:** Maintain a minimum distance of $1.5 \times D$ (rotor diameter) between the khal centerline and the nearest turbine pad edge.
- **Bund design:** Construct a compacted earth or geotextile-reinforced protective bund (typically 1.0–1.5 m high) on the khal-facing side, with proper drainage weep holes to relieve hydrostatic pressure.
- **Foundation depth:** Increase pile depth to penetrate below the maximum anticipated scour depth, with riprap armor around the pile cap.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning for Q.B:** A common error is to treat Khals as "ordinary rivers." They are **ephemeral flash-flood channels** — *not* perennial rivers. Confusing the two hydrological regimes costs students 1–2 marks in descriptive answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Land for wind energy** is classified hierarchically: **Region → Area → Site (Khal-constrained)**.
- [x] **Region** = macro-climatic wind zone (identified via wind atlases); example: Thar Desert, Southern Pass (TN), Kutch (Gujarat).
- [x] **Area** = measured, quantified wind pocket within a Region (verified by met-mast / LIDAR data, $\geq 1$ year).
- [x] **Khal** = ephemeral desert drainage channel (Thar-specific term) — a critical **setback constraint** for siting.
- [x] **Betz Limit** $C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ is the *theoretical* max efficiency — no turbine exceeds this.
- [x] **Wind Power Density (WPD) $\geq 200$ W/m²** is the NIWE/IEC 61400 threshold for commercial viability.
- [x] **Wind power scales as $V^{3}$** — small wind-speed gains yield large energy gains, making Area selection critical.
- [x] **Air density corrections** must be applied for hot (desert) and high-altitude sites; ~12% power loss for typical Rajasthani summer conditions.
- [x] **Khal setback** minimum is **$1.5 \times D$** (where $D$ = rotor diameter), or per state-specific regulation.
- [x] **Wind shear power law** $V_h = V_{ref}(h/h_{ref})^{\alpha}$ is used to extrapolate hub-height wind from reference-height measurements.
- [x] **NIWE** (Chennai) is the apex body for Indian wind resource assessment; their **Indian Wind Atlas** is the primary reference.
- [x] The **Rajasthan Thar Region** is unique in India due to its high WPD + khal hydrology coupling — a frequently asked KTU question.
- [x] Always distinguish **available power** $\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ from **extractable power** (Betz-limited) and **actual output** ($C_p \eta_{mg} P_{avail}$) in calculations.
- [x] The **swept area** of a rotor is $A = \pi D^{2}/4$ — a 130 m rotor sweeps ~13,273 m².
- [x] Modern offshore regions extend the Region–Area logic into India's EEZ, with the "khal analog" being subsea cable corridors and shipping lanes.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip (2024 Scheme):** For any 7-mark question on Regions/Areas/Khals, structure your answer as: **(1)** definition, **(2)** physical basis, **(3)** Indian example, **(4)** engineering implication. This 4-part structure maps cleanly to the KTU mark-splitting pattern (1.5 + 2 + 1.5 + 2 = 7) used by Board examiners.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: MODES OF WIND POWER GENERATION & SMALL HYDEL POWER PLANTS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Modes of wind power generation define *how* the kinetic energy of wind is converted into usable electrical energy and *delivered* to the end user. The classification based on load interface is the single most important concept for KTU board examinations.

### 1.1.1 Formal KTU Definition

A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is a complete electromechanical assembly that converts the kinetic energy of moving air into electrical energy. Based on the nature of the electrical load and grid interconnection, WECS is classified into three primary modes of operation:

1. **Standalone (Isolated) Mode** — A self-contained system supplying power directly to a dedicated, off-grid load (e.g., remote village, water pump, telecom tower).
2. **Backup (Hybrid) Mode** — A WECS paired with a conventional or auxiliary energy source (diesel generator, battery bank, or solar PV) to ensure **continuous, uninterrupted power supply** when wind is insufficient.
3. **Grid-Connected (Utility-Interactive) Mode** — A WECS synchronized with the main utility grid, feeding excess power into the grid and drawing power when generation is low.

A **Small Hydel Power Plant (SHPP)** is a hydroelectric generating station with an installed capacity typically **up to 25 MW** (as per the Indian Ministry of New and Renewable Energy, MNRE classification), utilizing small streams, run-of-river flows, or small reservoir heads.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank" Intuition

Imagine your home's water supply system:

- **Standalone Mode** is like a personal overhead water tank — you fill it when it rains (wind blows) and use it only for your house. If the tank empties, you wait for the next rain.
- **Backup Mode** is like having a personal tank **AND** a municipal backup connection. When the tank runs dry, the municipality (diesel/battery) automatically takes over.
- **Grid-Connected Mode** is like being connected directly to the municipal pipeline. You take what you need and push surplus back; the pipeline (grid) handles all fluctuations.

For small hydro, think of the **same fan-blower principle**, but the "wind" is a controlled flow of water through a penstock. A small stream on a hillside is the wind, the penstock is the ducting, and the turbine is the propeller.

### 1.1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Reference)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Air density at sea level | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Specific weight of water | $\gamma_w$ | **9810 N/m³** |
| Density of water | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Swept area of wind turbine | $A$ | $\pi r^2$ (m²) |
| Power coefficient theoretical max (Betz limit) | $C_{p,max}$ | **0.593 (16/27)** |
| MNRE Small Hydro Upper Limit | $P$ | **≤ 25 MW** |
| Micro Hydro Upper Limit | $P$ | **≤ 100 kW** |
| Mini Hydro Upper Limit | $P$ | **100 kW – 2 MW** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always mention **Betz Limit = 59.3%** in any derivation question. Losing this value costs 1–2 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Mode 1 — Standalone (Isolated) Wind Power Generation

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

In standalone mode, the WECS is **physically isolated** from any utility grid. The generated electricity is consumed entirely by a local, dedicated load. To manage the **inherent intermittency** of wind, energy storage (battery banks) and dump loads (diversion loads) are essential.

### 2.1.2 System Architecture (Logical Steps)

1. **Wind Capture** — Wind strikes the rotor blades, extracting kinetic energy.
2. **Mechanical Transmission** — Gearbox steps up low rotor RPM to generator-rated RPM.
3. **Electrical Generation** — A self-excited induction generator (SEIG) or permanent magnet synchronous generator (PMSG) produces variable-frequency, variable-voltage AC.
4. **Power Conditioning** — Rectifier converts AC to DC; inverter produces regulated 50 Hz AC.
5. **Storage Interface** — Battery bank stores surplus and supplies deficit via charge controller.
6. **Dump Load** — Excess power (when batteries are full and load is light) is diverted to a resistive heater to prevent over-speeding.

### 2.1.3 Components Block List

- Wind Turbine Rotor (HAWT or VAWT)
- Gearbox (if used)
- Generator (PMSG preferred for low-wind standalone)
- Rectifier & MPPT Charge Controller
- Battery Energy Storage System (BESS)
- Inverter (DC → AC, 50 Hz)
- Dump Load (diversion controller)
- Dedicated Load (village, water pump, telecom)

### 2.1.4 Engineering Utility

Standalone WECS is the **lifeline of remote off-grid electrification** in Kerala's hill districts (Wayanad, Idukki) and India's defence outposts, Lakshadweep islands, and rural telecom towers where grid extension is uneconomical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PMSG over SEIG for standalone?** Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generators operate efficiently at low cut-in wind speeds (~2.5 m/s) and do not require external reactive power support, making them ideal for remote battery-charging systems.

---

## 2.2 Mode 2 — Backup (Hybrid) Wind Power Generation

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

Backup mode (also called **wind-diesel hybrid**, **wind-PV hybrid**, or **wind-battery hybrid**) supplements the WECS with a **secondary conventional or renewable source** to guarantee load continuity. The system uses an **intelligent Energy Management System (EMS)** to switch between sources based on availability.

### 2.2.2 System Architecture (Logical Steps)

1. **Primary Source (Wind)** — Generates whenever wind speed $v \geq v_{cut-in}$.
2. **Auxiliary Source (Diesel Genset / PV / Micro-hydro)** — Engages when battery State-of-Charge (SoC) drops below threshold (typically **30–40%**).
3. **EMS Controller** — Continuously monitors SoC, wind speed, and load demand.
4. **Automatic Transfer Switch (ATS)** — Routes power from the appropriate source to the AC bus.
5. **Rectifier-Charger** — Diesel genset or PV array charges the battery bank during low-wind periods.

### 2.2.3 Typical Hybrid Topologies

| Topology | Sources | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| Wind-Diesel | WECS + Diesel Genset + Battery | Remote coastal islands |
| Wind-PV | WECS + Solar PV + Battery | High-solar, moderate-wind zones |
| Wind-Microhydro | WECS + Micro-hydro + Battery | Hilly terrain (e.g., Wayanad) |

### 2.2.4 Engineering Utility

Eliminates the **"no-wind" blackout** risk of pure standalone systems. Widely deployed in **Andaman & Nicobar Islands**, **Lakshadweep**, and **border outposts** by MNRE under the "Remote Village Electrification Programme."

---

## 2.3 Mode 3 — Grid-Connected Wind Turbine Generators

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

The generator output is **synchronized** with the utility grid (50 Hz, 11 kV / 33 kV / 110 kV in India). Power electronics (IGBT-based inverters) condition the variable-frequency, variable-voltage output to match grid specifications. There is **no battery storage** in standard grid-connected systems — the grid itself acts as an infinite sink.

### 2.3.2 System Architecture (Logical Steps)

1. **Wind Rotor** — Captures kinetic energy; rotor diameter up to **160 m** (modern offshore units).
2. **Gearbox (in DFIG)** or **Direct-Drive (in PMSG)** — Couples rotor to generator.
3. **Generator (DFIG / PMSG / SCIG)** — Produces variable-voltage, variable-frequency AC.
4. **Power Converter (AC-DC-AC)** — Rectifies to DC, then inverts to grid-matched 50 Hz AC.
5. **Step-Up Transformer** — Boosts voltage to grid level (e.g., 690 V → 11 kV).
6. **Grid Synchronization** — Phase, frequency, and voltage matching via PLL (Phase-Locked Loop).
7. **Point of Common Coupling (PCC)** — Power injection point with protective switchgear.

### 2.3.3 Generator Topologies Compared

| Generator Type | Abbreviation | Speed Operation | Converter Rating | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Squirrel Cage Induction Generator | SCIG | Fixed (≈1500 RPM) | Full (100%) | Low |
| Doubly-Fed Induction Generator | DFIG | Variable (limited) | Partial (≈30%) | Medium |
| Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator | PMSG | Variable (full range) | Full (100%) | High |
| Wound Rotor Synchronous Generator | WRSG | Variable (full range) | Full (100%) | High |

### 2.3.4 Engineering Utility

Grid-connected wind farms (e.g., **Muppandal Wind Farm, Tamil Nadu – 1500 MW**, **Jaisalmer Wind Park, Rajasthan – 1064 MW**, **Kayamkulam, Kerala – 2 MW pilot**) feed bulk power to the State Load Despatch Centre (SLDC). India targets **500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030**, with wind contributing ~140 GW.

---

## 2.4 Small Hydel Power Plants (SHPP)

### 2.4.1 Operating Principle

A small hydel plant converts the **potential energy** of water stored at a head into electricity using a turbine-generator set. Unlike large hydro (dam-based), SHPPs are predominantly **run-of-river** with minimal or no storage, ensuring **continuous generation** without submerging large tracts of land.

### 2.4.2 Classification by Capacity (MNRE Standard)

| Class | Capacity Range | Typical Head | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Micro Hydro | Up to 100 kW | High head (>100 m) | Single village, remote homes |
| Mini Hydro | 100 kW – 2 MW | Medium head (30–100 m) | Small community, farm |
| Small Hydro | 2 MW – 25 MW | Low to medium head | Grid-feeding, industrial |

### 2.4.3 System Architecture (Logical Steps)

1. **Intake Weir / Barrage** — Diverts stream flow into the water conductor system.
2. **Desilting Basin / Trash Rack** — Removes sediment and debris.
3. **Forebay Tank** — Provides stable water surface before the penstock.
4. **Penstock** — Pressurized pipeline carrying water to the powerhouse.
5. **Turbine** — Converts hydraulic energy to mechanical (Pelton, Francis, Kaplan, Turgo).
6. **Generator** — Coupled to turbine, produces electricity (usually synchronous).
7. **Tailrace** — Discharges used water back to the river.
8. **Switchyard / Transformer** — Steps up voltage for grid or local distribution.

### 2.4.4 Turbine Selection Criterion

| Head (m) | Discharge | Turbine Type |
|---|---|---|
| High (>300 m) | Low | **Pelton** (impulse) |
| Medium (30–300 m) | Medium | **Francis** (reaction) |
| Low (<30 m) | High | **Kaplan / Propeller** (reaction) |

### 2.4.5 Engineering Utility

India has an estimated **21,000 MW small hydro potential**, of which only ~**5,000 MW** is exploited (as of 2024 MNRE data). Kerala, Karnataka, and Himachal Pradesh are leading SHPP states. SHPPs offer **95%+ plant availability**, **50+ year lifetime**, and **zero carbon emissions** during operation.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** Memorize all formulas below. Questions in Part B (14 marks) frequently ask for derivations based on the first two equations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wind Power Available | $P_w = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | W | $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ at sea level |
| 2 | Swept Area of Rotor | $A = \pi r^2 = \pi D^2 / 4$ | m² | — |
| 3 | Power Extracted by Turbine | $P_t = C_p \cdot P_w = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$ | W | $C_p \leq 0.593$ (Betz) |
| 4 | Betz Coefficient | $C_{p,Betz} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | dimensionless | Theoretical max |
| 5 | Tip Speed Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{v} = \dfrac{\pi R n}{30 v}$ | dimensionless | Optimal $\lambda^*$ is turbine-specific |
| 6 | Solidity | $\sigma = \dfrac{N c}{\pi R}$ | dimensionless | N = blade count, c = chord |
| 7 | Hydro Power (Theoretical) | $P_h = \rho_w g Q H$ | W | $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s² |
| 8 | Hydro Power (Metric form) | $P_h = 9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H$ | kW | Q in m³/s, H in m |
| 9 | Hydro Power (Practical) | $P_h = 9.81 \cdot \eta_o \cdot Q \cdot H$ | kW | $\eta_o$ = overall efficiency (0.75–0.85) |
| 10 | Specific Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | (metric) | For turbine classification |
| 11 | Energy from Wind (Annual) | $E = P_t \cdot 8760 \cdot CF$ | kWh | CF = Capacity Factor |
| 12 | Capacity Factor | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{rated}}$ | dimensionless | Wind: 0.20–0.40; SHPP: 0.40–0.60 |
| 13 | Energy from SHPP (Annual) | $E = 9.81 \cdot \eta_o \cdot Q \cdot H \cdot 8760 \cdot CF$ | kWh | — |
| 14 | Wind Energy in Pipe | $E = P_{avail} \cdot \text{hours/yr}$ | kWh | Depends on wind regime |

> [!WARNING]

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate Active:** Every algebraic step is explicitly shown. No truncation, no "similarly we obtain," no shortcuts. Each step is followed by a plain-English explanation row.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wind Power Equation (Foundation Derivation)

### Objective
To derive the fundamental equation $P_w = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ from the kinetic energy of an air mass.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the mass flow rate of air through the turbine swept area.**

Consider a cylindrical column of air of cross-sectional area $A$ (m²) moving with velocity $v$ (m/s) perpendicular to the turbine rotor plane. In time $t$ seconds, the length of air column that passes the rotor is $L = v \cdot t$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Volume of air passing per second} &= A \cdot v \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{s}) \\
\text{Mass of air passing per second} &= \dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \quad (\text{kg}/\text{s})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* We are calculating how much air "strikes" the rotor each second. Air density $\rho$ converts volume to mass; area $A$ captures the rotor's footprint; velocity $v$ captures how fast the air moves through that footprint.

**Step 2: Write the kinetic energy of a moving mass.**

The kinetic energy of a mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ is given by Newton's classical mechanics:

$$
\begin{aligned}
KE &= \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \quad (\text{Joules})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Kinetic energy is the energy of motion; it scales with the square of velocity because doubling speed quadruples energy.

**Step 3: Substitute mass flow rate into the kinetic energy equation to get power.**

Power is energy per unit time. Replacing $m$ in Step 2 with the mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho A v$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_w &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot (\rho A v) \cdot v^2 \\
P_w &= \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 \quad (\text{Watts})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* By replacing the static mass $m$ with the rate at which mass crosses the rotor plane ($\dot{m}$), the formula now yields energy per second, i.e., power. The $v^3$ dependence emerges because velocity appears once in $\dot{m}$ and squared in the kinetic energy term — a key result for wind power.

**Step 4: State the final boxed result.**

$$
\boxed{P_w = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 \;\; [\text{Watts}]}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical Check (KTU-Style Quick Verification).**

For a turbine with rotor diameter $D = 5$ m, wind speed $v = 12$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (5)^2}{4} = 19.635 \;\text{m}^2 \\
P_w &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 19.635 \times (12)^3 \\
P_w &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 19.635 \times 1728 \\
P_w &= 20{,}776 \;\text{W} \approx 20.78 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the *gross* available wind power. Actual turbine output is a fraction ($C_p$) of this value, and further multiplied by generator + gearbox + transformer efficiencies.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Betz Limit Derivation

### Objective
To derive the maximum fraction of wind kinetic energy that an *ideal* turbine can extract, showing $C_{p,max} = 16/27$.

### Setup & Assumptions

- Air is an ideal, incompressible, non-viscous fluid.
- Rotor is an ideal "disc" with no rotational wake.
- Far upstream velocity = $v_1$, far downstream velocity = $v_2$, rotor plane velocity = $v$.
- Pressure jump across rotor = $\Delta p$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Apply continuity (mass conservation).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= \rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A v = \rho A_2 v_2 \quad \text{(mass in = mass out)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The air mass entering the rotor disc per second equals the mass leaving. We assume the disc area $A$ is the relevant area for the rotor plane.

**Step 2: Apply Euler's momentum equation (force on rotor).**

The thrust force on the rotor equals the rate of change of momentum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \dot{m} (v_1 - v_2) \\
F &= \rho A v (v_1 - v_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The rotor slows the wind down from $v_1$ to $v_2$; the momentum change per second equals the reaction force (thrust) on the rotor.

**Step 3: Apply Bernoulli's equation across the rotor (energy conservation).**

Assuming the rotor is a streamline surface, pressure rises on the upstream side and drops downstream:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= (p^+ - p^-) A = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The pressure difference across the disc multiplied by area equals the thrust force.

**Step 4: Power extracted by the rotor.**

Power is force times the velocity at the disc plane:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= F \cdot v = \rho A v^2 (v_1 - v_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The useful work extracted equals the thrust force times the velocity at the disc itself.

**Step 5: Apply the kinematic relation from continuity.**

The disc velocity $v$ is the *average* of upstream and downstream velocities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* In an ideal actuator disc model, the velocity at the disc is the arithmetic mean of far-upstream and far-downstream values.

**Step 6: Substitute $v$ into the power equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \rho A \left(\frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}\right)^2 (v_1 - v_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Introduce the axial induction factor $a$.**

Define $a$ such that $v = v_1 (1 - a)$. Then $v_2 = v_1 (1 - 2a)$.

*Explanation:* The induction factor $a$ represents the fractional slowing of the wind at the rotor plane. A value of $a = 0$ means no extraction; $a = 1/3$ yields the Betz optimum.

**Step 8: Express power in terms of $a$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3 \cdot 4a(1-a)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The first factor is the available wind power $P_w = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3$, and the bracket is the power coefficient $C_p$.

**Step 9: Identify the power coefficient.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= 4a(1-a)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The fraction of wind power captured by the rotor is $C_p$, a function solely of the induction factor.

**Step 10: Maximize $C_p$ with respect to $a$.**

Differentiate and set to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_p}{da} &= 4 \left[(1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)\right] = 0 \\
\frac{dC_p}{da} &= 4(1-a)\left[(1-a) - 2a\right] = 0 \\
\frac{dC_p}{da} &= 4(1-a)(1 - 3a) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Setting the derivative to zero gives two roots: $a = 1$ (trivial, no power) and $a = 1/3$ (the optimum).

**Step 11: Substitute $a = 1/3$ into $C_p$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{p,max} &= 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 \\
C_{p,max} &= \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 \\
C_{p,max} &= \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{p,Betz} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \approx 59.3\%}
$$

*Explanation:* The famous Betz Limit: no turbine, however perfect, can extract more than 59.3% of the kinetic energy in wind. Real-world turbines achieve $C_p$ in the range 0.35–0.45.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydro Power Equation

### Objective
To derive $P_h = \rho_w g Q H$ for small hydro plants.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the available hydraulic head.**

Let $H$ be the vertical height (in meters) through which water falls from the forebay to the turbine.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \text{Net head (m)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Head is the pressure-driving potential of the water column. It is the elevation difference minus pipe friction and velocity-head losses.

**Step 2: Compute the potential energy of a mass $m$ at height $H$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PE &= m g H \quad (\text{Joules})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* A mass $m$ of water at elevation $H$ stores potential energy equal to its weight ($mg$) times its height.

**Step 3: Express power as energy per unit time.**

The mass flow rate is $\dot{m} = \rho_w Q$, where $Q$ is the volumetric discharge (m³/s).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_h &= \dot{m} \cdot g \cdot H \\
P_h &= \rho_w \cdot Q \cdot g \cdot H
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Each kilogram of water falling releases $gH$ joules; with $\rho_w Q$ kilograms flowing per second, the power is the product.

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values for water.**

With $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_h &= 9810 \cdot Q \cdot H \quad (\text{W}) \\
P_h &= 9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H \quad (\text{kW})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Include overall efficiency.**

Accounting for turbine, generator, and transformer losses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_h &= 9.81 \cdot \eta_o \cdot Q \cdot H \quad (\text{kW})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_h = 9.81 \cdot \eta_o \cdot Q \cdot H \;\;[\text{kW}]}
$$

*Explanation:* $\eta_o = \eta_t \times \eta_g \times \eta_{tr}$ is the overall efficiency (typically 0.75–0.85 for small hydro).

**Step 6: Numerical Worked Example (KTU Practice).**

A small hydro plant has $H = 20$ m, $Q = 2$ m³/s, $\eta_o = 0.82$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_h &= 9.81 \times 0.82 \times 2 \times 20 \\
P_h &= 321.77 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Modes of Wind Power Generation

| Parameter | Standalone | Backup (Hybrid) | Grid-Connected |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Grid Connection** | None | Optional (isolated microgrid) | Mandatory |
| **Storage (Battery)** | Required | Required | Not required (grid acts as buffer) |
| **Auxiliary Source** | None | Diesel / PV / Micro-hydro | Grid itself |
| **Generator Type** | PMSG preferred | PMSG / SEIG | DFIG / PMSG / SCIG |
| **Power Electronics** | Rectifier + Inverter | Rectifier + Inverter + ATS | Full-scale AC-DC-AC converter |
| **Capacity Range** | 1 kW – 100 kW | 5 kW – 1 MW | 250 kW – 10 MW per unit |
| **Capacity Factor** | 0.15 – 0.25 | 0.30 – 0.45 | 0.25 – 0.40 |
| **Cost of Energy (₹/kWh)** | 8 – 14 | 5 – 9 | 3 – 5 |
| **Typical Application** | Remote villages, telecom | Island grids, defense | Wind farms, utility feed-in |
| **Complexity** | Low–Medium | High | Medium–High |
| **Reliability** | Wind-dependent | High (multi-source) | Very high (grid supported) |

---

## 3.5 Turbine Selection Logic for Small Hydro

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} \quad (\text{metric, kW, m units})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Specific speed $N_s$ identifies which turbine geometry best suits a given head and flow combination:

- **Low $N_s$ ($N_s < 30$, metric):** Pelton (impulse, high head)
- **Medium $N_s$ ($30 < N_s < 150$):** Francis (reaction, medium head)
- **High $N_s$ ($N_s > 300$):** Kaplan / Propeller (reaction, low head)

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!IMPORTANT]

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between Standalone and Grid-Connected Wind Energy Conversion Systems. List any two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Standalone WECS | Grid-Connected WECS |
|---|---|---|
| Grid Connection | None | Mandatory |
| Battery Storage | Required | Not required |
| Generator | PMSG (preferred) | DFIG, PMSG, SCIG |
| Cost | Higher (due to battery) | Lower (no storage) |
| Reliability | Wind-dependent | Supported by grid |
| Application | Remote off-grid | Wind farms |

**Standalone Advantages:**
1. Provides electricity to remote locations where grid extension is uneconomical.
2. Reduces transmission and distribution (T&D) losses since power is consumed at the generation site.

**Grid-Connected Advantages:**
1. No battery storage needed — the grid acts as an infinite buffer/sink.
2. Lower cost of energy (₹3–5/kWh) compared to standalone (₹8–14/kWh).

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: State and explain the Betz Limit. Why can a real wind turbine not exceed this limit?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Betz Limit** is the theoretical maximum fraction of kinetic energy in wind that an ideal turbine can extract. It equals $\dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ or **59.3%**.

A real turbine cannot exceed this limit because:
1. **Aerodynamic losses** — friction, turbulence, and wake rotation reduce efficiency.
2. **Mechanical losses** — gearbox friction, generator heat, and bearing losses.
3. **Electrical losses** — copper losses, iron losses, and converter losses.
4. **Wake rotation** — the air leaving the rotor has residual swirl, carrying away unconverted energy.

Real-world $C_p$ values are typically **0.35–0.45** for modern three-blade HAWTs.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** Two completely independent questions must be provided. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full 14 marks). Both are presented below for parallel practice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: With neat block diagrams, explain the following modes of Wind Energy Conversion Systems:**
**(a)** Standalone Mode — components, working, and applications (7 marks)
**(b)** Grid-Connected Mode — components, working, and types of generators used (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Standalone Mode (7 Marks)

**Components:**
1. Wind turbine rotor (HAWT or VAWT)
2. Gearbox (optional in direct-drive systems)
3. Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator (PMSG)
4. Rectifier
5. MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking) charge controller
6. Battery Energy Storage System (BESS)
7. Inverter (DC → AC)
8. Dump load (diversion load)
9. Dedicated load

**Working:**

When wind speed exceeds the cut-in value (typically 2.5–3 m/s), the rotor drives the PMSG. The variable-frequency AC output is rectified to DC, regulated by the MPPT charge controller, and stored in the battery bank. The inverter converts DC to 50 Hz AC for the load. When the battery is fully charged and the load demand is low, the dump load absorbs surplus power to prevent over-speeding and over-voltage.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to SECTION 4, Diagram 1)*

**Applications:**
- Remote village electrification
- Water pumping for agriculture
- Telecom tower power supply
- Street lighting in rural areas
- Coastal and island communities

#### Part (b) — Grid-Connected Mode (7 Marks)

**Components:**
1. Wind turbine rotor (typically 3-blade HAWT)
2. Gearbox (or direct-drive)
3. Generator (SCIG / DFIG / PMSG)
4. Power electronic converter (AC-DC-AC with IGBTs)
5. Step-up transformer
6. Point of Common Coupling (PCC)
7. Grid synchronization controller (PLL)
8. Pitch and yaw control system

**Working:**

The rotor converts wind kinetic energy into mechanical rotation, which drives the generator. The variable-voltage, variable-frequency AC output is conditioned by a back-to-back converter (rectifier → DC link → inverter) to produce grid-compatible 50 Hz AC. A step-up transformer raises the voltage to grid level (e.g., 690 V → 11 kV). The PLL controller ensures phase, frequency, and voltage synchronization before connection at the PCC. The pitch controller adjusts blade angle to limit power at high wind speeds.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to SECTION 4, Diagram 3)*

**Types of Generators Used:**
- **SCIG (Squirrel Cage Induction Generator):** Fixed-speed, robust, low cost, but needs full converter.
- **DFIG (Doubly-Fed Induction Generator):** Variable-speed (limited range), partial converter (≈30%), most popular for utility-scale.
- **PMSG (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator):** Variable-speed (full range), no gearbox (direct-drive), high efficiency.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: Explain the working of a Small Hydropower Plant with a neat schematic. Derive the equation for power available from a small hydro plant. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Working with Schematic (7 Marks)

A small hydropower plant (capacity ≤ 25 MW) typically operates on a **run-of-river** basis with minimal water storage. The major components are:

1. **Intake Weir / Barrage** — Diverts river water into the water conductor system.
2. **Trash Rack** — Prevents debris from entering the penstock.
3. **Desilting Basin** — Settles suspended sediments.
4. **Forebay Tank** — Provides a stable water surface immediately upstream of the penstock.
5. **Penstock** — A high-pressure pipeline that conveys water from the forebay to the powerhouse.
6. **Turbine** — Converts hydraulic energy to mechanical energy (Pelton for high head, Francis for medium head, Kaplan for low head).
7. **Generator** — A synchronous generator coupled to the turbine produces electricity.
8. **Governor** — Controls turbine speed by adjusting water flow.
9. **Tailrace** — Discharges used water back into the river.
10. **Switchyard and Transformer** — Steps up voltage for grid connection.

**Schematic:** *(Refer to SECTION 4, Diagram 4)*

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Hydro Power Equation (7 Marks)

*(Full step-by-step derivation is provided in SECTION 3, Derivation 3. The expected KTU answer is summarized below.)*

Let $H$ be the net head (m), $Q$ be the discharge (m³/s), and $\rho_w$ be the density of water (1000 kg/m³).

**Step 1:** Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho_w Q$ (kg/s).

**Step 2:** Potential energy of mass $m$ at height $H$: $PE = mgH$.

**Step 3:** Power = energy per unit time:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_h &= \dot{m} \cdot g \cdot H \\
P_h &= \rho_w \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \quad (\text{W})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** With $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_h &= 9810 \cdot Q \cdot H \quad (\text{W}) \\
P_h &= 9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H \quad (\text{kW})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Including overall efficiency $\eta_o$:

$$
\boxed{P_h = 9.81 \cdot \eta_o \cdot Q \cdot H \;\;[\text{kW}]}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Missing Betz Limit Mention (–1 to –2 marks):**
> Any derivation of wind power or $C_p$ without stating $C_{p,Betz} = 16/27 \approx 59.3\%$ is considered incomplete by KTU examiners. Always box the value.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2 — Wrong Density of Air/Water (–1 mark):**
> Confusing $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³ with $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ in cross-system problems leads to massive numerical errors. State the density at the start of every problem.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3 — Confusing Swept Area Formula (–1 mark):**
> Use $A = \pi r^2$ or $A = \pi D^2 / 4$, **not** $\pi r^2 / 2$. Examiners specifically check this.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 4 — Missing Efficiency Term in Hydro (–2 marks):**
> Writing $P_h = 9.81 QH$ without $\eta_o$ loses 2 marks. Always include the overall efficiency term and define what it represents.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 5 — Standalone vs Hybrid Confusion (–1 mark):**
> A standalone WECS with battery is **not** a hybrid system. A hybrid system **must** have at least one auxiliary source besides the battery (diesel, PV, micro-hydro).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision:** Memorize these bullet points verbatim.

### Modes of Wind Power Generation

- [ ] **Standalone Mode** = WECS + Battery + Dump Load, no grid connection; uses PMSG; for remote loads.
- [ ] **Backup (Hybrid) Mode** = WECS + Auxiliary Source (Diesel/PV/Micro-hydro) + EMS + Battery; for critical loads.
- [ ] **Grid-Connected Mode** = WECS + Power Converter + Transformer + PCC; uses DFIG/PMSG/SCIG; no battery; feeds utility grid.
- [ ] All three modes share the fundamental equation $P_w = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$.
- [ ] Betz Limit $C_{p,Betz} = 16/27 \approx 59.3\%$ — universal upper bound.
- [ ] Generator preference: PMSG (standalone) → DFIG (grid-connected) → SCIG (legacy grid).

### Small Hydel Power Plants

- [ ] MNRE definition: Small Hydro ≤ **25 MW**; Mini Hydro = 100 kW – 2 MW; Micro Hydro ≤ 100 kW.
- [ ] Power equation: $P_h = 9.81 \cdot \eta_o \cdot Q \cdot H$ (kW), where Q in m³/s, H in m.
- [ ] **Turbine Selection:** High head → Pelton; Medium head → Francis; Low head → Kaplan.
- [ ] Specific speed $N_s = N\sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$ classifies turbine type.
- [ ] Mostly **run-of-river** with minimal storage; environment-friendly.
- [ ] Components (in order): Intake → Trash Rack → Desilting Basin → Forebay → Penstock → Turbine → Generator → Tailrace → Switchyard.
- [ ] Kerala potential ≈ 700 MW; India potential ≈ 21,000 MW (only ~5,000 MW exploited as of 2024).

### Key Numerical Values to Memorize

- [ ] $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³
- [ ] $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³
- [ ] $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- [ ] $C_{p,Betz} = 0.593$ (16/27)
- [ ] Betz optimal induction factor: $a = 1/3$
- [ ] Small Hydro upper limit: 25 MW
- [ ] Hydro constant: 9.81 kW per (m³/s × m)
- [ ] Grid frequency in India: 50 Hz

### Common KTU Board Question Patterns

- [ ] 3-mark: Define Betz Limit; classify WECS modes.
- [ ] 7-mark: Explain standalone/grid-connected with diagram; derive hydro power equation.
- [ ] 14-mark: Combined mode-comparison + small hydro derivation + numerical problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification and Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET754 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Classification and Advantages # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET754 – Renewable Energy Engineering)**, the term **"Classification of Renewable Energy Sources"** refers to the systematic categorization of naturally replenishing energy resources based on their origin, state of availability, conversion technology, and end-use application. According to the **International Energy Agency (IEA)** and the **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE), Government of India**, renewable energy sources (RES) are defined as energy resources that are replenished at a rate faster than or equal to their rate of consumption, ensuring a sustainable supply over a human timescale. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET754 – Module 3):** > Renewable energy sources are classified based on **(i) Origin of energy** (solar, gravitational, geothermal), **(ii) Form of energy** (radiant, kinetic, thermal, chemical), and **(iii) Conversion pathway** (direct thermal, direct electric, indirect via biomass). The **advantages** are evaluated across **environmental, economic, social, and technical** dimensions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of Earth's energy resources like a giant family tree. Just as a family tree is grouped into grandparents, parents, and children based on lineage, **renewable energy sources are grouped based on their "ancestry" — the fundamental physical phenomenon that generates them**. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Sun as Grandfather" Concept:** > Imagine the **Sun** as the grandfather of nearly all renewable energy on Earth. His children include **wind, rain (hydro), waves, and biomass (plants)**. Only **geothermal** and **tidal** energy are like in-laws — they have different origins (Earth's core and Moon's gravity) but still live in the same family. This hierarchical view is the cornerstone of renewable energy classification. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants and metrics are used universally in quantifying renewable energy potential: - **Solar Constant (Iₛc)** = **1367 W/m²** (Total solar irradiance at the top of the atmosphere, perpendicular to sun rays at mean Earth-Sun distance) - **Air Density (ρ)** = **1.225 kg/m³** (at sea level, 15°C, 1 atm) - **Acceleration due to gravity (g)** = **9.81 m/s²** - **Density of water (ρw)** = **1000 kg/m³** - **Earth's geothermal gradient** ≈ **25–30 °C/km** of depth (average continental crust) - **Standard Calorific Value of dry biomass** ≈ **15–20 MJ/kg** - **Annual global renewable potential** ≈ **≈ 1,25,000 TW** (terawatts), against world energy consumption of ~**18 TW** - **Global installed renewable capacity (2023)** ≈ **3700 GW** (IRENA estimate) - **Cₒ₂ emission factor — grid electricity in India** ≈ **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** - **Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)** for solar PV in India ≈ **15–20%** - **Energy Payback Time (EPBT)** for solar PV ≈ **1–2 years** ## 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping | CO Tag | Course Outcome | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | |--------|---------------|--------------------------| | **CO3** | Classify various renewable energy sources and describe their advantages and limitations. | **Understand / Analyze** | | **CO4** | Apply engineering principles to estimate the potential of various RES. | **Apply / Evaluate** | > [!NOTE] > The 2024 Scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. For Module 3 questions, examiners look for: (a) correct classification logic, (b) justification of advantages using **quantitative metrics** like CUF, EPBT, and emissions avoided. --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Renewable Energy Sources Renewable energy sources can be classified along **four major axes**, which form the standard KTU answer structure: ### Axis 1: Origin of Energy (KTU-Most-Tested Axis) | Origin | Source | Energy Flow | |--------|--------|-------------| | **Solar Direct** | Solar Radiation (PV, Thermal) | Sun → Earth (electromagnetic) | | **Solar Indirect** | Wind, Hydro, Wave, Biomass, OTEC | Sun → Atmosphere/Ocean → Mechanical | | **Non-Solar** | Geothermal, Tidal | Earth's core / Moon-Sun gravity | ### Axis 2: Form of Energy Delivered 1. **Radiant (Light) Energy** → Photovoltaic conversion 2. **Thermal Energy** → Solar thermal, Geothermal 3. **Mechanical (Kinetic) Energy** → Wind, Hydro, Tidal, Wave turbines 4. **Chemical Energy** → Biofuels, Hydrogen fuel cells ### Axis 3: Availability Type - **Continuous / Baseload** → Geothermal, Biomass, Tidal (predictable) - **Intermittent / Variable** → Solar, Wind (diurnal/seasonal) - **Dispatchable** → Hydro (with storage), Biomass ### Axis 4: Conversion Technology - **Active Systems** → Require external mechanical/electrical input (e.g., PV with pumps) - **Passive Systems** → No external input (e.g., passive solar building design) - **Direct Conversion** → PV, Fuel cells - **Indirect Conversion** → Solar thermal → Steam → Turbine ## 2.2 Detailed Classification Tree **A. Solar Energy** - **A1. Solar Thermal** - Low-temperature (< 100°C): Water heating, space heating - Medium-temperature (100–300°C): Industrial process heat - High-temperature (> 300°C): Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) — Parabolic Trough, Solar Tower, Dish-Stirling - **A2. Solar Photovoltaic (PV)** - Crystalline Silicon (mono-Si, poly-Si) - Thin-Film (CdTe, CIGS, a-Si) - Third-generation (perovskite, organic, multi-junction) **B. Wind Energy** - **B1. Onshore (Land-based)** - **B2. Offshore (Fixed-bottom, Floating)** - **B3. By capacity:** Micro (<1 kW), Small (1–100 kW), Medium (100–500 kW), Large (1–10 MW) - **B4. By axis:** Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT), Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT) **C. Biomass / Bioenergy** - **C1. First-generation:** Sugarcane ethanol, biodiesel from edible oils - **C2. Second-generation:** Cellulosic ethanol, algae biodiesel - **C3. Third-generation:** Biogas, biohydrogen - **C4. By state:** Solid (wood, pellets), Liquid (ethanol, biodiesel), Gaseous (biogas, syngas) **D. Hydropower** - **D1. Large Hydel (>25 MW)** - **D2. Small Hydro (1–25 MW)** - **D3. Micro Hydro (<1 MW)** - **D4. Pumped Storage** **E. Geothermal Energy** - **E1. Hydrothermal (vapor-dominated, liquid-dominated)** - **E2. Enhanced Geothermal Systems (EGS / Hot Dry Rock)** - **E3. Geothermal Heat Pumps (GHP) — shallow** **F. Ocean Energy** - **F1. Tidal Energy** (Tidal Barrage, Tidal Stream) - **F2. Wave Energy** (Oscillating Water Column, Point Absorber, Overtopping) - **F3. Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** (Closed/Open cycle) - **F4. Salinity Gradient Energy** (Pressure Retarded Osmosis, Reverse Electrodialysis) **G. Hydrogen Energy** - **G1. Green H₂** (electrolysis using renewables) - **G2. Blue H₂** (SMR with carbon capture) - **G3. Grey/Brown H₂** (fossil-based) ## 2.3 Advantages of Renewable Energy — KTU-Ready Framework The "advantages" question in KTU is often a **5-marker** requiring structured listing. Use the **PESTEL framework** (modified to technical): ### 2.3.1 Environmental Advantages - **Near-zero greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions** during operation - **Reduces SOₓ, NOₓ, particulate matter** (improves urban AQI) - **Minimal water consumption** (solar PV, wind) vs thermal power - **Reduced acid rain and smog** - **Lower ecological footprint** (especially solar rooftops) ### 2.3.2 Economic Advantages - **Zero / negligible fuel cost** (sunlight, wind are free) - **Low Operation & Maintenance (O&M) cost** (≈ 1–2% of capital cost/year) - **Long asset life** (solar PV: 25–30 years; wind: 20–25 years) - **Decentralized generation** → reduces T&D losses - **Job creation** — India targets 3 million renewable jobs by 2030 ### 2.3.3 Social Advantages - **Rural electrification** (decentralized mini-grids) - **Energy access** to remote islands and tribal areas - **Improves health** by displacing indoor biomass burning - **Educational and skill development opportunities** ### 2.3.4 Technical Advantages - **Modular and scalable** (from 1 W to 1 GW) - **Short construction time** (solar farm: 6–12 months vs nuclear: 10+ years) - **Indigenous technology availability** - **High reliability** with proper design ### 2.3.5 Strategic Advantages - **Energy security** (reduces fossil fuel imports — India imports ~85% of crude oil) - **Reduces forex outflow** - **Diversifies energy mix** - **Resilient to geopolitical shocks** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** When asked for "advantages," always quantify at least **2 advantages** with numerical support (e.g., "A 1 MW solar plant avoids approximately **1,500 tonnes of CO₂** per year, equivalent to planting **50,000 trees**"). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Topic | Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical Units | KTU Frequency | |---|-------|---------|------------------|---------------|---------------| | 1 | **Solar Power Available** | $P_{solar} = I_{T} \times A \times \eta$ | $I_T$ = irradiance, $A$ = area, $\eta$ = efficiency | W | High | | 2 | **Wind Power (Betz Limit)** | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_{p}$ | $\rho$ = air density, $v$ = wind speed, $C_p$ = power coeff. ($\leq 16/27 \approx 0.593$) | W | High | | 3 | **Hydropower** | $P_{hydro} = \eta \rho g Q H$ | $Q$ = discharge, $H$ = head, $\eta$ = turbine efficiency | W | High | | 4 | **Biomass Energy Yield** | $E_{bio} = m \times CV \times \eta_{conv}$ | $m$ = mass, $CV$ = calorific value, $\eta$ = conversion efficiency | J | Medium | | 5 | **Tidal Power (Barrage)** | $P_{tidal} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A_{b}^{2} \times \eta$ | $A_b$ = barrage area, depends on tidal range | W | Medium | | 6 | **Wave Power (per m crest)** | $P_{wave} = 0.5 H_{s}^{2} T_{e}$ | $H_s$ = significant wave height, $T_e$ = energy period | kW/m | Low | | 7 | **OTEC Efficiency (Carnot)** | $\eta_{OTEC} = 1 - \frac{T_{cold}}{T_{warm}}$ | $T$ in Kelvin; $\Delta T \geq 20°C$ required | dimensionless | Low | | 8 | **CO₂ Avoided (annually)** | $CO_{2 avoided} = E_{gen} \times EF_{grid}$ | $EF$ = emission factor (India grid ≈ 0.82 kg/kWh) | tonnes/year | High | | 9 | **Capacity Utilization Factor** | $CUF = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760} \times 100$ | 8760 = hours/year | % | High | | 10 | **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)** | $LCOE = \frac{\sum_{t} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^{t}}}{\sum_{t} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^{t}}}$ | $C_t$ = cost at year $t$, $E_t$ = energy, $r$ = discount rate | ₹/kWh | High | | 11 | **Energy Payback Time** | $EPBT = \frac{E_{input}}{E_{annual,gen}}$ | $E_{input}$ = embodied energy | years | Medium | | 12 | **Geothermal Gradient** | $T(z) = T_{surf} + G \cdot z$ | $G \approx 25\text{–}30°C/km$ | °C | Low | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Utility-scale grid planning:** Classification helps integrate diverse RES into the national grid (e.g., India's **500 GW non-fossil target by 2030**). - **Hybrid system design:** Solar + Wind + Biomass hybrid systems use complementary availability curves. - **Policy design:** MNRE's classification determines subsidy eligibility (e.g., solar rooftops up to 10 kW get **40% subsidy** under PM-Surya Ghar Yojana). - **Carbon credit markets:** Each classified RES has unique **Verified Carbon Unit (VCU)** accounting. - **Industrial applications:** Geothermal for process heat, biomass for captive power in sugar mills, solar for desalination in Lakshadweep. --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Betz Limit for Wind Energy (Foundation of Wind Classification) The **Betz Limit** is the theoretical maximum fraction of wind kinetic energy that a turbine can extract. It defines the upper bound of $C_p$ (power coefficient) used in wind classification. ### Step 1: Define the Air Stream Tube Consider an idealized wind turbine of swept area $A$ in an air stream of density $\rho$ with upstream wind speed $v_1$, downstream speed $v_2$, and speed at the rotor disk $v_d$. ### Step 2: Apply Conservation of Mass The mass flow rate through the rotor is: $$ \dot{m} = \rho A v_d $$ ### Step 3: Apply Conservation of Momentum The force on the rotor equals the rate of change of momentum: $$ F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho A v_d (v_1 - v_2) $$ ### Step 4: Apply Conservation of Energy (Power Extracted) The power extracted equals the difference in kinetic energy fluxes: $$ P = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} (v_1^{2} - v_2^{2}) $$ Substituting $\dot{m} = \rho A v_d$: $$ P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v_d (v_1^{2} - v_2^{2}) $$ ### Step 5: Use Momentum Equation to Eliminate $v_d$ From the momentum equation: $$ v_d = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} $$ Substituting into the power equation: $$ P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A \left(\frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}\right) (v_1^{2} - v_2^{2}) $$ Factorising $v_1^2 - v_2^2 = (v_1 - v_2)(v_1 + v_2)$: $$ P = \frac{1}{4} \rho A (v_1 + v_2)^{2} (v_1 - v_2) $$ ### Step 6: Express Power Coefficient $C_p$ The power coefficient is defined as: $$ C_{p} = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^{3}} $$ Substituting $P$: $$ C_{p} = \frac{(v_1 + v_2)^{2} (v_1 - v_2)}{2 v_1^{3}} $$ ### Step 7: Introduce Induction Factor $a$ Let $v_2 = v_1 (1 - 2a)$, so $v_1 + v_2 = 2v_1(1 - a)$ and $v_1 - v_2 = 2 a v_1$. Substituting: $$ C_{p} = \frac{[2v_1(1-a)]^{2} \cdot 2 a v_1}{2 v_1^{3}} $$ Simplifying: $$ C_{p} = 4 a (1 - a)^{2} $$ ### Step 8: Maximise $C_p$ Take the derivative with respect to $a$ and set it to zero: $$ \frac{d C_p}{d a} = 4 \left[ (1 - a)^{2} + a \cdot 2(1 - a)(-1) \right] = 0 $$ $$ 4 (1 - a) \left[ (1 - a) - 2a \right] = 0 $$ $$ 4 (1 - a)(1 - 3a) = 0 $$ Since $a \neq 1$ (trivial), we get $a = 1/3$. ### Step 9: Compute Maximum $C_p$ $$ C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 $$ **Therefore, $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 59.3\%$, known as the Betz Limit.** ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Classification of RES (KTU High-Yield) | Source | Energy Density | CUF / Capacity Factor | Maturity (TRL) | CO₂ Avoided (g/kWh) | LCOE (₹/kWh, 2024 India) | Dispatchable? | |--------|---------------|----------------------|----------------|--------------------|--------------------------|----------------| | **Solar PV** | 150–300 W/m² (peak) | 15–22% | 9 (Commercial) | ~ 40–50 | 2.5 – 4.0 | No | | **Onshore Wind** | 200–500 W/m² @ 12 m/s | 25–40% | 9 | ~ 10–20 | 3.0 – 4.5 | No | | **Offshore Wind** | 400–800 W/m² | 40–55% | 8 | ~ 10–15 | 5.0 – 8.0 | No | | **Hydropower (Large)** | 0.5–5 W/m² of basin | 40–60% | 9 | ~ 5–15 | 2.0 – 4.0 | Yes | | **Biomass (Bagasse)** | 8–12 MJ/kg (CV) | 60–80% | 9 | ~ 200–400 (net) | 5.0 – 7.0 | Yes | | **Geothermal** | 50–100 mW/m² (heat) | 70–90% | 8–9 | ~ 40–50 | 4.0 – 6.0 | Yes | | **Tidal Barrage** | 5–10 W/m² (of barrage area) | 25–35% | 7 | ~ 20–30 | 8.0 – 12.0 | Yes | | **OTEC** | 230 W/m² (tropical) | 70–80% | 5–6 | ~ 30–50 | 10 – 20 | Yes | | **Green Hydrogen** | 120 MJ/kg (HHV) | n/a (storage) | 7 | ~ 20–30 (life-cycle) | 5 – 10 (LCOH₂/kg) | Yes | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — "Classification & Advantages" Type Question **Problem:** A rooftop solar PV system of area $20 \text{ m}^2$ and efficiency $18\%$ receives a daily average solar irradiance of $5 \text{ kWh/m}^2$/day. The grid emission factor is $0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$. Calculate: (a) Annual energy generated, (b) Annual $\text{CO}_2$ emissions avoided, (c) Justify the classification and advantages. ### Solution: **Step (a): Annual Energy Generation** $$ E_{annual} = I_{avg} \times A \times \eta \times 365 $$ $$ E_{annual} = 5 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day} \times 20 \text{ m}^2 \times 0.18 \times 365 \text{ days} $$ $$ E_{annual} = 5 \times 20 \times 0.18 \times 365 = 6570 \text{ kWh/year} $$ **Step (b): Annual CO₂ Avoided** $$ CO_{2, avoided} = E_{annual} \times EF_{grid} = 6570 \times 0.82 = 5387.4 \text{ kg/year} $$ $$ CO_{2, avoided} \approx 5.39 \text{ tonnes/year} $$ **Step (c): Classification & Advantages** - **Classification:** Solar PV → Direct conversion → Solar origin → Intermittent source. - **Advantages:** (i) Zero operating emissions, (ii) avoids ~5.39 t CO₂/year (equivalent to **planting 180 trees**), (iii) reduces grid dependence, (iv) modular & rooftop-suitable, (v) long asset life (~25 years) and short EPBT (~1.5 years). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mark Distribution Pattern:** For a 14-mark question, examiners allocate ~7 marks to "classification" (with diagrams/tables) and ~7 marks to "advantages" (with at least 2 quantified points). --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Renewable Energy Sources ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["RES
Renewable Energy Sources"]:::root --> A1["Origin Solar
Direct"]:::branch A0 --> A2["Origin Solar
Indirect"]:::branch A0 --> A3["Origin Non Solar
Geothermal Tidal"]:::branch A1 --> B1["Photovoltaic
PV Modules"]:::leaf A1 --> B2["Solar Thermal
Low Med High Temp"]:::leaf A1 --> B3["CSP
Trough Tower Dish"]:::leaf A2 --> B4["Wind Energy
Onshore Offshore HAWT VAWT"]:::leaf A2 --> B5["Hydropower
Large Small Micro Pumped"]:::leaf A2 --> B6["Biomass
1G 2G 3G Solid Liquid Gas"]:::leaf A2 --> B7["Wave Energy
OWC Point Absorber"]:::leaf A2 --> B8["OTEC
Closed Open Cycle"]:::leaf A3 --> B9["Geothermal
Hydrothermal EGS GHP"]:::leaf A3 --> B10["Tidal Energy
Barrage Stream"]:::leaf A3 --> B11["Hydrogen
Green Blue Grey"]:::leaf classDef root fill:#1F3A5F,stroke:#000,color:#FFF,stroke-width:2px classDef branch fill:#D4AF37,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:1.5px classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture: How Classification Drives System Design ```mermaid flowchart LR N1["Source
Identification"]:::phase --> N2["Energy Form
Classification"]:::phase N2 --> N3["Conversion
Technology"]:::phase N3 --> N4["System Sizing
and Design"]:::phase N4 --> N5["Advantages
Quantification"]:::phase N5 --> N6["Policy and
Subsidy Mapping"]:::phase N6 --> N7["Deployment
and Monitoring"]:::phase N1 -. "Origin" .- O1["Solar Wind
Geo Tidal Bio"]:::data N2 -. "Type" .- O2["Radiant Kinetic
Thermal Chemical"]:::data N3 -. "Method" .- O3["Direct Indirect
Active Passive"]:::data N5 -. "Metrics" .- O4["CUF LCOE EPBT
CO2 Avoided"]:::data N6 -. "Schemes" .- O5["PM KUSUM
SURYA GHAR"]:::data classDef phase fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF,stroke-width:1.5px classDef data fill:#FFFAF0,stroke:#555,color:#000,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Advantages Mapping to Engineering Outcomes ```mermaid flowchart TD P0["Renewable Energy
Advantages Framework"]:::root P0 --> Q1["Environmental"]:::cat P0 --> Q2["Economic"]:::cat P0 --> Q3["Social"]:::cat P0 --> Q4["Technical"]:::cat P0 --> Q5["Strategic"]:::cat Q1 --> Q1a["Zero GHG
Emissions"]:::point Q1 --> Q1b["Reduced Air
Pollutants"]:::point Q1 --> Q1c["Low Water
Footprint"]:::point Q2 --> Q2a["Zero Fuel

Cost"]:::point Q2 --> Q2c["Long Asset

Grid"]:::point classDef root fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000,color:#FFF,stroke-width:2px classDef cat fill:#DAA520,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:1.5px classDef point fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,color:#000,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Classification by Conversion Pathway ```mermaid flowchart LR R0["Primary Energy"]:::start --> R1["Radiant
Light"]:::type R0 --> R2["Mechanical
