Fuel Cells"]:::device classDef start fill:#00008B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF,stroke-width:2px classDef type fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:1.5px classDef device fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:1px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Tip:** For a KTU 14-mark question, examiners reward students who include **at least one labelled diagram** of classification. The Mermaid block diagrams above can be directly drawn in answer sheets by hand, simplified, and credited. --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **"Differentiate between active and passive solar energy systems with one example each."** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Active Solar System | Passive Solar System | |-----------|--------------------|-----------------------| | **Definition** | Uses external mechanical/electrical devices (pumps, fans) to collect, store, and distribute solar energy. | Uses architectural design and building materials (no external power) to harness solar energy. | | **Components** | Solar collector, pump, storage tank, controls. | South-facing windows, thermal mass, insulation. | | **Examples** | Solar water heater with electric pump, PV-powered ventilation. | Trombe wall, sunspace, roof overhangs. | | **Cost** | Higher initial cost. | Minimal incremental cost. | | **Efficiency** | Higher (controlled). | Lower (design-dependent). | --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **"List any four advantages of renewable energy sources over conventional fossil fuels."** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer (Any 4, prefer quantified):** 1. **Zero operating GHG emissions** — A 1 MW solar plant avoids ≈ 1,500 tonnes of CO₂/year, equivalent to **planting 50,000 trees** annually. 2. **Negligible fuel cost** — Sunlight, wind, and tides are free inputs, while coal/imported oil costs India > **$160 billion/year** in imports. 3. **Decentralized generation** — Reduces transmission & distribution losses (~20–25% in India) by enabling local generation. 4. **Modular and scalable** — Can be built in 6–12 months (solar/wind) versus 7–10 years for nuclear/thermal. 5. **Energy security** — Reduces import dependence (India imports **85% of crude oil**, **50% of natural gas**). 6. **Job creation** — India targets **3 million renewable energy jobs by 2030**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > ❌ Writing generic advantages without **numbers, units, or Indian context** → loses 3–4 marks. > ❌ Mixing classification axes (e.g., confusing "form" with "origin") → loses 2 marks. > ❌ Omitting a diagram/table for a 14-mark answer → loses at least 2 marks. > ❌ Failing to mention **CUF, LCOE, or EPBT** when asked about advantages → loses 2–3 marks. > ✅ **Best practice:** Use a **table** for classification + **quantified bullets** for advantages + **one diagram** + **one Indian example (e.g., Kamuthi Solar, Muppandal Wind Farm).** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]` **(a)** Classify the various renewable energy sources based on **(i) origin** and **(ii) availability** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss in detail the **environmental, economic, and social advantages** of renewable energy. Support your answer with **at least two quantified examples** relevant to India. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution: **(a) Classification of Renewable Energy Sources (7 marks)** **Based on Origin:** | Category | Source | Example | |----------|--------|---------| | **Direct Solar** | Solar radiation | Solar PV, Solar Thermal, CSP | | **Indirect Solar** | Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Wave, OTEC | Wind turbine, hydropower plant, bagasse cogeneration | | **Non-Solar** | Geothermal, Tidal | Puga valley (Ladakh) geothermal, Gulf of Kutch tidal | **Based on Availability:** | Type | Source | Reason | |------|--------|--------| | **Continuous / Baseload** | Geothermal, Biomass, Tidal | Earth heat, predictable tides | | **Intermittent / Variable** | Solar, Wind | Day/night, seasonal | | **Dispatchable (with storage)** | Hydro (reservoir), Biomass | Stored water, biomass stockpile | **[2 marks reserved for a neat classification diagram/flowchart]** **(b) Advantages with Quantified Examples (7 marks)** **Environmental Advantages (3 marks):** - **GHG reduction:** India's installed renewable capacity of ~190 GW (2024) displaces approximately **300 million tonnes of CO₂** annually. - **Air quality:** Replacing 1 GW of coal with solar reduces **SO₂ emissions by ~12,000 tonnes/year** and PM2.5 by ~200 tonnes/year. - **Water savings:** Solar PV uses **0.02 gallons/kWh** vs coal's 0.49 gallons/kWh — for a 1 GW plant, saves **3.7 billion litres/year**. **Economic Advantages (2 marks):** - **LCOE decline:** Solar PV LCOE in India has fallen from **₹17/kWh (2010) to ₹2.5/kWh (2024)** — a **85% reduction**. - **Fuel savings:** A 1 MW solar plant saves **₹50–70 lakhs/year** in diesel generation cost in rural areas. - **Investment:** India committed **$500 billion** to renewable infrastructure by 2030. **Social Advantages (2 marks):** - **Rural electrification:** Under **Saubhagya scheme**, ~18,000 villages electrified using solar mini-grids. - **Employment:** India targets **3 million renewable jobs by 2030** (currently ~3.4 lakh in solar, ~50,000 in wind). - **Health:** Displacing biomass cooking saves **~4 million premature deaths/year globally** from indoor air pollution. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **four-quadrant classification** of renewable energy sources (origin, form, conversion, availability) with a neat flowchart. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A proposed solar PV plant has an area of **5,000 m²** and module efficiency of **20%**. The site receives an annual average daily Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI) of **5.5 kWh/m²/day**. Calculate: **(i)** Annual energy generated, **(ii)** Equivalent CO₂ avoided annually (grid EF = 0.82 kg/kWh), **(iii)** Justify the **advantages** of deploying this plant with at least 4 points. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution: **(a) Four-Quadrant Classification (7 marks)** | Axis | Categories | Example | |------|-----------|---------| | **Origin** | Solar direct, Solar indirect, Non-solar | PV (Direct), Wind (Indirect), Geothermal (Non-solar) | | **Form** | Radiant, Kinetic, Thermal, Chemical | PV (Radiant), Wind (Kinetic), Geothermal (Thermal), H₂ (Chemical) | | **Conversion** | Direct (PV, fuel cell), Indirect (solar thermal), Active, Passive | PV = Direct+Active, Trombe wall = Passive | | **Availability** | Baseload, Variable, Dispatchable | Geothermal, Solar, Pumped hydro | **[3 marks for a flowchart, 4 marks for tabular explanation]** **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **(i) Annual Energy Generated:** $$ E_{annual} = GHI \times A \times \eta \times 365 $$ $$ E_{annual} = 5.5 \times 5000 \times 0.20 \times 365 $$ $$ E_{annual} = 5.5 \times 5000 \times 0.20 \times 365 = 2{,}007{,}500 \text{ kWh/year} $$ $$ \boxed{E_{annual} \approx 2.01 \text{ GWh/year}} $$ **(ii) CO₂ Avoided:** $$ CO_{2, avoided} = E_{annual} \times EF_{grid} = 2{,}007{,}500 \times 0.82 $$ $$ CO_{2, avoided} = 1{,}646{,}150 \text{ kg/year} = 1646 \text{ tonnes/year} $$ $$ \boxed{CO_{2, avoided} \approx 1646 \text{ tonnes/year} \quad (\text{equivalent to } 55{,}000 \text{ trees})} $$ **(iii) Justification — Advantages (4 points):** 1. **Carbon mitigation:** Avoids **1,646 tonnes of CO₂/year** — directly supports India's **Panchamrit** commitment at COP26. 2. **Energy security:** Annual generation of **2.01 GWh** powers ~**1,800 households** continuously, reducing grid draw. 3. **Economic:** With LCOE = ₹2.5/kWh, annual revenue ≈ **₹50 lakhs** (assuming PPA at ₹2.5/kWh). 4. **Low EPBT & modular:** Such a plant has **EPBT ≈ 1.5 years** and **lifecycle ≥ 25 years** — net positive energy for **>23 years**. 5. **Scalable and indigenous:** Modules are produced under **PLI scheme** (₹4,500 crore allocation), supporting Make-in-India. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔑 KTU 2024 — Rapid Revision Checklist for "Classification and Advantages":** - ✅ **Three primary classification axes (most-asked):** Origin, Form, Availability. - ✅ **Major RES (must-memorize list):** Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal, Wave, OTEC, Hydrogen. - ✅ **Solar Constant** = **1367 W/m²** — non-negotiable numerical. - ✅ **Betz Limit** = **16/27 ≈ 59.3%** — foundation of wind classification. - ✅ **Hydropower formula** = $P = \eta \rho g Q H$ — use $g = 9.81$ m/s². - ✅ **Solar formula** = $P = I \times A \times \eta$ — distinguish from $E$ (energy). - ✅ **Grid emission factor (India)** = **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** — needed for all CO₂ calculations. - ✅ **Always give Indian examples:** Kamuthi (Asia's largest solar), Muppandal (wind), Puga (geothermal), Gulf of Kutch (tidal), Sagardwip (wave). - ✅ **Quantify every advantage** with numbers — examiners reward numerical reasoning. - ✅ **LCOE order (2024, India):** Solar (₹2.5–4) < Wind (₹3–4.5) < Coal (₹4–5) < Biomass (₹5–7) < Nuclear (₹6–8). - ✅ **Advantages framework:** Use **PESTEL-modified** = Environmental, Economic, Social, Technical, Strategic. - ✅ **For 14-mark questions, mandatory inclusions:** (1) One classification table/diagram, (2) At least 2 quantified advantages, (3) One Indian context example, (4) A neat labeled conclusion. - ✅ **CUF ranges (remember):** Solar PV 15–22%, Wind 25–40%, Hydro 40–60%, Geothermal 70–90%, Biomass 60–80%. - ✅ **TRL (Technology Readiness Level) context:** Most RES are at TRL 8–9 (commercial); OTEC and wave are at TRL 5–7 (demonstration). - ✅ **Avoid these traps:** Don't confuse **dispatchable** with **baseload**; don't say "renewable = zero emissions" — manufacturing emissions exist (life-cycle assessment view). > [!NOTE] > **Final Tip for KTU Exam Day:** > Begin every classification answer with a **2-line definition**, follow with a **table/flowchart**, then **advantages with numbers**. End with a **concluding line** linking to India's **500 GW non-fossil target by 2030**. This structure typically scores **13–14/14** in KTU valuations. ---

topics.title: Energy from Ocean – Tidal Power Generation – Wave Power Generation, Bio Mass Energy – Biomass Conversion Technologies – Bio Gasification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET754 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy from Ocean – Tidal Power Generation – Wave Power Generation, Bio Mass Energy – Biomass Conversion Technologies – Bio Gasification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ocean Energy – The Macro Perspective

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Ocean Energy refers to the renewable energy harnessed from the various forms of oceanic phenomena including tidal rise and fall, wave motion, ocean thermal gradients, and salinity gradients. For this module, the focus is strictly on **Tidal Power** and **Wave Power** as mechanical-to-electrical conversion systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Highlight:**
> Ocean energy is categorized as a **diurnal/seasonal predictable** renewable source. Unlike solar/wind, tidal energy is **astronomically deterministic** (driven by Moon-Sun-Earth geometry), making it one of the most reliable renewable resources for grid baseload support.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the ocean as a giant, slow-moving battery. The Moon and Sun "wind it up" twice a day (tides), and winds "shake" it constantly (waves). Tidal Power is like installing a turbine in a dammed river estuary — you wait for the ocean to fill a basin, then let it rush out through a turbine. Wave Power is like attaching a buoy to a piston — every bobbing motion of the surface pumps fluid through a generator.

---

### 1.2 Tidal Power Generation – Formal Definition

**Definition:**
Tidal Power is the conversion of the **potential energy** stored in the differential water head created between a coastal basin and the open sea during tidal cycles, into electrical energy using low-head hydraulic turbines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants for Tidal Calculations:**
> - **Density of seawater:** $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (NOT 1000 kg/m³ — the salt makes it denser!)
> - **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - **Typical tidal range:** $2 - 10 \text{ m}$ (commercially viable above 5 m)
> - **Tidal cycle period:** $\approx 12 \text{ hours } 25 \text{ minutes}$ (semi-diurnal)

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a two-story swimming pool next to the sea with a controllable gate. When the tide rises, the sea level goes up, you close the gate, and now the pool is "lower" than the sea. When the tide falls, you open the gate — the higher sea water rushes down to fill the lower pool, spinning a turbine on the way. Reverse the process on the next tide. That is **single-effect tidal generation**.

---

### 1.3 Wave Power Generation – Formal Definition

**Definition:**
Wave Power is the conversion of the **kinetic and potential energy** present in ocean surface gravity waves into useful mechanical work (and ultimately electricity) using oscillating water columns, point absorbers, attenuators, or overtopping devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants for Wave Energy:**
> - **Density of seawater:** $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Wave power per unit crest length (deep water):** $P = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{64 \pi}$
> - **Typical wave height $H$:** $1 - 5 \text{ m}$
> - **Typical wave period $T$:** $5 - 20 \text{ s}$

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A cork floating on the ocean surface moves in a circle (or ellipse in shallow water) as a wave passes. That motion contains energy. If you can mechanically couple thousands of corks to a generator — or trap air above a column of water that bobs up and down — you can harvest that orbital energy. Wave Power is essentially "wind energy on a delay" because waves are just wind's stored mechanical energy.

---

### 1.4 Biomass Energy – Formal Definition

**Definition:**
Biomass Energy is the chemical energy stored in organic, non-fossilized biological material (crops, residues, algae, forestry waste, animal manure, municipal solid waste) that can be released via **thermochemical**, **biochemical**, or **chemical** conversion routes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomass is Carbon-Neutral (in theory):**
> The CO₂ released during combustion is reabsorbed by the next generation of plants via photosynthesis, closing the carbon cycle on a human timescale (years) versus fossil fuels (millions of years).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Biomass is essentially **bottled sunlight**. Plants used chlorophyll like solar panels, capturing photons, combining them with CO₂ and water via photosynthesis ($6CO_2 + 6H_2O \rightarrow C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$), and storing the resulting sugar's chemical bonds. When we burn that sugar (or digest it), we release the original solar energy.

---

### 1.5 Biomass Conversion Technologies – The 4 Main Routes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandated Classification:**
> The syllabus strictly requires understanding of **Thermochemical** (Combustion, Gasification, Pyrolysis), **Biochemical** (Anaerobic Digestion, Fermentation), and **Chemical** (Transesterification) routes. **Biogasification** is the dedicated focus of the final section.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of biomass as a multi-story chemical apartment building:
- **Combustion** = demolition with explosives (burn it all at once, get heat).
- **Pyrolysis** = careful disassembly in a vacuum (no oxygen, get bio-oil, char, gas).
- **Gasification** = controlled partial demolition (limited oxygen, get synthesis gas / syngas).
- **Anaerobic Digestion** = letting nature's microorganisms "renovate" it without air (get biogas).

---

### 1.6 Biogasification (Biogas Production) – Formal Definition

**Definition:**
Biogasification is the **biochemical conversion** of wet biomass (animal dung, sewage sludge, kitchen waste, energy crops) into a combustible gas mixture called **biogas** (typically 55–65% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂, traces of H₂S, N₂, water vapor) via **anaerobic digestion** in the absence of oxygen by a consortium of microorganisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Biogas Composition:**
> - **Methane (CH₄):** 55–65%
> - **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂):** 30–40%
> - **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S):** 0.1–0.5% (must be removed — corrosive!)
> - **Water Vapor:** saturated
> - **Calorific Value:** $\approx 20 - 25 \text{ MJ/m}^3$ (about half of natural gas)

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Biogasification is like a cow's four-stomach digestive system, but enclosed in a sealed steel tank. Bacteria break down complex carbohydrates/proteins/fats in four sequential stages (Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis), producing methane as the final "exhaust." A cow does this naturally; a **biogas plant** just does it in a controlled, gas-collecting vessel called a **digester**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Tidal Power Generation – Operational Principle

#### 2.1.1 The Four Operating Modes

Tidal barrage systems can operate in **four distinct modes**:

| Mode | Operation | Energy Captured |
|------|-----------|-----------------|
| **1. Ebb Generation** | Basin fills at high tide through sluice gates; at low tide, water flows out **through the turbine** back to sea | Outflow (Falling tide) |
| **2. Flood Generation** | Basin empty at low tide; water flows **in through turbine** during rising tide | Inflow (Rising tide) |
| **3. Two-way (Dual) Generation** | Turbines operate during **both** flood and ebb | Both directions |
| **4. Pumping Mode** | Excess grid electricity used to **pump water** into basin at high tide, increasing head for later generation | Augmented head |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:**
> The **ebb generation** mode is most common because the basin stays full longer at high tide, providing a longer generation window and more predictable output.

#### 2.1.2 Tidal Power System Components

- **Barrage:** A dam-like structure across an estuary with sluice gates, ship locks, and turbine housings.
- **Sluice Gates:** Large gates for gravity filling/emptying of the basin without power generation.
- **Turbines:** **Low-head** Kaplan or bulb turbines (head typically 2–10 m, flow rates massive).
- **Embankments:** Side walls connecting barrage to shore.

#### 2.1.3 Site Selection Criteria (KTU Board Exam Favorite)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget **geographical constraints**. Tidal plants need narrow estuaries with high tidal range — only a few sites worldwide qualify (La Rance, France; Sihwa Lake, South Korea; Annapolis Royal, Canada; proposed Kutch, India).

**Essential criteria:**
1. **High tidal range** (> 5 m for economic viability)
2. **Narrow bay or estuary** (minimize barrage length & cost)
3. **Adequate basin area** (large storage volume)
4. **Strong foundation** (rock for barrage structure)
5. **Minimal environmental/ecological disruption**
6. **Proximity to load center / grid**

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet – Tidal Energy

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{available} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$ | Available tidal power per cycle | W |
| 2 | $E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2 \cdot \frac{T}{2}$ | Energy per half tidal cycle | J |
| 3 | $P_{avg} = \frac{\rho g A R^2}{2T}$ | Average power over one full cycle | W |
| 4 | $P = \rho g Q H_{net}$ | Hydraulic power (turbine input) | W |
| 5 | $H_{net} = H_{gross} - h_{f}$ | Net head after friction losses | m |
| 6 | $E_{annual} = 0.55 \times P_{avg} \times 8760$ | Realistic annual energy (45% downtime for ebb-only) | Wh |
| 7 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{turbine} \cdot \eta_{generator} \cdot \eta_{transformer}$ | System efficiency | – |

Where:
- $A$ = Basin area (m²)
- $R$ = Tidal range (m)
- $T$ = Tidal period ($\approx 12.42$ hours = 44,712 s)
- $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (seawater)
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

---

### 2.3 Wave Power Generation – Operating Principles

#### 2.3.1 Deep Water vs Shallow Water Waves

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:**
> In **deep water** (depth $d > \lambda/2$), water particles move in **circular orbits** — energy is trapped near the surface.
> In **shallow water** ($d < \lambda/20$), orbits become **elliptical** and bottom friction eventually breaks the wave.

**Real-world utility:** Wave energy devices are typically deployed in **transitional waters** ($d \sim \lambda/4$) where there's enough depth for survivability but wave bottom effects begin influencing wave shape — allowing easier mechanical coupling.

#### 2.3.2 Wave Energy Conversion Devices – Classification

| Class | Description | Example |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| **Point Absorber** | Small buoy oscillating relative to a fixed reference; heave motion | PowerBuoy (USA) |
| **Attenuator** | Long multi-segment floating body aligned with wave direction | Pelamis (UK) |
| **Terminator** | Device oriented perpendicular to wave direction, intercepts wave crest | Oscillating Water Column (OWC) |
| **Overtopping** | Waves spill into a raised reservoir; water released through low-head turbines | Wave Dragon (Denmark) |
| **Oscillating Water Column (OWC)** | Trapped air column above water column oscillates, driving Wells turbine | LIMPET (Islay, Scotland) |

#### 2.3.3 Wave Power Available per Unit Crest Width

For a regular sinusoidal wave in deep water, the average power transmitted per unit length of wave crest is:

$$P = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{64 \pi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:**
> Wave power scales with **H²** (wave height squared). A 4 m wave delivers **16 times** the power of a 1 m wave! This is why storm-wave survivability is the engineering bottleneck.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet – Wave Energy

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{64 \pi}$ | Deep water wave power per unit crest length | W/m |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{g T^2}{2 \pi}$ | Deep water wavelength | m |
| 3 | $c = \dfrac{g T}{2 \pi}$ | Deep water wave celerity (phase speed) | m/s |
| 4 | $c_g = \dfrac{c}{2}$ | Deep water group velocity (energy transport speed) | m/s |
| 5 | $E_{linear} = \dfrac{1}{8} \rho g H^2$ | Energy density per unit sea surface area | J/m² |
| 6 | $P = E_{linear} \cdot c_g$ | Power = Energy density × Group velocity | W/m |
| 7 | $E_{annual} = 0.20 \text{ to } 0.30 \times P_{rated} \times 8760$ | Capacity factor for wave devices (15–30%) | Wh |
| 8 | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{g \lambda}{2 \pi} \tanh\left(\dfrac{2 \pi d}{\lambda}\right)}$ | General wave celerity (dispersion relation) | m/s |

---

### 2.5 Biomass Conversion Technologies – Deep Dive

#### 2.5.1 The Three Conversion Families

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** Students must be able to **draw and label** the process flow diagram for each conversion route, identify inputs, outputs, operating conditions, and typical efficiencies.

**Family A: Thermochemical Conversion**

| Process | Temp (°C) | O₂ supply | Main Products | Key Use |
|---------|-----------|-----------|---------------|---------|
| **Combustion** | 800–1200 | Excess (Stoichiometric) | Heat, CO₂, H₂O, ash | Steam generation, CHP |
| **Gasification** | 700–900 | Limited (sub-stoichiometric) | Syngas (CO + H₂), CH₄, tars | Power, FT diesel |
| **Pyrolysis** | 400–800 | **Zero** (inert atmosphere) | Bio-oil, biochar, pyrolysis gas | Liquid fuel, soil amendment |
| **Torrefaction** | 200–300 | Zero (inert) | Torrefied biomass (solid fuel) | Coal replacement |

**Family B: Biochemical Conversion**

| Process | Conditions | Main Products | Key Use |
|---------|------------|---------------|---------|
| **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** | 30–55 °C, no O₂, bacteria | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂), digestate | Electricity, cooking, fertilizer |
| **Fermentation** | 30 °C, yeast | Ethanol | Transport fuel, beverages |
| **Composting** | Aerobic, mesophilic | Humus, CO₂ | Soil conditioner |

**Family C: Chemical Conversion**

| Process | Reagent | Product | Use |
|---------|---------|---------|-----|
| **Transesterification** | Methanol + NaOH/KOH catalyst | Biodiesel (FAME) + Glycerol | Diesel substitute |
| **Hydrolysis** | Acid/Enzyme | Sugars | Fermentation feedstock |

---

### 2.6 Biogasification – Detailed Process Biochemistry

#### 2.6.1 The Four Sequential Microbial Stages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:**
> The four stages MUST be memorized with their inputs/outputs and the responsible microorganism class. This is a frequent 7-mark question.

| Stage | Microorganism | Substrate | Product | Equation (Simplified) |
|-------|---------------|-----------|---------|------------------------|
| **1. Hydrolysis** | Facultative bacteria | Complex polymers (carbs, proteins, fats) | Monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) | Polymer + H₂O → Monomers |
| **2. Acidogenesis** | Acidogenic bacteria | Monomers | Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs), H₂, CO₂ | C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 CH₃CH₂OH + 2 CO₂ |
| **3. Acetogenesis** | Acetogenic bacteria | VFAs, alcohols | Acetate, H₂, CO₂ | CH₃CH₂OH + H₂O → CH₃COOH + 2 H₂ |
| **4. Methanogenesis** | Methanogenic archaea | Acetate, H₂/CO₂ | Methane, CO₂ | CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂ (Acetoclastic) |

#### 2.6.2 Digester Types (KTU Classification)

| Type | Construction | Suitability |
|------|--------------|-------------|
| **Fixed Dome (Chinese)** | Underground masonry, no moving parts | Rural India, low cost |
| **Floating Drum (Indian/GoBar)** | Steel gas holder floats on slurry | Small farms, visible gas |
| **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** | Mechanically mixed, heated | Industrial scale |
| **Plug Flow Reactor** | Long horizontal tank, no mixing | High solids feedstock |
| **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB)** | Granular sludge bed, upward flow | Wastewater treatment |

#### 2.6.3 Operational Parameters (Critical for KTU Numericals)

- **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** $HRT = V/Q$ where V = digester volume, Q = daily feed volume
- **Volumetric Organic Loading Rate (OLR):** $OLR = \dot{m}_{VS}/V$ (kg VS/m³·day)
- **Temperature:** Mesophilic (30–40 °C) vs Thermophilic (50–55 °C)
- **pH:** Optimal 6.8–7.4 (Methanogens are very pH-sensitive!)
- **C/N ratio:** Optimal 20:1 to 30:1

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet – Biomass & Biogasification

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $LHV = 33.83 - 0.046 W$ | Lower Heating Value (Wood, dry basis approximation) | MJ/kg |
| 2 | $E_{biomass} = m \times LHV$ | Energy content of biomass | MJ |
| 3 | $\eta_{combustion} = \dfrac{T_{flame} - T_{flue}}{T_{flame} - T_{ambient}}$ | Combustion efficiency (Sankey) | – |
| 4 | $CH_4 \text{ yield} = 0.35 \times \text{VS}_{loaded}$ | Empirical methane yield (typical) | m³ CH₄/kg VS |
| 5 | $V_{biogas} = 0.4 \times \text{VS}_{loaded} \times \eta_{digestion}$ | Biogas yield | m³ |
| 6 | $HRT = \dfrac{V_{digester}}{Q_{daily}}$ | Hydraulic Retention Time | days |
| 7 | $OLR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{VS, daily}}{V_{digester}}$ | Organic Loading Rate | kg VS/m³·day |
| 8 | $E_{biogas} = V_{biogas} \times CV_{biogas}$ | Energy in biogas | MJ |
| 9 | $P_{elec} = \dfrac{V_{biogas} \times CV_{biogas} \times \eta_{engine}}{3600}$ | Electrical output power | kW |
| 10 | $C/N_{optimal} = 20\text{ to }30$ | Required for microbial balance | – |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Available Tidal Power from a Basin

**Problem Statement:**
Derive the expression for the average power available from a tidal barrage of basin area $A$ with tidal range $R$, assuming ebb-mode operation.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1: Set up the potential energy of the impounded water.**

When the basin is full (high tide), the water surface inside the basin is at the same level as the sea. As the sea level drops by a tidal range $R$, water flows out through the turbines, doing work. The work done equals the **change in potential energy** between two states: full basin and empty basin.

Consider the basin as a vertical column of cross-sectional area $A$. At any instant during ebb, the head difference between the basin and sea is $h$. Initially $h = 0$, finally $h = R$.

**Step 2: Express the instantaneous water volume in the basin.**

At intermediate state, basin water depth above sea level = $R - h$. But more rigorously, let us define the head $h$ as the difference between **basin level** and **sea level** during outflow.

$$h = \text{Basin level} - \text{Sea level}$$

At $t = 0$ (high tide): $h = 0$
At $t = T/2$ (low tide): $h = R$ (after full discharge)

**Step 3: Compute the work done by gravity during infinitesimal outflow.**

Consider an infinitesimal slice of water of thickness $dh$ discharged through the turbine. Its mass is:

$$dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot (-dh)$$

(Negative because $h$ is increasing as basin empties relative to sea, but water is leaving.)

The potential energy released when this slice falls through the head $h$ (from basin level to sea level):

$$dW = dm \cdot g \cdot h = -\rho g A h \, dh$$

**Step 4: Integrate over the full discharge cycle.**

The total energy captured per half-cycle (high tide → low tide):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{0}^{R} \rho g A h \, dh \quad \text{(taking absolute value)} \\
W &= \rho g A \int_{0}^{R} h \, dh \\
W &= \rho g A \left[\frac{h^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{R} \\
W &= \frac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the KTU textbook result:** $E_{per cycle} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho g A R^2$ Joules per half-cycle.

**Step 5: Compute average power over the full tidal cycle.**

The complete tidal period is $T = 12.42$ hours $= 44,712$ seconds. Energy is only captured during the **ebb half-cycle** (in single-effect mode), so useful energy per period is:

$$E_{useful} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$$

Average power over the full period:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= \frac{E_{useful}}{T_{period}} \\
P_{avg} &= \frac{\rho g A R^2}{2 T} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute numerical values for KTU standard problem.**

For a basin area $A = 10 \text{ km}^2 = 10^7 \text{ m}^2$, tidal range $R = 8 \text{ m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 10^7 \times 8^2}{2 \times 44712} \\
P_{avg} &= \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 10^7 \times 64}{89424} \\
P_{avg} &= \frac{6.4357 \times 10^{12}}{89424} \\
P_{avg} &\approx 7.20 \times 10^{7} \text{ W} \\
P_{avg} &\approx 72 \text{ MW (mechanical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reality Check:** With overall plant efficiency $\approx 30\%$, the **electrical** output is $72 \times 0.30 \approx 21.6 \text{ MW}$. This is the typical magnitude for commercial tidal plants like La Rance (240 MW installed, France).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Wave Power per Unit Crest Width

**Step 1: Define a regular (monochromatic) sinusoidal wave.**

The free surface elevation is:

$$\eta(x, t) = \frac{H}{2} \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

where:
- $H$ = wave height (trough-to-crest)
- $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ = wave number
- $\omega = 2\pi/T$ = angular frequency

**Step 2: Compute total energy per unit sea surface area.**

The total wave energy density (kinetic + potential, time-averaged) for a linear Airy wave is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{density} &= \frac{1}{8} \rho g H^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation of this:**
- Potential energy per unit area = $\frac{1}{2} \rho g \overline{\eta^2}$ where $\overline{\eta^2} = H^2/8$
- So $E_p = \frac{1}{16} \rho g H^2$
- By equipartition (linear wave theory), $E_k = E_p$
- $E_{total} = E_p + E_k = \frac{1}{8} \rho g H^2$ ✓

**Step 3: Compute energy transport velocity (group velocity).**

Energy does not travel at the phase velocity $c$; it travels at the **group velocity**:

$$c_g = \frac{c}{2} \quad \text{(deep water)}$$

This is a fundamental result of wave dispersion — the envelope of a wave packet moves at $c/2$ while individual crests move at $c$.

**Step 4: Multiply energy density by group velocity to get power per unit crest length.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= E_{density} \times c_g \\
P &= \frac{1}{8} \rho g H^2 \times \frac{c}{2} \\
P &= \frac{1}{16} \rho g H^2 \cdot c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute deep water phase velocity.**

Deep water: $c = \dfrac{gT}{2\pi}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{1}{16} \rho g H^2 \cdot \frac{g T}{2 \pi} \\
P &= \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32 \pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Verify with KTU textbook formulation.**

Some texts use $\dfrac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{64 \pi}$ — this represents the **time-averaged** power where $H$ is defined as twice the amplitude (i.e., trough-to-crest, as KTU convention). With $H$ being the full wave height:

$$P = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{64 \pi}$$

Both expressions are equivalent depending on whether $H$ is the full height or the amplitude — the KTU board expects the **$64\pi$ form** with $H$ as full wave height.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example – Biogas Plant Sizing

**Problem (KTU 2024 Expected Pattern):**
A village has 500 cattle producing 12 kg of dung per animal per day. The dung has 18% total solids (TS) and 80% of TS is volatile solids (VS). The digester operates at mesophilic conditions with a 40-day HRT. Methane yield is 0.25 m³ CH₄ per kg VS loaded. Calculate:
(a) Daily VS load
(b) Required digester volume
(c) Daily methane production
(d) Daily electrical energy if generator efficiency is 30% (CV of CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³)

**Step (a): Daily VS load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total daily dung} &= 500 \times 12 = 6000 \text{ kg/day} \\
\text{Total Solids (TS)} &= 6000 \times 0.18 = 1080 \text{ kg/day} \\
\text{Volatile Solids (VS)} &= 1080 \times 0.80 = 864 \text{ kg VS/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b): Required digester volume**

Using HRT = V/Q where Q is daily feed (assuming density ~ 1000 kg/m³, so 6000 kg ≈ 6 m³/day):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= HRT \times Q \\
V &= 40 \text{ days} \times 6 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \\
V &= 240 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practice:** Use 80% of this as actual digester volume; the rest is gas holder.

**Step (c): Daily methane production**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CH_4} &= \text{VS}_{daily} \times \text{Yield} \\
V_{CH_4} &= 864 \times 0.25 \\
V_{CH_4} &= 216 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (d): Daily electrical energy**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{thermal} &= V_{CH_4} \times CV_{CH_4} \\
E_{thermal} &= 216 \text{ m}^3 \times 35.8 \text{ MJ/m}^3 \\
E_{thermal} &= 7732.8 \text{ MJ/day} \\
\\
E_{electrical} &= E_{thermal} \times \eta_{gen} \\
E_{electrical} &= 7732.8 \times 0.30 \\
E_{electrical} &= 2319.84 \text{ MJ/day} \\
\\
E_{electrical, kWh} &= \frac{2319.84 \times 10^3}{3600} \\
E_{electrical, kWh} &\approx 644.4 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This 644 kWh/day is sufficient for approximately 20–25 rural households** (assuming 1 kW peak, 8 hours/day = 8 kWh/day per household, with diversity factor). This is exactly the scale of Indian Jawaharlal Nehru National Solar Mission village biogas projects — a KTU-board favorite real-world example.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Ready)

| Parameter | Tidal | Wave | Biomass (Combustion) | Biogasification |
|-----------|-------|------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Source type** | Gravitational (predictable) | Meteorological (semi-predictable) | Stored solar | Stored solar |
| **Energy form** | Potential (head) | Kinetic + Potential | Chemical | Chemical |
| **Conversion device** | Kaplan/bulb turbine | OWC, point absorber | Boiler + steam turbine | Anaerobic digester + IC engine |
| **Capacity factor** | 25–35% | 15–30% | 60–90% | 70–90% (dispatchable) |
| **Storage requirement** | Implicit (basin) | None | Required (feedstock pile) | Implicit (biogas holder) |
| **Geographic limit** | Coastal estuaries | Coastline with fetch | Land-agnostic | Land-agnostic |
| **Major drawback** | High civil cost, marine impact | Survivability in storms | Air emissions, land use | Slow (HRT 30–60 d) |
| **LCOE (USD/MWh)** | 100–250 | 150–500 | 60–150 | 60–150 |
| **CO₂ emissions** | Near zero | Near zero | Neutral | Negative (offsets methane from waste) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the major advantages and limitations of tidal power generation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. **Predictable and inexhaustible** — Tides are governed by celestial mechanics, not weather.
2. **Non-polluting** — No fuel combustion, no greenhouse gases during operation.
3. **Long lifespan** — Barrage life is 75–100 years vs. 25 years for thermal plant.
4. **High energy density** — Single barrage can produce hundreds of MW.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
1. **High capital cost** — Barrage construction in marine environment is expensive.
2. **Limited sites** — Only a handful of estuaries worldwide have the right combination of range and basin shape.
3. **Intermittent** — Power available only during certain tidal phases (~40% of the time).
4. **Environmental impact** — Affects marine ecosystem, fish migration, sedimentation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*State the role of methanogenic bacteria in biogas production. Why is the C/N ratio important?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of methanogens (2 marks):**
Methanogenic archaea are strictly anaerobic microorganisms that perform the **final stage (methanogenesis)** of anaerobic digestion. They convert acetate ($CH_3COOH$) and hydrogen-carbon dioxide ($H_2 + CO_2$) into methane via two pathways:
- **Acetoclastic:** $CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$ (~70% of methane)
- **Hydrogenotrophic:** $CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$ (~30% of methane)

They are extremely sensitive to oxygen, pH, and temperature changes.

**Importance of C/N ratio (1 mark):**
Optimal C/N ratio is **20:1 to 30:1**. If C/N is too high, nitrogen is rapidly depleted, slowing microbial growth. If C/N is too low, excess nitrogen releases ammonia, raising pH and inhibiting methanogens.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> When answering 14-mark questions in this module:
> - **Forgetting constants:** Always write $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (seawater) explicitly — using 1000 is a 0.5-mark loss.
> - **Showing no derivation:** Board examiners **demand** at least 4–5 derivation steps; jumping to final formula = 2 marks lost.
> - **Skipping diagrams:** A neat labeled diagram of digester/barrage/OWC = guaranteed 2 marks; missing it = guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> - **Wrong units:** Power in kW vs MW, energy in Wh vs kWh — common confusion.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the working of a tidal power plant operating in ebb generation mode. List the factors affecting site selection. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Ebb Generation Mode — Working (5 marks):**

In **ebb generation mode**, the tidal barrage operates as follows:

1. **High Tide:** As the sea tide rises, water enters the **basin** through **sluice gates** (low-head loss passages). The turbines are NOT used during filling. The basin water level rises to match the peak sea level.
2. **Slack Period:** At high tide, there is no head difference — sluice gates close, trapping water in the basin.
3. **Falling Tide:** The sea level begins to drop. A head difference $H$ develops between the basin (high) and sea (low).
4. **Generation:** When the head reaches a minimum threshold (~1–2 m), the **turbines** are opened. Water flows from the basin back to the sea, driving the **Kaplan/bulb turbines**.
5. **Low Tide:** Generation continues until the basin level approaches sea level (small head). Then sluice gates may be used to fully drain.
6. **Cycle Repeats:** The next high tide re-fills the basin through sluices.

**(Sketch – to be drawn on answer sheet):**
```
   High Tide           Low Tide
   =======Sea          ===Sea
   |   Basin    |     |   Basin    |
   |=========== |     |            |
   |  (Filled)  |     |  (Empty)   |
   |    Water   |     |            |
   ============|     =============|
        ^                v
   Sluice gates      Turbines
   OPEN (filling)    OPEN (generating)
```

**(ii) Site Selection Factors (2 marks):**

1. **Tidal range > 5 m** (commercial viability threshold)
2. **Narrow, shallow estuary** (minimizes barrage cost)
3. **Adequate basin area** (provides storage volume)
4. **Strong rock foundation** (supports barrage civil works)
5. **Minimal ecological/environmental disruption**
6. **Accessible to load centers and grid infrastructure**

---

**(b) A tidal barrage has a basin area of 20 km² and operates with an average tidal range of 7 m. Calculate the average power output and annual energy generation. Assume single-effect (ebb) operation with overall plant efficiency of 30%. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Basin area $A = 20 \text{ km}^2 = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$
- Tidal range $R = 7 \text{ m}$
- Density of seawater $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Tidal period $T = 12.42 \text{ hr} = 44,712 \text{ s}$
- Overall efficiency $\eta = 0.30$

**Step 1: Energy per cycle (single-effect)**

$$E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cycle} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times (20 \times 10^6) \times 7^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 20 \times 10^6 \times 49 \\
&= 4.926 \times 10^{12} \text{ J per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Average mechanical power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{mech} &= \frac{E_{cycle}}{T} \\
&= \frac{4.926 \times 10^{12}}{44712} \\
&= 1.10 \times 10^{8} \text{ W} \\
&\approx 110 \text{ MW (mechanical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Average electrical power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{elec} &= \eta \times P_{mech} \\
&= 0.30 \times 110 \\
&= 33 \text{ MW (electrical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Annual energy generation**

> [!NOTE]
> Account for downtime. In single-effect ebb mode, generation is possible for only ~45% of the time. Use the conservative factor of 0.55 in the formula as per Section 2.7.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{annual} &= P_{elec} \times 8760 \times 0.55 \\
&= 33 \times 8760 \times 0.55 \\
&= 158,994 \text{ MWh/year} \\
&\approx 159 \text{ GWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_{avg,elec} = 33 \text{ MW}$, $E_{annual} \approx 159 \text{ GWh/year}$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**(a) Explain the working of an Oscillating Water Column (OWC) wave energy converter with a labeled diagram. State its advantages. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (5 marks):**

An **Oscillating Water Column (OWC)** is a partially submerged hollow structure open to the sea below. It consists of:

1. **Water Column:** The sea water enters the column from below through a submerged opening.
2. **Air Chamber:** Above the water column inside the structure, a trapped air column exists.
3. **Wells Turbine:** Connected to the air chamber via a duct; a self-rectifying turbine that rotates in the same direction irrespective of airflow direction.
4. **Generator:** Coupled to the Wells turbine via a shaft.

**Operating Cycle:**
- **Wave crest arrives:** Water inside column rises → compresses air above → air flows **outward** through turbine → turbine spins.
- **Wave trough arrives:** Water inside column falls → air expands → air flows **inward** through same turbine → turbine continues spinning **same direction** (this is the genius of the Wells turbine's symmetrical airfoil design).
- The reciprocating bidirectional air flow is thus **rectified** into unidirectional rotation.
- The generator continuously produces AC power.

**Diagram (to be drawn on answer sheet):**
```
              ↑ Air out
          ┌───┴───┐
   Wave → │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│ ← Wells Turbine → Generator
   →→→→→ │▓ Air ▓│
   →→→→→ │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│
          │ Water│
          │ ░░░░ │
          └──┬───┘
             ↓
           Sea bed
```

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **No moving parts in seawater** — only the air column moves; turbine is in dry air → minimal corrosion, long life.
2. **Survivability** — Structure absorbs wave energy; can be built into coastal cliffs.
3. **Bidirectional rectification** — Wells turbine handles both directions without valves.
4. **Modular scalability** — Multiple chambers can be added.

---

**(b) Explain the four biochemical stages of biogasification. A cattle farm has 200 animals producing 10 kg dung/animal/day. The VS content is 20% of wet dung. Methane yield = 0.30 m³/kg VS. Calorific value of methane = 35.8 MJ/m³. Calculate the daily electrical energy if the generator efficiency is 28%. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Four Stages of Biogasification (3 marks):**

| Stage | Substrate | Product | Microorganism |
|-------|-----------|---------|----------------|
| **Hydrolysis** | Carbohydrates, proteins, fats | Sugars, amino acids, fatty acids | Hydrolytic bacteria |
| **Acidogenesis** | Monomers | Volatile fatty acids (VFAs), H₂, CO₂ | Acidogenic bacteria |
| **Acetogenesis** | VFAs, alcohols | Acetate, H₂, CO₂ | Acetogenic bacteria |
| **Methanogenesis** | Acetate, H₂/CO₂ | CH₄, CO₂ (biogas) | Methanogenic archaea |

**Key equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hydrolysis: } (C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + nH_2O &\rightarrow nC_6H_{12}O_6 \\
\text{Acidogenesis: } C_6H_{12}O_6 &\rightarrow 2CH_3CH_2OH + 2CO_2 \\
\text{Acetogenesis: } CH_3CH_2OH + H_2O &\rightarrow CH_3COOH + 2H_2 \\
\text{Methanogenesis: } CH_3COOH &\rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Daily Electrical Energy Calculation (4 marks):**

**Given:**
- Number of animals: $N = 200$
- Dung per animal per day: $m = 10 \text{ kg/day}$
- VS fraction: $f_{VS} = 0.20$
- Methane yield: $Y = 0.30 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg VS}$
- CV of methane: $CV = 35.8 \text{ MJ/m}^3$
- Generator efficiency: $\eta = 0.28$

**Step 1: Daily volatile solids**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total dung} &= N \times m = 200 \times 10 = 2000 \text{ kg/day} \\
VS_{daily} &= 2000 \times 0.20 = 400 \text{ kg VS/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Daily methane volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CH_4} &= VS_{daily} \times Y \\
&= 400 \times 0.30 \\
&= 120 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Daily thermal energy**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{thermal} &= V_{CH_4} \times CV \\
&= 120 \times 35.8 \\
&= 4296 \text{ MJ/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Daily electrical energy**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{electrical} &= E_{thermal} \times \eta \\
&= 4296 \times 0.28 \\
&= 1202.88 \text{ MJ/day} \\
\\
E_{kWh} &= \frac{1202.88 \times 10^3}{3600} \\
&\approx 334.13 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** **Daily electrical energy ≈ 334 kWh/day**

This energy can supply ~40 rural households with basic lighting and small appliances — a typical KVIC/NBAIM biogas plant benchmark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Prep**

#### **🔷 Tidal Power**
- ⭐ **Formula:** $E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2}\rho g A R^2$; $\rho_{seawater} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- ⭐ **Seawater density ≠ freshwater density** — always 1025, not 1000.
- ⭐ **Period:** $T = 12 \text{ hr } 25 \text{ min} = 44,712$ s
- ⭐ **Four modes:** Ebb, Flood, Two-way, Pumping
- ⭐ **Ebb mode = most common**, gives ~45% capacity factor in single-effect
- ⭐ **Turbine type:** Kaplan or Bulb (low-head, high-flow)
- ⭐ **Site criteria:** Range > 5 m, narrow estuary, large basin area
- ⭐ **Examples:** La Rance (240 MW, France), Sihwa (254 MW, S. Korea), Annapolis (20 MW, Canada)
- ⭐ **Indian site:** Gulf of Kutch / Gulf of Cambay (proposed)

#### **🔷 Wave Power**
- ⭐ **Formula (KTU form):** $P = \dfrac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{64 \pi}$ per unit crest width
- ⭐ **Energy density:** $E = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2$ (J/m²)
- ⭐ **Group velocity (deep water):** $c_g = c/2$
- ⭐ **Wavelength (deep):** $\lambda = gT^2/(2\pi)$
- ⭐ **Devices:** OWC, Point Absorber, Attenuator, Overtopping, Terminator
- ⭐ **Wells turbine** — self-rectifying, key component of OWC
- ⭐ **Capacity factor:** 15–30% (low due to wave variability)
- ⭐ **Survivability in storms** — biggest engineering challenge

#### **🔷 Biomass & Conversion Technologies**
- ⭐ **Biomass = bottled sunlight** — solar energy stored via photosynthesis
- ⭐ **3 Conversion families:** Thermochemical / Biochemical / Chemical
- ⭐ **Combustion:** Excess O₂, 800–1200 °C → heat + ash
- ⭐ **Gasification:** Limited O₂, 700–900 °C → syngas (CO + H₂)
- ⭐ **Pyrolysis:** Zero O₂, 400–800 °C → bio-oil + char + gas
- ⭐ **Fast pyrolysis** maximizes bio-oil yield (~75% by weight)
- ⭐ **LHV formula:** $LHV \approx 33.83 - 0.046W$ (where W = moisture %)

#### **🔷 Biogasification (Anaerobic Digestion)**
- ⭐ **4 Stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis
- ⭐ **Biogas composition:** 55–65% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂, traces H₂S, H₂O
- ⭐ **Calorific value:** ~20–25 MJ/m³ (vs 35.8 MJ/m³ for pure CH₄)
- ⭐ **HRT formula:** $HRT = V/Q$ (typical 30–60 days for mesophilic)
- ⭐ **OLR formula:** $OLR = \dot{m}_{VS}/V$
- ⭐ **C/N ratio optimal:** 20–30 : 1
- ⭐ **pH optimal:** 6.8–7.4 (methanogens sensitive!)
- ⭐ **Temperature:** Mesophilic (30–40 °C) preferred for stability
- ⭐ **Digesters:** Fixed dome (KVIC), Floating drum (Gobar gas), CSTR, UASB
- ⭐ **H₂S removal** mandatory — iron oxide scrubbers (corrosion in engine!)
- ⭐ **Methanogens are archaea**, not bacteria — strict anaerobes
- ⭐ **By-product digestate** = excellent organic fertilizer (NPK + micronutrients)
- ⭐ **Equation to remember:** $CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$ (acetoclastic methanogenesis, ~70% of CH₄)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio Ethanol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET754 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bio Ethanol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition
> **Bioethanol** is a biofuel produced from the fermentation of sugars, starches, or lignocellulosic biomass, with the chemical formula **C₂H₅OH** (ethyl alcohol) and a molecular weight of **46.07 g/mol**. It is a colourless, volatile, and flammable liquid that serves as a renewable alternative to petroleum-based gasoline, either as a direct substitute (E85, E100) or as a blending component (E5, E10, E20) in spark-ignition (SI) engines.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, bioethanol is classified under **first-generation biofuels** when derived from sugar/starch crops, **second-generation** from lignocellulosic biomass, and **third-generation** from algal feedstocks. It is governed by Indian Standard specifications under **IS 15464:2004 (Anhydrous Ethanol for Automotive Use)** and **IS 1460:2005** when blended with gasoline.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your kitchen sugar jar. If you add the right yeast to a sugar solution and seal it in a warm place, bubbles (CO₂) will appear, and after a few days, you'll have a mildly alcoholic liquid. **That is exactly what industrial bioethanol production does — just on a massive scale.** Microorganisms (yeast, *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) "eat" the sugar and "excrete" ethanol as a metabolic by-product, because ethanol is essentially the yeast's waste alcohol — and crucially, it is also a clean-burning liquid fuel for our cars.

> [!NOTE] Key Intuitive Concept
> Think of bioethanol as **"stored sunlight in liquid form"** — the sugarcane/corn absorbed solar energy via photosynthesis, converting CO₂ + H₂O into sugars, which are then biologically converted into a transportable, combustible fuel.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Thermochemical Properties

| Property | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Chemical Formula | **C₂H₅OH** | Identical to fossil ethanol — only feedstock differs |
| Molecular Weight | **46.07 g/mol** | Lower than gasoline (~110 g/mol) |
| Density | **0.789 kg/L** (at 20°C) | Lighter than water |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **26.8 MJ/kg** | ~33 % lower than gasoline (44 MJ/kg) |
| Research Octane Number (RON) | **108–110** | Higher than gasoline (~92) — enables higher CR |
| Stoichiometric AFR | **9.0 : 1** | Leaner than gasoline (14.7 : 1) |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization | **842 kJ/kg** | High → excellent charge cooling |
| Auto-ignition Temperature | **363 °C** | High → safe handling |
| Flash Point | **12.8 °C** | Low → fire hazard in storage |
| Water Solubility | **Miscible in all proportions** | Causes phase separation in blends |

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Highlight — Why These Numbers Matter in Exams
> The **High Octane Rating (108–110)** allows engines to operate at higher compression ratios, while the **High Latent Heat of Vaporization (842 kJ/kg)** enables superior volumetric efficiency through internal charge cooling. These are the two single most-favoured exam points under the "advantages" question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generations of Bioethanol

**First Generation (1G) Bioethanol**
- Feedstocks: Sugarcane, sugar beet, corn, sorghum, molasses
- Process: Direct fermentation of sugars or saccharified starches
- Status: **Commercially deployed** globally; Brazil (sugarcane), USA (corn)
- Limitation: **Food vs. Fuel** conflict

**Second Generation (2G) Bioethanol**
- Feedstocks: Agricultural residues (rice/wheat straw, bagasse), forestry waste, dedicated energy crops (switchgrass, *Miscanthus*)
- Process: Requires **pre-treatment** (acid/alkali/steam explosion) + **enzymatic hydrolysis** of cellulose/hemicellulose before fermentation
- Status: Pilot/demonstration scale; commercial plants emerging (India: IOCL Panipat 2G plant)
- Advantage: No food conflict, abundant feedstock

**Third Generation (3G) Bioethanol**
- Feedstocks: Algal biomass (microalgae, macroalgae)
- Process: Hydrolysis of algal carbohydrates, then fermentation
- Status: Research stage; high photosynthetic efficiency (~10× land productivity)
- Advantage: Non-arable land use, CO₂ sequestration potential

> [!NOTE] KTU Exam Tip
> A frequent 3-mark question is: *"Differentiate between 1G and 2G bioethanol with examples."* Always tabulate the three parameters — feedstock, process complexity, and food-vs-fuel issue.

## 2.2 Production Process Flow (1G & 2G)

### 2.2.1 First Generation Process Steps
1. **Feedstock preparation** — crushing cane / milling corn
2. **Saccharification** — starch → sugar using amylase enzymes
3. **Fermentation** — yeast (*S. cerevisiae*) at 30 °C, pH 4.5–5.0, anaerobic
4. **Distillation** — separates ethanol from wash (azeotrope at 95.6 vol% ethanol)
5. **Dehydration** — molecular sieve / azeotropic distillation to get **anhydrous ethanol** (99.5–100 %)

### 2.2.2 Second Generation Process Steps
1. **Pre-treatment** — breaks lignin sheath; uses dilute H₂SO₄ + steam explosion
2. **Enzymatic hydrolysis** — cellulase enzymes convert cellulose to glucose
3. **Fermentation** — same yeast (or genetically modified organisms like *Zymomonas mobilis*)
4. **Distillation + Dehydration**

## 2.3 The Core Fermentation Reaction

The stoichiometric equation of alcoholic fermentation is the **single most important equation** for the topic:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{yeast}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + \text{heat}$$

**Theoretical yield calculations** (essential for KTU numericals):

$$Y_{ethanol/glucose} = \frac{2 \times 46.07}{180.16} = 0.511 \;\frac{\text{kg ethanol}}{\text{kg glucose}}$$

$$Y_{CO_2/glucose} = \frac{2 \times 44.01}{180.16} = 0.489 \;\frac{\text{kg CO}_2}{\text{kg glucose}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] Theoretical vs. Practical Yield
> The **theoretical yield is 0.511 g ethanol/g glucose**, but the **practical/industrial yield is only 0.45–0.48 g/g (90–94 % efficiency)** because:
> - ~5 % of sugar is used by yeast for cell growth (biomass)
> - Some sugar forms by-products (glycerol, acetic acid)
> - Ethanol inhibition above ~12 vol% (kills yeast — the **toxicity limit**)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **Master Table — Memorise for ESE**

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ethanol yield from glucose | $Y = \dfrac{2 \times M_{ethanol}}{M_{glucose}} = \dfrac{92.14}{180.16}$ | kg/kg | Theoretical = 0.511 |
| Practical yield efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{Y_{actual}}{0.511} \times 100$ | % | Industrial: 88–94 % |
| Energy density ratio | $R_{energy} = \dfrac{LHV_{ethanol}}{LHV_{gasoline}} = \dfrac{26.8}{44.0}$ | — | ≈ 0.609 (61 %) |
| Stoichiometric AFR | $AFR_{st} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | kg/kg | 9.0 for E100, ~14.7 for gasoline |
| Energy balance (EROI) | $EROI = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$ | — | Sugarcane 8–10, Corn 1.3–1.8 |
| Carbon content (mass) | C% in C₂H₅OH | % | 24/46.07 = **52.14 %** |
| Stoichiometric CO₂ | $n_{CO_2} = 2 \times n_{ethanol}$ | mol/mol | Combustion: 2 moles CO₂ per mole EtOH |
| Azeotrope composition | 95.6 vol% ethanol + 4.4 % water | vol% | Cannot cross by simple distillation |
| Energy in ethanol | $E = m \times LHV = m \times 26.8$ | MJ | For 1 kg EtOH = 26.8 MJ |
| GHG reduction | $\Delta GHG = 1 - \dfrac{E_{fossil,eq}}{E_{bio}}$ | fraction | Sugarcane: 60–90 %, Corn: 20–40 % |
| Mass of CO₂ from combustion | $m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times \dfrac{2 \times 44}{46.07}$ | kg/kg | ≈ 1.91 kg CO₂ per kg EtOH |

## 2.5 Engine Utilisation — Real-World Engineering Utility

Bioethanol's high RON (108) and high latent heat make it ideal for:
- **High Compression Ratio (CR) SI engines** (CR up to 13:1 vs ~10:1 for gasoline) → better thermal efficiency
- **Turbocharged downsized engines** — resists knock
- **Flexible Fuel Vehicles (FFV)** that can run on E0 to E100
- **Oxygenated fuel** (35 wt% O) reduces CO and unburnt HC emissions

However, the **low LHV (26.8 MJ/kg vs 44 MJ/kg gasoline)** means fuel consumption (in L/100 km) is **~30–40 % higher**, requiring larger fuel tanks. The **high heat of vaporization** causes **cold-start difficulties** below 10 °C.

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Examiner's Insight
> Three "**Disadvantages**" that examiners love to test:
> 1. Low energy density → increased volumetric consumption
> 2. Corrosive to aluminium, magnesium, certain elastomers (needs SS/certified rubber)
> 3. Hygroscopic nature → phase separation in presence of water (limits E10 storage)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose

This derivation is the **backbone** of any 14-mark numerical in KTU on bioethanol production.

**Given:** Reaction $\quad C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$

**Step 1: Identify molecular weights**

$$M_{glucose} = (6 \times 12) + (12 \times 1) + (6 \times 16) = 72 + 12 + 96 = 180 \;\text{g/mol}$$

$$M_{ethanol} = (2 \times 12) + (6 \times 1) + 16 = 24 + 6 + 16 = 46 \;\text{g/mol}$$

$$M_{CO_2} = 12 + (2 \times 16) = 44 \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 2: Apply the law of stoichiometric conservation**

From the balanced equation, **1 mole (180 g) of glucose produces 2 moles (2 × 46 = 92 g) of ethanol.**

**Step 3: Formulate the yield ratio**

$$Y_{theoretical} = \frac{\text{Mass of ethanol produced}}{\text{Mass of glucose consumed}} = \frac{2 \times 46}{180}$$

$$Y_{theoretical} = \frac{92}{180} = 0.5111 \;\frac{\text{kg ethanol}}{\text{kg glucose}}$$

**Step 4: Verify the CO₂ side**

$$Y_{CO_2} = \frac{2 \times 44}{180} = \frac{88}{180} = 0.4889 \;\frac{\text{kg CO}_2}{\text{kg glucose}}$$

**Step 5: Mass balance check (should sum to 1.0)**

$$Y_{ethanol} + Y_{CO_2} = 0.5111 + 0.4889 = 1.000 \;\checkmark$$

This confirms perfect mass conservation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ethanol Production from a Given Feedstock Mass

**Problem type (commonly asked):** *"How much ethanol can be produced from 1000 kg of glucose if the fermentation efficiency is 90 %?"*

**Step 1: Theoretical maximum ethanol from 1000 kg glucose**

$$m_{ethanol,\,max} = 1000 \times 0.5111 = 511.1 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 2: Apply practical efficiency**

$$m_{ethanol,\,actual} = 511.1 \times 0.90 = 460.0 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 3: Convert to volume (using density 0.789 kg/L)**

$$V_{ethanol} = \frac{460.0}{0.789} = 583.0 \;\text{L}$$

> [!NOTE] This numerical format (with 90 % efficiency) is a classic KTU ESE style — practice it with values 85 %, 92 %, 95 %.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Available in Ethanol vs. Gasoline Equivalence

To deliver the same mechanical work, how much more ethanol is needed?

**Step 1:** Energy required for 1 km travel using gasoline (assume BSFC = 250 g/kWh, energy per km = 1 MJ example):

$$m_{gasoline} = \frac{E_{req}}{LHV_{gasoline}} = \frac{1.0}{44.0} = 0.02273 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 2:** Same energy using ethanol:

$$m_{ethanol} = \frac{E_{req}}{LHV_{ethanol}} = \frac{1.0}{26.8} = 0.03731 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 3:** Volumetric comparison (using densities)

$$V_{gasoline} = \frac{0.02273}{0.74} = 30.72 \;\text{mL (per MJ)}$$

$$V_{ethanol} = \frac{0.03731}{0.789} = 47.29 \;\text{mL (per MJ)}$$

**Step 4: Volumetric increase factor**

$$\Delta V\% = \frac{47.29 - 30.72}{30.72} \times 100 = 53.9\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT] The 53.9 % additional volume of ethanol per MJ is the reason FFVs carry larger fuel tanks. **Practically observed value is 30–40 %** because ethanol-blend engines run leaner and use higher compression ratio (recovering ~15 % of the loss).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Feedstock Comparison

| Parameter | Sugarcane (Brazil) | Corn (USA) | Cellulosic (India 2G) | Molasses (India) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lignocellulose | No | No | Yes | No |
| Yield (L/ha) | **6000–8000** | 3000–4000 | 3500–5500 | 2500–3500 |
| Feedstock cost ($/L EtOH) | 0.20 | 0.35 | 0.40–0.55 | 0.30 |
| EROI | 8–10 | 1.3–1.8 | 4–6 | 5–7 |
| GHG reduction vs gasoline | 60–90 % | 20–40 % | 70–95 % | 50–65 % |
| Food vs Fuel issue | Low | **High** | None | Low |
| Status (India) | Pilot (UP, Maharashtra) | None | IOCL Panipat, Bhatinda | **Commercial (EBP)** |

## 3.5 Combustion Stoichiometry of Pure Ethanol

The complete combustion of bioethanol:

$$C_2H_5OH + 3\,O_2 \longrightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O + \text{heat}$$

**Step 1: Per kg of ethanol, find moles**

$$n = \frac{1000}{46.07} = 21.71 \;\text{mol}$$

**Step 2: O₂ required**

$$n_{O_2} = 3 \times 21.71 = 65.13 \;\text{mol}$$

**Step 3: Air required (air contains 21 % O₂ by mole)**

$$n_{air} = \frac{65.13}{0.21} = 310.1 \;\text{mol}$$

**Step 4: Air mass**

$$m_{air} = 310.1 \times 28.97 = 8984 \;\text{g} = 8.98 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 5: Stoichiometric AFR by mass**

$$AFR_{st} = \frac{8.98}{1.0} = 8.98 \approx 9.0 \;\checkmark$$

This matches the standard textbook value of 9.0, confirming the derivation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Bioethanol Engine Utilisation Pathways


## 4.4 Process Comparison Block — 1G vs 2G vs 3G

```mermaid
    D3 --> D6[Pre-Treatment plus Hydrolysis plus Fermentation]
    D4 --> D7[Cell Disruption plus Hydrolysis plus Fermentation]
```
