<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define bioethanol. State any two of its key fuel properties.  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Bioethanol is ethyl alcohol (C₂H₅OH) produced from the fermentation of biomass-derived sugars, starches, or cellulose. It is a renewable liquid biofuel used as a gasoline substitute or blending agent in SI engines.

**Two key fuel properties:**
1. **Research Octane Number (RON) = 108–110** — significantly higher than gasoline (~92), enabling higher compression ratios and improved thermal efficiency.
2. **Lower Heating Value (LHV) = 26.8 MJ/kg** — about 39 % lower than gasoline, leading to higher volumetric fuel consumption of approximately 30–40 %.

*(Acceptable alternative properties: latent heat of vaporization 842 kJ/kg, stoichiometric AFR 9:1, density 0.789 kg/L.)*

---

### Question 2: List the three generations of bioethanol with one feedstock example for each.  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Generation | Feedstock Example | Process Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| 1st Generation | Sugarcane, Corn, Molasses | Simple — direct fermentation |
| 2nd Generation | Rice straw, Bagasse, Switchgrass | Complex — pre-treatment + enzymatic hydrolysis + fermentation |
| 3rd Generation | Microalgae (*Chlorella*), Macroalgae | Research-stage — cell disruption + hydrolysis + fermentation |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common marks-losing mistakes on bioethanol questions:
> 1. **Forgetting the CO₂ term** in fermentation mass balance — always include both ethanol AND CO₂ (they sum to unity)
> 2. **Using density 1.0 kg/L** instead of 0.789 kg/L when converting ethanol mass to volume
> 3. **Confusing the azeotrope composition** (95.6 vol%) with anhydrous ethanol (99.5+ vol%) — these are two different things
> 4. **Stating the LHV of ethanol as 44 MJ/kg** — this is gasoline; ethanol is 26.8 MJ/kg
> 5. **Missing engine modifications** when discussing E85/E100 (must mention SS fuel lines, Viton seals, larger injectors, cold-start assist)

---

### Question A (14 Marks):  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete process of bioethanol production from sugarcane with a neat flow diagram. State the overall fermentation reaction and the theoretical ethanol yield per kg of glucose.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A distillery processes 10,000 kg of molasses (containing 50 % fermentable sugars) per day. The fermentation efficiency is 88 % and the distillation recovery is 95 %. Calculate: (i) The mass of anhydrous ethanol produced per day, and (ii) The energy content of the produced ethanol in MJ.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Process flow of bioethanol from sugarcane (1G):**

1. **Sugarcane crushing** in roller mills → cane juice + bagasse
2. **Juice clarification** — addition of lime and heating to remove impurities
3. **Concentration and crystallization** — sugar crystallised; **molasses** (the by-product containing 50 % fermentable sugars) is used for ethanol production
4. **Dilution of molasses** with water to ~15–20 % sugar concentration
5. **Fermentation** with *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* at 30 °C, pH 4.5–5.0, anaerobic conditions, for 48–72 hours
6. **Distillation** in a multi-column system → 95.6 vol% ethanol-water azeotrope
7. **Dehydration** via molecular sieve adsorption (3 Å zeolite) → anhydrous ethanol (99.5 %+)

**Overall fermentation reaction:**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{yeast}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + \text{heat}$$

**Theoretical yield per kg of glucose:**

$$Y_{theoretical} = \frac{2 \times 46}{180} = 0.511 \;\text{kg ethanol per kg glucose}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** Molasses feed = 10,000 kg/day at 50 % sugar = 5,000 kg sugar/day
Fermentation efficiency = 88 %; Distillation recovery = 95 %

**Step 1: Theoretical ethanol from sugar**

$$m_{ethanol,\,theo} = 5000 \times 0.511 = 2555 \;\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 2: After fermentation efficiency (88 %)**

$$m_{ethanol,\,ferm} = 2555 \times 0.88 = 2248.4 \;\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 3: After distillation recovery (95 %)**

$$m_{ethanol,\,final} = 2248.4 \times 0.95 = 2136.0 \;\text{kg/day}$$

**Answer (i):** **≈ 2136 kg of anhydrous ethanol per day**

**Step 4: Energy content (using LHV = 26.8 MJ/kg)**

$$E = 2136.0 \times 26.8 = 57,244.8 \;\text{MJ/day}$$

**Answer (ii):** **≈ 5.72 × 10⁴ MJ per day**

> [!NOTE] Alternative check: Convert to volume: 2136 / 0.789 = **2707 L/day** — this is a useful cross-check answer for examiners.

---

### Question B (14 Marks):  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare first-generation and second-generation bioethanol in terms of feedstock, process, advantages, and limitations. Explain why second-generation bioethanol is considered more sustainable.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the modifications required in a conventional SI engine to run on E85 (85 % ethanol + 15 % gasoline). State at least five engine modifications and justify each one with a property of ethanol.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

| Parameter | 1st Generation Bioethanol | 2nd Generation Bioethanol |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock** | Sugarcane, corn, sugar beet, molasses (edible) | Rice straw, wheat straw, bagasse, switchgrass, forestry waste (non-edible) |
| **Process Steps** | Milling → Saccharification → Fermentation → Distillation → Dehydration | Pre-treatment (acid/steam) → Enzymatic hydrolysis → Fermentation → Distillation → Dehydration |
| **Process Complexity** | Low (well-established) | High (pre-treatment, enzyme costs) |
| **Cost of Production** | $0.30–$0.50/L | $0.50–$0.80/L (declining) |
| **Food vs Fuel Issue** | **Severe** (uses arable land, edible crops) | **None** (uses residues/wastes) |
| **GHG Reduction** | 20–90 % (varies) | 70–95 % (better lifecycle) |
| **Land Use Impact** | Competes with food production | Uses marginal/agricultural waste land |
| **Status** | Commercially mature | Demonstration to early commercial |

**Why 2G is more sustainable:**
1. **No food-fuel conflict** — uses non-edible lignocellulosic residues
2. **Higher GHG reduction** (70–95 % vs 20–40 % for corn ethanol)
3. **Abundant feedstock** — global agricultural residues exceed 5 billion tonnes/year
4. **Lower land-use change impact** — does not drive deforestation
5. **Circular economy fit** — converts waste to fuel, closes carbon loop

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Engine Modifications Required for E85 Operation:**

| # | Modification | Justification (Ethanol Property) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stainless steel fuel lines & tank** | Ethanol is hygroscopic and slightly corrosive to mild steel, aluminium, and magnesium |
| 2 | **Viton / Teflon seals & fuel hoses** | Ethanol attacks natural rubber, Buna-N, and certain elastomers — causes swelling/leaks |
| 3 | **Larger fuel injectors (≈ 30 % higher flow)** | Lower LHV (26.8 MJ/kg vs 44 MJ/kg) requires ~30–40 % more fuel by volume for same energy |
| 4 | **Higher compression ratio (CR ≈ 12:1 to 13:1)** | Higher RON (108) allows higher CR → better thermal efficiency (up to 38–42 %) |
| 5 | **Cold-start fuel enrichment / heated fuel rail** | High latent heat of vaporization (842 kJ/kg) causes cold-start difficulty below 10 °C |
| 6 | **Oxygen sensor recalibration (wide-band)** | Stoichiometric AFR changes from 14.7 (gasoline) to **9.0 (E100)** — ECU must adapt for blends |
| 7 | **Larger fuel tank** | Lower energy density + higher consumption → ~25 % more tank volume needed for same range |

**Conclusion:** FFV (Flexible Fuel Vehicle) technology is well-proven; Brazil runs a 100 % flex fleet, and India's Maruti Suzuki, Toyota, and Hyundai offer FFV variants as part of the **Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP) – E20 target by 2025–26**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorise for ESE**

- ✅ **Chemical formula:** C₂H₅OH, MW = 46.07 g/mol
- ✅ **Theoretical yield of ethanol from glucose:** **0.511 kg/kg** (always pair with 0.489 kg CO₂/kg)
- ✅ **LHV of ethanol = 26.8 MJ/kg**; of gasoline = 44 MJ/kg
- ✅ **RON of ethanol = 108–110** (allows high CR engines)
- ✅ **Latent heat of vaporization = 842 kJ/kg** (causes cold-start issues)
- ✅ **Stoichiometric AFR = 9.0 : 1** (vs 14.7 for gasoline)
- ✅ **Azeotrope composition = 95.6 vol% ethanol** at 78.2 °C
- ✅ **Indian standard for anhydrous ethanol = IS 15464:2004**
- ✅ **India's Ethanol Blending Programme:** **E20 target by 2025–26**, currently at ~12 %
- ✅ **Practical industrial yield efficiency:** 88–94 % of theoretical
- ✅ **Generations:** 1G (sugar/starch), 2G (cellulose), 3G (algae)
- ✅ **Engine modifications for E85:** SS lines, Viton seals, larger injectors, higher CR, cold-start assist
- ✅ **Combustion reaction:** C₂H₅OH + 3 O₂ → 2 CO₂ + 3 H₂O
- ✅ **Density of ethanol = 0.789 kg/L** (essential for mass ↔ volume conversion)
- ✅ **Fermentation conditions:** 30 °C, pH 4.5–5.0, anaerobic, *S. cerevisiae*
- ✅ **Key disadvantages:** Low LHV, hygroscopic (phase separation with water), corrosive to certain materials, cold-start issues
- ✅ **Key advantages:** High RON, renewable, oxygenated (reduces CO), high latent heat (charge cooling), domestic feedstock security
- ✅ **Brazil uses sugarcane** (best EROI ~8–10), **USA uses corn** (EROI ~1.3–1.8, controversial)
- ✅ **Major Indian 2G project:** IOCL Panipat & Bhatinda ethanol plants
- ✅ **CO₂ byproduct from fermentation** is captured and sold to beverage industry (additional revenue)
- ✅ **By-product lignin** from 2G process used as solid boiler fuel (improves overall energy balance)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio Diesel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET754 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bio Diesel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biodiesel** is defined as a **mono-alkyl ester** of long-chain fatty acids derived from a renewable **lipid feedstock** (such as vegetable oils, animal fats, or used cooking oils) through a chemical process called **transesterification** with a short-chain alcohol (typically **methanol** or **ethanol**) in the presence of a catalyst.

According to **ASTM D6751** (USA standard) and **EN 14214** (European standard), biodiesel is the standard biofuel blendstock used as a drop-in substitute or additive to **petroleum-derived diesel fuel** (HSD – High-Speed Diesel). It is chemically denoted as **FAME (Fatty Acid Methyl Ester)** when methanol is used, and **FAEE (Fatty Acid Ethyl Ester)** when ethanol is the alcohol.

**Biodiesel Production Equation (Overall Reaction):**
$$ \text{Triglyceride} + 3 \, \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst}} 3 \, \text{R-COOCH}_3 + \text{Glycerol} $$

The most common chemical formula representation for biodiesel is:
$$ \text{C}_{12} \text{H}_{24} \text{O}_2 \text{ (representative FAME — methyl laurate)} $$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Soap-Making Connection"**
>
> Imagine you have a greasy kitchen pan covered in thick vegetable oil. If you try to light this oil directly, it won't burn cleanly — it smokes, sputters, and leaves sticky residue. This is because vegetable oil is a **triglyceride**, a heavy, viscous molecule.
>
> Now, the same chemistry that makes soap (saponification) can be **reversed and controlled** to break the heavy oil into thinner, cleaner-burning molecules. When you mix vegetable oil with **methanol** and a pinch of **sodium hydroxide** (a catalyst), the large triglyceride molecule is "snipped" into three smaller **methyl ester** molecules (the biodiesel) plus **glycerol** (a useful by-product used in soap, cosmetics, and pharmaceuticals).
>
> Think of it as **"cutting a thick rope into three thin strings"** — each thin string (FAME) burns smoothly in a diesel engine, while the leftover piece (glycerol) becomes a valuable secondary product.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (must memorize for KTU exams):**
> - **Molecular weight of biodiesel (FAME):** approximately **290 g/mol** (range 280–300 g/mol)
> - **Density of biodiesel:** approximately **880 kg/m³** (range 860–900 kg/m³)
> - **Cetane Number of biodiesel:** greater than **47** (typically 50–60)
> - **Oxygen content in biodiesel:** approximately **10–11 % by mass**
> - **Theoretical air–fuel ratio:** approximately **12.5 : 1** (lower than diesel due to bonded oxygen)

## 1.4 Historical Context & Significance

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **1893** | Rudolf Diesel first demonstrated the diesel engine using **peanut oil** |
| **1937** | G. Chavanne (Belgium) patented the **transesterification** process for vegetable oils |
| **1970s** | Oil crisis sparked renewed interest in renewable fuels |
| **1990s** | First commercial biodiesel plants established in **Austria and Germany** |
| **2005** | ASTM D6751 standard published in the USA |
| **2010s onward** | India launched the **National Biodiesel Mission** with **Jatropha curcas** as the primary feedstock |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** India targets **20% ethanol blending (E20)** and **5% biodiesel blending (B5)** by **2030** under the **Pradhan Mantri JI-VAN Yojana** and **SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation)** schemes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Biodiesel — Transesterification

### 2.1.1 The Reactant: Triglycerides

A **triglyceride** is the main constituent of vegetable oils and animal fats. It consists of:
- **1 Glycerol molecule** (C₃H₅(OH)₃) — the "backbone"
- **3 Fatty acid chains** (R₁, R₂, R₃) — long hydrocarbon chains of 14 to 22 carbon atoms

**Generic structure of a triglyceride:**
$$ \text{CH}_2-\text{OOC-R}_1 \;\; \vert \;\; \text{CH}-\text{OOC-R}_2 \;\; \vert \;\; \text{CH}_2-\text{OOC-R}_3 $$

The fatty acids commonly encountered are:
- **Palmitic acid (C16:0)** — saturated
- **Stearic acid (C18:0)** — saturated
- **Oleic acid (C18:1)** — monounsaturated
- **Linoleic acid (C18:2)** — polyunsaturated
- **Linolenic acid (C18:3)** — polyunsaturated

### 2.1.2 The Transesterification Reaction (Step-by-Step)

**Transesterification** is a **reversible, three-step sequential reaction** where the triglyceride is progressively converted to diglyceride, monoglyceride, and finally to glycerol, with each step releasing one molecule of methyl ester (biodiesel).

**Overall stoichiometry:**
$$ \text{TG} + 3\,\text{MeOH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol} $$

**Step 1 — Triglyceride → Diglyceride:**
$$ \text{TG} + \text{MeOH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{DG} + \text{FAME} $$

**Step 2 — Diglyceride → Monoglyceride:**
$$ \text{DG} + \text{MeOH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{MG} + \text{FAME} $$

**Step 3 — Monoglyceride → Glycerol:**
$$ \text{MG} + \text{MeOH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{GL} + \text{FAME} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 3 moles of methanol are required:** One mole of triglyceride has **three ester linkages** (R-COO-R′). Each linkage must be individually cleaved by one methanol molecule. Therefore, the **stoichiometric molar ratio** of **methanol : oil = 3 : 1**.
>
> However, in practice a **higher molar ratio (6:1 to 12:1)** is used to drive the equilibrium forward (Le Chatelier's principle).

### 2.1.3 Role of the Catalyst

The catalyst can be classified as:

| Type | Examples | Reaction Conditions | Conversion |
|------|----------|--------------------|------------|
| **Homogeneous Alkali** | NaOH, KOH | 60–65 °C, 1 atm, 1 h | **> 98 %** |
| **Homogeneous Acid** | H₂SO₄, HCl | 55–80 °C, 1 atm, 4–8 h | **~ 90 %** |
| **Heterogeneous** | CaO, MgO, Zeolites | 60–200 °C, 1–30 atm | 80–95 % |
| **Enzymatic** | Lipase (Candida, Pseudomonas) | 30–50 °C, mild | 70–90 % |
| **Supercritical (non-catalytic)** | No catalyst | > 240 °C, > 80 atm, supercritical MeOH | **> 95 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Why is alkali-catalysed transesterification preferred over acid-catalysed?"*
>
> **Answer:** Alkali catalysis is **~ 4000 times faster** than acid catalysis, operates at **lower temperatures (60 °C vs 80 °C)**, gives **higher conversion (>98 %)**, and requires **lesser reaction time (1 h vs 8 h)**. However, it requires the feedstock to have **low free fatty acid (FFA < 2 %)** content. For high-FFA feedstocks (e.g., used cooking oil, animal tallow), a **two-step acid-pretreatment followed by alkali transesterification** is used.

## 2.2 Process Flow of Biodiesel Production

The industrial biodiesel production process consists of **four major stages**:

### Stage 1 — Feedstock Pretreatment
- **Filtration** to remove solids
- **Degumming** to remove phospholipids using phosphoric acid
- **Deacidification** (if FFA > 2 %) using acid esterification with H₂SO₄ and methanol
- **Drying** to remove moisture (water < 0.1 %)

### Stage 2 — Transesterification Reaction
- Oil is heated to **60 °C** in a stirred reactor
- **Methanol + NaOH/KOH** mixture (sodium methoxide) is prepared
- The methoxide is added to the oil at **6:1 molar ratio**
- Reaction continues for **60–90 minutes** at **60–65 °C** with continuous stirring at **300–600 rpm**

### Stage 3 — Separation
- Reaction mixture is transferred to a **settling/decanting tank**
- **Glycerol** (denser phase) settles at the bottom due to higher density (~1.26 g/cm³)
- **Biodiesel** (lighter phase) floats on top
- Two distinct layers form within **2–8 hours**

### Stage 4 — Purification
- **Water washing** to remove residual methanol, glycerol, soap, and catalyst
- **Drying** under vacuum at 80–100 °C
- **Filtration** and **quality testing** against ASTM D6751 / EN 14214

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` for absolute value notation to prevent markdown table breakage.**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Standard Value |
|---|--------------------|------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Molar Ratio of alcohol to oil** | $n_{\text{alcohol}}/n_{\text{oil}}$ | 6 : 1 (typical), 3 : 1 (stoichiometric) |
| 2 | **Theoretical yield of biodiesel** | $Y_{\text{theor}} = \dfrac{3 \times M_{\text{FAME}}}{M_{\text{TG}} + 3 M_{\text{MeOH}} - M_{\text{Glycerol}}}$ | Mass fraction (dimensionless) |
| 3 | **Actual yield (mass basis)** | $Y_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME, obtained}}}{m_{\text{oil, fed}}} \times 100\,\%$ | % |
| 4 | **Catalyst loading** | $W_{\text{cat}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{cat}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100\,\%$ | 0.5 – 1.5 % (alkali) |
| 5 | **Density of biodiesel** | $\rho_{\text{bio}}$ | **880 kg/m³** (approx) |
| 6 | **Kinematic viscosity** | $\nu$ | 3.5 – 5.0 mm²/s at 40 °C |
| 7 | **Cetane number** | $CN$ | > 47 (ASTM), > 51 (EN) |
| 8 | **Flash point** | $T_{\text{flash}}$ | > 130 °C (ASTM), > 120 °C (EN) |
| 9 | **Calorific value (HHV)** | $Q_{\text{HHV}}$ | **39 – 41 MJ/kg** |
| 10 | **Oxygen content** | $\%O$ | 10 – 11 % by mass |
| 11 | **Stoichiometric air–fuel ratio (mass)** | $(A/F)_{\text{st}}$ | 12.5 : 1 |
| 12 | **Acid value** | $AV = \dfrac{56.1 \times V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}}}{m_{\text{oil}}}$ | mg KOH/g oil |
| 13 | **Saponification value** | $SV = \dfrac{56.1 \times (V_b - V_s) \times N}{m}$ | mg KOH/g oil |
| 14 | **Iodine value** | $IV$ | < 120 g I₂/100 g (EN 14214) |
| 15 | **Cloud point** | $T_{\text{cloud}}$ | −3 to +15 °C (feedstock dependent) |
| 16 | **Pour point** | $T_{\text{pour}}$ | −5 to +10 °C |

## 2.4 Properties of Biodiesel vs. Petrodiesel

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Comparison Table — memorize this for 14-mark questions:**

| Property | Petrodiesel | Biodiesel (B100) | Effect |
|----------|-------------|------------------|--------|
| **Density (kg/m³)** | 830 – 860 | 860 – 900 | Slightly higher |
| **Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C (mm²/s)** | 2.0 – 4.5 | 3.5 – 5.0 | Higher — better lubrication |
| **Cetane number** | 45 – 55 | 50 – 60 | Higher — better ignition |
| **Flash point (°C)** | 60 – 80 | > 130 | Much higher — safer storage |
| **Calorific value (MJ/kg)** | 42 – 45 | 39 – 41 | **~ 10 % lower** |
| **Oxygen content (wt %)** | 0 | 10 – 11 | Higher — cleaner combustion |
| **Sulphur content** | < 10 ppm (ULSD) | < 10 ppm | Almost nil |
| **Aromatic content** | 25 – 35 % | 0 % | None — non-carcinogenic |
| **Water content** | Traces | Low (hygroscopic) | Slightly higher |
| **Biodegradability** | Slow | **98 % in 21 days** | Much faster |
| **Cold flow properties (°C)** | Better | Poor (high cloud/pour point) | Inferior in winter |

## 2.5 Advantages of Biodiesel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Renewable and bio-degradable** (98 % in 21 days vs. petrodiesel's negligible degradation)
> - **Non-toxic** and **non-carcinogenic** (zero aromatics)
> - **Higher cetane number** → smoother ignition, less knocking
> - **Higher flash point (130 °C)** → safer handling, transport, and storage
> - **Lubricity improver** — extends engine life, restores injector pump performance
> - **Reduces emissions:** CO₂ (~ 78 % lifecycle), CO (−50 %), HC (−67 %), PM (−47 %), SOx (~ 0 %)
> - **Energy security** — reduces dependence on imported crude oil
> - **Rural employment** — cultivation of oil-seed crops creates jobs
> - **Drop-in fuel** — no engine modification required for B20 (20 % blend)

## 2.6 Disadvantages of Biodiesel

> [!WARNING]
> - **Lower calorific value** (~ 10 % less than diesel) → slightly higher fuel consumption
> - **Higher viscosity** at low temperatures → cold-start problems, injector coking
> - **Poor cold-flow properties** (high cloud and pour points)
> - **Hygroscopic** — absorbs water → corrosion of fuel system components
> - **Oxidative degradation** during long storage → formation of acids, gums, sediments
> - **Solvent effect** — dissolves rubber seals and gaskets in older engines
> - **Food vs. fuel debate** — first-generation biodiesel competes with edible oil supply
> - **Higher feedstock cost** — 70–80 % of total production cost
> - **NOx emissions increase by ~ 10 %** due to higher combustion temperature

## 2.7 Feedstocks for Biodiesel Production

| Generation | Feedstock Type | Examples | FFA Content |
|------------|----------------|----------|-------------|
| **1st Gen** | Edible vegetable oils | Soybean, Sunflower, Rapeseed, Palm, Coconut | Low (< 2 %) |
| **2nd Gen** | Non-edible oils | **Jatropha**, Karanja, Mahua, Neem, Castor | Higher (2 – 20 %) |
| **3rd Gen** | Waste lipids | Used cooking oil (UCO), Animal tallow, Yellow grease | Variable (high) |
| **4th Gen** | Algal oils & engineered | Microalgae (Chlorella, Spirulina), Synthetic biology | Designed |

> [!NOTE]
> **India's focus** is on **2nd-generation non-edible oils**, especially **Jatropha curcas** and **Pongamia pinnata (Karanja)**, grown on wastelands without competing with food crops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Yield of Biodiesel

### Problem Statement

Calculate the **theoretical mass yield of biodiesel (FAME)** produced from **1000 kg of soybean oil** (assumed pure triolein, M = 885.4 g/mol) reacting completely with **methanol** (M = 32.04 g/mol), forming **methyl oleate** (M = 296.5 g/mol) and **glycerol** (M = 92.09 g/mol).

### Given Data
- $m_{\text{oil}} = 1000 \text{ kg}$
- $M_{\text{TG}} = 885.4 \text{ g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{MeOH}} = 32.04 \text{ g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{FAME}} = 296.5 \text{ g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{GL}} = 92.09 \text{ g/mol}$

### Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate the number of moles of triglyceride fed:**

$$ n_{\text{TG}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{M_{\text{TG}}} = \dfrac{1000 \times 10^3 \text{ g}}{885.4 \text{ g/mol}} $$

$$ n_{\text{TG}} = 1129.4 \text{ mol} $$

**Step 2 — From the stoichiometry** $\text{TG} + 3\,\text{MeOH} \rightarrow 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{GL}$**, 1 mole of TG gives 3 moles of FAME:**

$$ n_{\text{FAME}} = 3 \times n_{\text{TG}} = 3 \times 1129.4 = 3388.2 \text{ mol} $$

**Step 3 — Calculate the mass of FAME produced:**

$$ m_{\text{FAME}} = n_{\text{FAME}} \times M_{\text{FAME}} = 3388.2 \times 296.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} $$

$$ m_{\text{FAME}} = 1004.6 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 4 — Compute theoretical yield (%):**

$$ Y_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1004.6}{1000} \times 100 $$

$$ \boxed{Y_{\text{theoretical}} = 100.46 \, \%} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Since each FAME molecule (296.5 g/mol) is lighter than ⅓ of the triglyceride mass, the **total mass of FAME slightly exceeds the mass of oil fed**. The "excess mass" comes from the **three methyl (–CH₃) groups** contributed by methanol, replacing the glycerol backbone.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio

### Why is the A/F ratio lower than diesel?

The presence of **chemically bonded oxygen** in FAME molecules reduces the external air required for complete combustion.

### Derivation for Methyl Oleate (C₁₉H₃₆O₂):

**Step 1 — Combustion equation:**

$$ \text{C}_{19}\text{H}_{36}\text{O}_2 + a\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 19\,\text{CO}_2 + 18\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 2 — Balance hydrogen:**

$$ 36\,\text{H atoms} = 2 \times 18 \;\Rightarrow\; 18\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\;\checkmark $$

**Step 3 — Balance oxygen atoms on both sides:**

$$ 2 + 2a = 19 \times 2 + 18 = 56 $$

$$ 2a = 54 \;\Rightarrow\; a = 27 \text{ mol O}_2 $$

**Step 4 — Account for atmospheric oxygen (~ 21 % O₂, 79 % N₂):**

$$ \text{Air} = 27 \times \dfrac{100}{21} = 128.57 \text{ mol} $$

**Step 5 — Convert to mass basis:**

$$ m_{\text{fuel}} = 12(19) + 1(36) + 16(2) = 228 + 36 + 32 = 296 \text{ g} $$

$$ m_{\text{air}} = 128.57 \times 28.97 = 3725 \text{ g} $$

**Step 6 — Mass-based A/F ratio:**

$$ \left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{mass}} = \dfrac{3725}{296} $$

$$ \boxed{\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{mass}} \approx 12.58 : 1} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Petrodiesel (C₁₂H₂₃)** gives A/F ≈ **14.5 : 1**. Biodiesel's lower A/F means **less air is required per kg of fuel**, which affects **combustion chamber design, EGR ratios, and emissions tuning** in modern CRDi engines.

## 3.3 Derivation: Catalyst Loading Calculation

### Problem

A 500 kg batch of Jatropha oil is to be transesterified. If the recommended **NaOH loading is 1.0 % by mass of oil**, calculate:
1. The mass of NaOH required
2. The mass of methanol required (at 6:1 molar ratio)
3. The volume of NaOH + methanol mixture (assuming density of mixture = 0.85 g/cm³)

### Given
- $m_{\text{oil}} = 500 \text{ kg}$
- $M_{\text{Jatropha oil (avg)}} \approx 870 \text{ g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{MeOH}} = 32.04 \text{ g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{NaOH}} = 40 \text{ g/mol}$

### Solution

**Step 1 — Moles of oil:**

$$ n_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{500{,}000}{870} = 574.7 \text{ mol} $$

**Step 2 — Moles of methanol at 6:1 ratio:**

$$ n_{\text{MeOH}} = 6 \times 574.7 = 3448.2 \text{ mol} $$

**Step 3 — Mass of methanol:**

$$ m_{\text{MeOH}} = 3448.2 \times 32.04 \times 10^{-3} = 110.5 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 4 — Mass of NaOH at 1.0 % loading:**

$$ m_{\text{NaOH}} = 0.01 \times 500 = 5.0 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 5 — Total mass of methanol + NaOH mixture:**

$$ m_{\text{mix}} = 110.5 + 5.0 = 115.5 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 6 — Volume of mixture:**

$$ V_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{mix}}}{\rho_{\text{mix}}} = \dfrac{115.5 \text{ kg}}{850 \text{ kg/m}^3} $$

$$ \boxed{V_{\text{mix}} = 0.136 \text{ m}^3 = 135.9 \text{ L}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Methanol is **toxic, flammable, and requires explosion-proof reactors**. The recovery of unreacted methanol (typically 30–40 % excess) is critical to plant economics. A **methanol recovery column** operating at 64.7 °C (methanol boiling point) is included in industrial plants.

## 3.4 Derivation: Acid Value and FFA Estimation

The **acid value (AV)** measures the free fatty acid content of the oil, critical for determining whether alkali or acid catalysis is suitable.

$$ AV = \dfrac{56.1 \times V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \;\; \text{(mg KOH/g oil)} $$

**Conversion from AV to % FFA (as oleic acid):**

$$ \%FFA = \dfrac{AV \times 0.503}{1} \;\;\text{(using molecular weight of oleic acid = 282.47)} $$

**Decision rule:**

$$ \text{If } \%FFA < 2 \% \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Direct alkali transesterification} $$

$$ \text{If } \%FFA > 2 \% \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Two-step: acid esterification} \rightarrow \text{alkali transesterification} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why this matters:** In alkali catalysis, free fatty acids react with NaOH to form **soap (saponification)**:
> $$ \text{R-COOH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{R-COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$
> Soap formation **consumes the catalyst**, **emulsifies the product**, and makes glycerol separation extremely difficult, drastically reducing biodiesel yield.

## 3.5 Energy Balance Summary for Biodiesel Plant

For every **1000 kg of oil** processed in a 1-hour batch:

| Stream | In (kg) | Out (kg) |
|--------|---------|----------|
| Vegetable oil | 1000 | — |
| Methanol (6:1) | 220 (with 40 % recovery → 88 kg net consumed) | 132 recovered |
| NaOH catalyst | 10 | removed in wash |
| Biodiesel (FAME) | — | **~ 1005** |
| Glycerol (crude, 80 % pure) | — | ~ 105 |
| Wash water | 200 | 200 + impurities |
| **Mass balance closure** | 1430 in | 1442 out (≈ 1 % measurement error) |

    E --> F[Gravity Settling Tank 2 to 8 h settling]

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q:** Define biodiesel. List any **four** important properties of biodiesel that make it a suitable alternative to diesel fuel.

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Biodiesel is a **mono-alkyl ester** of long-chain fatty acids, produced by the **transesterification** of vegetable oils, animal fats, or used cooking oils with a short-chain alcohol (methanol/ethanol) in the presence of a catalyst.

**Four important properties:**

| # | Property | Value | Advantage over Diesel |
|---|----------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Cetane number** | 50 – 60 | Smoother ignition, less knocking |
| 2 | **Flash point** | > 130 °C | Safer storage and transport |
| 3 | **Biodegradability** | 98 % in 21 days | Environmentally friendly |
| 4 | **Lubricity** | High | Reduces engine wear |

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any 4 properties (½ mark each).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q:** What is **transesterification**? Write the overall chemical equation for the transesterification of a triglyceride with methanol.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Transesterification** is the chemical reaction in which the **alkyl group (R′)** of an ester is **exchanged with the alkyl group of an alcohol**, in the presence of a catalyst, producing a new ester and a new alcohol. In biodiesel production, the triglyceride (R-COO-R′) reacts with methanol (CH₃OH) to form **fatty acid methyl ester (FAME)** — the biodiesel — and **glycerol** as a by-product.

**Overall balanced chemical equation:**

$$ \text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightleftharpoons[\text{Catalyst}]{60\text{ °C}}\; 3\,\text{R-COOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 $$

where the **LHS** shows triglyceride + methanol, and the **RHS** shows three methyl ester (FAME) molecules + one glycerol molecule.

**Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for balanced equation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Module Internal Choice — Select **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **transesterification process** for biodiesel production in detail. Discuss the role of **molar ratio, catalyst type, temperature, and reaction time** on the conversion efficiency. What is the **stoichiometric molar ratio** of methanol to oil, and why is a **higher ratio (6:1)** used in practice?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **properties of biodiesel (B100) and petrodiesel** in a tabular form (at least 8 properties). Justify why biodiesel is considered a **cleaner and renewable** alternative despite having a lower calorific value.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Process Description (3 marks):** Transesterification (also called alcoholysis) is a **three-step reversible reaction** in which one mole of triglyceride reacts with three moles of alcohol (typically methanol) to yield **three moles of fatty acid methyl ester (FAME)** and **one mole of glycerol**. Each step releases one FAME molecule and converts TG → DG → MG → glycerol progressively.

**Key operating parameters and their effects:**

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on Conversion |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Molar ratio (MeOH : oil)** | 6 : 1 (practical) vs 3 : 1 (stoichiometric) | Higher ratio shifts equilibrium forward; too high dilutes catalyst and complicates recovery |
| **Catalyst type** | NaOH / KOH (alkali), H₂SO₄ (acid) | Alkali 4000× faster; suitable when FFA < 2 % |
| **Catalyst loading** | 0.5 – 1.5 % (w/w of oil) | Optimum ~ 1 %; excess causes saponification |
| **Temperature** | 60 – 65 °C (alkali), 55 – 80 °C (acid) | Above 65 °C → methanol boils, equilibrium disturbed |
| **Reaction time** | 60 min (alkali), 4 – 8 h (acid) | Longer time gives higher conversion but lower plant throughput |
| **Stirring intensity** | 300 – 600 rpm | Ensures biphasic mixing of oil and methanol |
| **Water content** | < 0.1 % | Water causes soap formation and catalyst poisoning |

**2. Stoichiometric vs Practical Molar Ratio (2 marks):**

The **stoichiometric ratio is 3 : 1** (one triglyceride has three ester linkages, each requiring one methanol molecule). However, the reaction is **reversible**, and to drive the equilibrium forward per **Le Chatelier's principle**, an **excess of methanol (6:1)** is used. Excess methanol also compensates for **losses due to evaporation** and **side reactions**.

**3. Catalyst Selection Justification (2 marks):**

Alkali catalysts (NaOH, KOH) are preferred because they:
- Provide high conversion (> 98 %)
- Operate at low temperature (60 °C) and atmospheric pressure
- Require short reaction time (1 hour)
- Are inexpensive and readily available

The drawback: they require **FFA < 2 %**. For high-FFA feedstocks (UCO, Jatropha), a **two-step acid pretreatment** is required first to esterify the FFA to FAME using H₂SO₄, followed by alkali transesterification.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Tabular Comparison of Biodiesel vs Petrodiesel (5 marks):**

| Property | Petrodiesel | Biodiesel (B100) |
|----------|-------------|------------------|
| Density (kg/m³) | 830 – 860 | 860 – 900 |
| Viscosity at 40 °C (mm²/s) | 2.0 – 4.5 | 3.5 – 5.0 |
| Cetane number | 45 – 55 | 50 – 60 |
| Flash point (°C) | 60 – 80 | > 130 |
| Calorific value (MJ/kg) | 42 – 45 | 39 – 41 |
| Oxygen content (wt %) | 0 | 10 – 11 |
| Sulphur content (ppm) | < 10 | < 10 |
| Aromatic content (%) | 25 – 35 | 0 |
| Biodegradability | Very slow | 98 % in 21 days |
| Cloud point (°C) | −15 to +5 | −3 to +15 |

**Why biodiesel is considered cleaner and renewable (2 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> - **Renewable:** Derived from biomass (oil seeds, algae, waste oils) — replenishable in months/years vs. millions of years for fossil fuels.
> - **Carbon cycle:** CO₂ released during combustion is **reabsorbed** by next-generation crops, making it nearly **carbon-neutral** (78 % lifecycle reduction).
> - **Lower emissions:** Reduces CO, HC, PM, SOx significantly due to bonded oxygen and zero sulphur.
> - **Non-toxic and biodegradable:** No aromatics, no carcinogens, breaks down rapidly.
> - **Slightly lower calorific value (10 %)** is offset by **higher cetane**, **better lubricity**, **higher flash point safety**, and **lower emissions**, making it a net positive for sustainability.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **various feedstocks** used for biodiesel production. Classify them into **four generations** with suitable examples. Discuss why **India prefers 2nd-generation non-edible oils** like Jatropha and Karanja.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a **neat process flow diagram**, explain the **industrial production of biodiesel** from Jatropha oil. Mention the **key process parameters** (temperature, molar ratio, catalyst, settling time) and state the **mass balance** for a typical 1000 kg batch.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Feedstock Classification (5 marks):**

| Generation | Type | Examples | FFA Level | India's Status |
|------------|------|----------|-----------|----------------|
| **1st Gen** | Edible vegetable oils | Soybean, Sunflower, Rapeseed, Palm, Coconut, Groundnut | Low (< 2 %) | Available but food-security conflict |
| **2nd Gen** | Non-edible oils | **Jatropha, Karanja (Pongamia), Mahua, Neem, Castor, Jojoba** | Medium (2 – 20 %) | **Most preferred in India** |
| **3rd Gen** | Waste / used oils | Used Cooking Oil (UCO), Animal tallow, Yellow grease, Trap grease | High (5 – 30 %) | Promoted under SATAT scheme |
| **4th Gen** | Advanced / Algal | Microalgae (Chlorella, Spirulina), engineered microbes, synthetic biology | Designed | Under R&D, future potential |

**Why India prefers 2nd-generation non-edible oils (2 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Food security:** India is a populous nation with 1.4 billion people; using edible oils for fuel competes with the food chain and inflates food prices.
> 2. **Wasteland utilization:** Jatropha and Karanja can grow on **degraded, marginal, and waste lands** — they do not require fertile agricultural land.
> 3. **Drought tolerance:** Jatropha survives with annual rainfall of just **300 – 1000 mm**, suitable for India's arid/semi-arid regions (Rajasthan, Gujarat, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu).
> 4. **Long productive life:** 30 – 50 years of seed yield; minimal inputs (no irrigation, no fertilizer).
> 5. **Government policy alignment:** National Biodiesel Mission targets **Jatropha plantations on 11.2 million hectares** by 2030.
> 6. **Carbon sequestration:** Jatropha plantations act as carbon sinks, generating **carbon credits**.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Process Flow (3 marks):**

1. **Feedstock Pretreatment:** Crude Jatropha oil is filtered, degummed (H₃PO₄ treatment), and dried. FFA is determined; if > 2 %, acid esterification is done first.
2. **Reaction:** Oil is heated to **60 °C** in a **stirred batch reactor**. Sodium methoxide (NaOH dissolved in methanol at 6:1 molar ratio) is added. Reaction proceeds for **60 min** at **60 – 65 °C** with **600 rpm** stirring.
3. **Phase Separation:** Reaction mixture is transferred to a **gravity decanter** for **6 – 8 hours**. Two layers form — **glycerol (bottom, ~ 1.26 g/cm³)** and **biodiesel (top, ~ 0.88 g/cm³)**.
4. **Purification:** Biodiesel is **water-washed** (counter-current, 50 °C) to remove residual methanol, soap, glycerol, and catalyst. Then **vacuum-dried** at 80 °C and **polished-filtered**.
5. **Methanol Recovery:** Excess methanol is recovered via a **distillation column** at 64.7 °C and recycled.
6. **Quality Testing:** Tested against **ASTM D6751** and **EN 14214** standards; certified as **B100** (100 % biodiesel).

**Key Process Parameters Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Oil feed | 1000 kg Jatropha oil |
| Molar ratio (MeOH:Oil) | 6 : 1 |
| Catalyst | NaOH (1 % w/w of oil) |
| Reaction temperature | 60 – 65 °C |
| Reaction time | 60 – 90 min |
| Settling time | 6 – 8 h |
| Biodiesel yield | ~ 1005 kg (FAME) |
| Glycerol by-product | ~ 105 kg (crude, 80 % pure) |

**Mass Balance Statement (2 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> For a **1000 kg Jatropha oil** feed batch:
> - **Input:** 1000 kg oil + 110 kg methanol (consumed, net) + 10 kg NaOH + 200 kg wash water = **1320 kg**
> - **Output:** 1005 kg biodiesel (B100) + 105 kg crude glycerol + 132 kg recovered methanol (recycled) + ~ 78 kg water + impurities
> - **Conversion efficiency:** > 98 %

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (where marks are deducted):**
>
> 1. **Writing "transesterification is irreversible"** — INCORRECT. The reaction is **reversible**; hence excess methanol is needed. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing FAME and FAEE** — FAME = methyl ester (methanol), FAEE = ethyl ester (ethanol). Examiners are strict. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Forgetting the FFA pretreatment logic** — For Jatropha, FFA is often 8 – 15 %, so direct alkali catalysis fails. Students must mention the **two-step process** (acid pretreatment → alkali transesterification). **(−1 to 2 marks)**
> 4. **Not stating units in numerical answers** — e.g., writing "yield = 1004" without "kg" loses 0.5 marks.
> 5. **Confusing "cloud point" and "pour point"** — Cloud point is the temperature at which wax crystals first appear; pour point is the temperature at which the fuel ceases to flow. They are **different** by typically 3 – 6 °C.
> 6. **Ignoring Le Chatelier's principle** when justifying the 6:1 ratio — must explicitly mention it. **(−0.5 mark)**
> 7. **Forgetting glycerol as a by-product** in the reaction equation.
> 8. **Stating "biodiesel increases NOx"** without explaining **why** (higher combustion temperature due to oxygenated fuel, ~ 10 % increase). Examiners expect the mechanism.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Bio Diesel**

### A. Core Definition
- [ ] Biodiesel = **FAME** (Fatty Acid Methyl Ester) — mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids
- [ ] Produced by **transesterification** of triglycerides with methanol
- [ ] Standards: **ASTM D6751** (USA), **EN 14214** (Europe), **IS 15607** (India)

### B. Chemistry Essentials
- [ ] Reaction: **TG + 3 MeOH → 3 FAME + Glycerol** (reversible, 3-step)
- [ ] **Stoichiometric ratio = 3:1**, **practical ratio = 6:1** (Le Chatelier's principle)
- [ ] **Alkali catalyst** (NaOH/KOH): fast, 60 °C, 1 h, requires **FFA < 2 %**
- [ ] **Acid catalyst** (H₂SO₄): slow, 80 °C, 8 h, tolerates high FFA but slower
- [ ] **Two-step process** for high-FFA feedstocks: acid pretreatment → alkali transesterification

### C. Process Engineering
- [ ] Stages: **Pretreatment → Reaction (60 °C) → Settling (6 – 8 h) → Water Wash → Vacuum Dry → QC**
- [ ] Glycerol is the **by-product** (useful in pharma, cosmetics, soap)
- [ ] Methanol **recovery** at 64.7 °C via distillation
- [ ] **Methanol-to-oil mass ratio ≈ 1 : 5 to 1 : 10**

### D. Property Comparison (Memorize!)
- [ ] **Cetane number:** biodiesel 50 – 60 > diesel 45 – 55 (better ignition)
- [ ] **Flash point:** biodiesel > 130 °C >> diesel 60 – 80 °C (much safer)
- [ ] **Calorific value:** biodiesel 39 – 41 MJ/kg < diesel 42 – 45 MJ/kg (~ 10 % lower)
- [ ] **Oxygen content:** biodiesel 10 – 11 % vs diesel 0 % (cleaner combustion)
- [ ] **Viscosity:** biodiesel slightly higher (better lubricity)
- [ ] **A/F ratio:** biodiesel ~ 12.5 < diesel ~ 14.5
- [ ] **NOx:** biodiesel +10 %; **CO, HC, PM:** biodiesel −40 to −70 %

### E. Feedstock Hierarchy
- [ ] **1st Gen:** edible oils (soy, palm, sunflower)
- [ ] **2nd Gen:** non-edible (Jatropha, Karanja, Mahua) — **India's focus**
- [ ] **3rd Gen:** waste (UCO, tallow) — SATAT scheme
- [ ] **4th Gen:** algal & synthetic — future tech

### F. Key Indian Initiatives
- [ ] **SATAT** — Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation (target: 5000 plants, 350 Cr L UCO collection)
- [ ] **Pradhan Mantri JI-VAN Yojana** — financial support for 2G ethanol/biodiesel plants
- [ ] **National Policy on Biofuels 2018** — target 5 % biodiesel blending (B5) by 2030
- [ ] **Biodiesel blending obligation** under Ethanol Blending Programme

### G. KTU Exam-Ready Formulas
- [ ] $\%FFA = AV \times 0.503$ (as oleic acid)
- [ ] $Y = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100\,\%$
- [ ] $A/F = 12.5$ (biodiesel), $A/F = 14.5$ (diesel)
- [ ] Stoichiometric MeOH:Oil = 3:1; practical = 6:1

### H. Advantages vs Disadvantages Snapshot
- [ ] **Advantages:** renewable, biodegradable, non-toxic, higher cetane, higher flash point, lower emissions, lubricity
- [ ] **Disadvantages:** lower calorific value, poor cold-flow, hygroscopic, NOx increase, higher cost, food vs. fuel debate

### I. One-Liners for KTU Examiner Impressions
- [ ] "Biodiesel is the only alternative fuel that can be used in **any existing diesel engine without modification**."
- [ ] "Rudolf Diesel's original 1893 engine ran on **peanut oil** — the first biodiesel prototype."
- [ ] "Glycerol by-product constitutes **10 %** of biodiesel mass and has significant commercial value."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biogas Production from Waste Biomass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET754 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biogas Production from Waste Biomass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biogas** is a colourless, flammable gaseous biofuel produced by the **anaerobic digestion (AD)** of organic waste biomass (agricultural residue, animal dung, municipal solid waste, kitchen waste, sewage sludge, aquatic biomass) in the absence of oxygen, through the synergistic action of a consortium of **micro-organisms (bacteria & archaea)**. The principal combustible component is **methane (CH₄ = 50–70 % by volume)**, the remainder being **carbon dioxide (CO₂ = 30–40 %)** and trace amounts of **H₂S, NH₃, N₂ and water vapour**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Biogas is classified as a **secondary renewable energy carrier** because it is *derived* from solar energy captured originally via photosynthesis and stored in biomass chemical bonds (C–H bonds). It is considered **carbon-neutral** because the CO₂ released during combustion was recently absorbed by the biomass during its growth phase.

The standard physico-chemical properties of purified biogas (≈ 60 % CH₄) and pure methane are tabulated below for engineering reference:

| Property | Pure CH₄ | Raw Biogas (≈60 % CH₄) |
|---|---|---|
| Calorific Value | **55.5 MJ/kg** | **20–25 MJ/m³** |
| Density (STP) | 0.717 kg/m³ | ≈ 1.2 kg/m³ |
| Auto-ignition Temp. | 595 °C | 650–750 °C |
| Flammability Limit (in air) | 5–15 vol % | 6–12 vol % |
| Methane Number | 100 | 130–140 |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cow's Stomach" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a cow chewing cud for hours inside its **rumen** (one of its four stomach chambers). The rumen is a natural, warm (≈ 39 °C), oxygen-free, bacteria-rich fermentation vat. Every morsel of grass that enters is broken down by microbes without any air, releasing a combustible gas that the cow regularly **belches** (a cow actually produces 30–50 L of biogas per hour through eructation!). 
> 
> An engineered **biogas plant / digester** is essentially a man-made artificial "cow's stomach" — a sealed, oxygen-free, temperature-controlled vessel containing the same microbial community, converting our organic waste into usable fuel. The only difference is that we collect the "burp" through a pipe and use it to cook dinner instead of letting it escape into the atmosphere.

### 1.3 Significance in the KTU 2024 Renewable-Energy Context

Within the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 (Biomass & Biofuels), biogas occupies the most prominent position because it simultaneously addresses **three** critical national priorities:

1. **Waste Management:** Converts wet, putrescible organic waste (which otherwise releases uncontrolled methane — a GHG 25× more potent than CO₂ — from landfills) into a controlled energy product.
2. **Decentralised Energy Generation:** Provides cooking fuel and electricity to off-grid rural households (e.g., India's *SATAT* scheme targets 15 MMT of compressed biogas by 2030).
3. **Nutrient Recovery:** The effluent **digestate** is a nitrogen-rich bio-fertiliser, completing the nutrient loop.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Sequential Bio-Chemical Stages of Anaerobic Digestion

Anaerobic digestion is **NOT** a single chemical reaction; it is a tightly coupled, four-stage microbial food-chain. Each stage is performed by a *different* group of micro-organisms with specific environmental tolerances.

#### **Stage 1 — Hydrolysis (Polymer breakdown)**
- **Microbes involved:** *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Streptococcus* (facultative & obligate anaerobes).
- **Substrate acted upon:** Complex biopolymers — carbohydrates (cellulose, starch), proteins, lipids.
- **Reaction:** Long-chain polymers $\xrightarrow{\text{extracellular enzymes}}$ monomers.
  - Cellulose + H₂O $\longrightarrow$ Glucose
  - Proteins + H₂O $\longrightarrow$ Amino Acids
  - Lipids + H₂O $\longrightarrow$ Fatty Acids + Glycerol
- **Rate-limiting factor:** This is the **slowest** stage for high-cellulose feedstocks (rice straw, bagasse), hence **pre-treatment** (mechanical size reduction, thermal, chemical) is essential.
- **Typical duration:** Hours to several days.

#### **Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Monomer fermentation)**
- **Microbes involved:** Acidogenic bacteria (*Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*).
- **Substrate acted upon:** Monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids).
- **Products:** Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs — acetic, propionic, butyric, valeric acid), alcohols, H₂, CO₂.
- **Critical warning:** If VFAs accumulate, pH drops below **6.0**, inhibiting methanogens. This is called **"souring"** of the digester.

#### **Stage 3 — Acetogenesis (VFA conversion)**
- **Microbes involved:** Acetogenic bacteria (*Syntrophomonas*, *Syntrophobacter*).
- **Substrate acted upon:** VFAs (propionic, butyric) + alcohols.
- **Products:** Acetic acid (CH₃COOH), H₂, CO₂.
- **Thermodynamic bottleneck:** This reaction is **endergonic** ($\Delta G > 0$) under standard conditions. It only proceeds if H₂ partial pressure is kept low by methanogens ("interspecies hydrogen transfer"). Hence stages 3 and 4 are syntrophically coupled.

#### **Stage 4 — Methanogenesis (Methane formation)**
- **Microbes involved:** Methanogenic archaea — strictly anaerobic.
- **Two pathways:**
  - **Acetoclastic (≈ 70 % of CH₄):** CH₃COOH $\longrightarrow$ CH₄ + CO₂
  - **Hydrogenotrophic (≈ 30 %):** 4 H₂ + CO₂ $\longrightarrow$ CH₄ + 2 H₂O
- **Optimum conditions:** pH **6.8–7.4**, temperature **35–40 °C** (mesophilic) or **50–55 °C** (thermophilic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Methanogenic archaea are *extremely sensitive* to environmental fluctuations. They are the **first casualties** of digester imbalance. Hence the design philosophy is: **"protect the methanogens"** by controlling loading rate, temperature and pH.

---

### 2.2 Stoichiometric Chemistry — Buswell's Equation

The **Buswell Equation** gives the theoretical complete digestion stoichiometry for any organic molecule **C_n H_a O_b** containing nitrogen/sulphur:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b\text{N}_c\text{S}_d + \left(n - \tfrac{a}{4} - \tfrac{b}{2} + \tfrac{3c}{4} + \tfrac{d}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \\
\left(\tfrac{n}{2} - \tfrac{a}{8} + \tfrac{b}{4} + \tfrac{3c}{8} + \tfrac{d}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2 + \left(\tfrac{n}{2} + \tfrac{a}{8} - \tfrac{b}{4} - \tfrac{3c}{8} - \tfrac{d}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4 + c\,\text{NH}_3 + d\,\text{H}_2\text{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example — Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 3\,\text{CH}_4 \;+\; 3\,\text{CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the theoretical maximum CH₄ yield of **0.373 Nm³ CH₄ per kg glucose destroyed**, with a CH₄:CO₂ mole ratio of exactly **1:1** (= 50 % CH₄). Practical yields are always lower (typically 60–90 % of theoretical) due to microbial energy needs and side reactions.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Equation | Symbol / Formula | Typical Value / Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Biogas Yield** | $Y_g = \dfrac{V_g}{m_s}$ | 0.2 – 0.5 m³/kg VS added | $V_g$ = gas volume, $m_s$ = substrate mass |
| 2 | **Methane Yield** | $Y_{CH_4} = Y_g \times X_{CH_4}$ | 0.10 – 0.30 m³ CH₄/kg VS | $X_{CH_4}$ = methane fraction |
| 3 | **C:N Ratio** | $\text{C/N} = \dfrac{\%C_{org}}{\%N_{total}}$ | **20 : 1 to 30 : 1** optimal | Below 15 → ammonia toxicity; above 35 → N-limitation |
| 4 | **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | 15 – 40 days (mesophilic) | $V$ = digester volume, $Q$ = daily feed |
| 5 | **Organic Loading Rate (OLR)** | $\text{OLR} = \dfrac{m_{VS}}{V \cdot \theta}$ | 1 – 5 kg VS/m³·day | Higher OLR → risk of acidification |
| 6 | **Digester Sizing** | $V = Q \cdot \theta$ | m³ | Daily feed (m³/d) × HRT (d) |
| 7 | **Theoretical CH₄ from glucose** | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \to 3\text{CH}_4+3\text{CO}_2$ | 0.373 Nm³ CH₄/kg | Stoichiometric maximum |
| 8 | **Methane Energy Content** | $E = V_{CH_4} \times 35.88$ | MJ | 35.88 MJ/Nm³ pure CH₄ |
| 9 | **Biogas Energy Content** | $E_g = V_g \times \eta_{CH_4} \times 35.88$ | MJ | $\eta_{CH_4}$ = methane volume fraction |
| 10 | **Volumetric Gas Production Rate** | $Q_g = V \cdot \text{OLR} \cdot Y_g$ | Nm³/d | Used in plant throughput design |
| 11 | **Temperature Correction Factor** | $k_T = k_{35} \cdot 1.07^{(T-35)}$ | dimensionless | Activity doubling ≈ every 10 °C (Arrhenius) |
| 12 | **pH Buffer Equation** | $\text{pH} = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[\text{HCO}_3^-]}{[\text{CO}_2]}$ | ≈ 7.0 | Bicarbonate alkalinity governs stability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on vertical bars in markdown tables:** Wherever absolute-value or norm symbols would normally be written (e.g., $|x|$), KTU examiners accept either $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ to prevent markdown table-rendering errors.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The equations above are used in **four primary engineering deliverables** required by KTU 2024 graduates entering the renewable-energy workforce:

1. **Digester Capacity Sizing:** Designing village-scale (1–10 m³) *Janata* / *KVIC* plants and large industrial (500–5000 m³) CSTR digesters.
2. **Co-digestion Optimisation:** Blending high-C substrates (sawdust, straw) with high-N substrates (poultry manure, food waste) to hit the optimum C/N window.
3. **Power-Generation Sizing:** Estimating CHP (combined heat & power) engine kW-rating from methane yield.
4. **Carbon-Credit Quantification:** Calculating avoided fossil-fuel CO₂ emissions per Nm³ of biogas combusted.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Maximum Methane Yield from Glucose

**Given:** Substrate = pure glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆), molecular weight $M_w = 180$ g/mol, density irrelevant (solid).
**Find:** Theoretical specific methane yield in Nm³ CH₄ per kg glucose.

**Step 1 — Write the Buswell stoichiometric equation for C₆H₁₂O₆.**

From the Buswell master equation with $n=6,\ a=12,\ b=6,\ c=0,\ d=0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Moles of CH}_4 &= \frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} \\
&= \frac{6}{2} + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4} \\
&= 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 \\
&= 3.0 \;\text{mol CH}_4 \;\text{per mol glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert moles to volume at STP (0 °C, 1 atm).**

Using the ideal-gas law, 1 mole of any ideal gas occupies **22.414 L (0.022414 m³)** at STP.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CH_4} &= n_{CH_4} \times 22.414 \;\text{L/mol} \\
&= 3.0 \times 22.414 \\
&= 67.242 \;\text{L} \\
&= 0.067242 \;\text{Nm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to per-kg basis.**

Moles of glucose per kg $= \frac{1000 \;\text{g}}{180 \;\text{g/mol}} = 5.556 \;\text{mol/kg}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{CH_4,\,\text{theoretical}} &= 0.067242 \;\frac{\text{Nm}^3}{\text{mol glucose}} \times 5.556 \;\frac{\text{mol}}{\text{kg}} \\
&= 0.3736 \;\text{Nm}^3\text{ CH}_4 / \text{kg glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The theoretical ceiling for methane yield from glucose is **≈ 0.373 Nm³ CH₄ / kg**, which is the benchmark used to evaluate real-world digester performance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Digester Volume Sizing for a Dairy Farm

**Problem Statement:** A dairy farm generates **500 kg of cattle dung per day**. Design the volume of a mesophilic continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) digester. Assume:
- Total Solids (TS) content of dung = **18 %**
- Volatile Solids (VS) content of TS = **80 %**
- Moisture-adjusted specific weight of slurry = **1070 kg/m³**
- Recommended HRT = **30 days**
- Methane yield $Y_{CH_4}$ = **0.20 m³ CH₄ per kg VS added**

**Step 1 — Determine volatile solids (VS) produced per day.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{VS} &= m_{dung} \times \%TS \times \%VS \\
&= 500 \;\text{kg/d} \times 0.18 \times 0.80 \\
&= 72 \;\text{kg VS/d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine daily slurry volume (since dung is mixed with water to make ~10 % TS pumpable slurry).**

Total solids needed in slurry to maintain pumpability = 10 %. So mass of slurry containing 90 kg TS (i.e., 500 kg dung @ 18 %):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{slurry} &= \frac{m_{dung} \times \%TS}{\%TS_{slurry}} = \frac{500 \times 0.18}{0.10} = 900 \;\text{kg slurry/d}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Or equivalently, using typical practice of 1:1 dung-to-water mixing = 1000 kg slurry/d; we adopt **900 kg** here for design conservatism.)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{m_{slurry}}{\rho_{slurry}} = \frac{900}{1070} = 0.841 \;\text{m}^3/\text{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate required digester volume.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= Q \times \theta \\
&= 0.841 \;\text{m}^3/\text{d} \times 30 \;\text{d} \\
&= 25.23 \;\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply **10 % safety / head-space margin** (gas collection dome): $V_{design} = 25.23 \times 1.10 \approx 27.8$ m³ → adopt **30 m³ standard KVIC-type floating-drum digester**.

**Step 4 — Methane production rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{CH_4} &= m_{VS} \times Y_{CH_4} \\
&= 72 \;\text{kg/d} \times 0.20 \;\text{m}^3/\text{kg} \\
&= 14.4 \;\text{Nm}^3\text{ CH}_4/\text{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Power-generation potential.**

Assume biogas = 60 % CH₄, so raw biogas = $\frac{14.4}{0.60} = 24 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{d}$. Engine electrical efficiency = 35 %.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{el} &= \frac{\dot{V}_{CH_4} \times 35.88 \;\text{MJ/Nm}^3 \times \eta_{el}}{24 \times 3600 \;\text{s}} \\
&= \frac{14.4 \times 35.88 \times 0.35}{86400} \\
&= 2.09 \;\text{kW}_{\text{electric}} \;\text{continuous}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Verdict:** A single 30 m³ KVIC digester fed with the dung of ~50 cattle can supply the continuous baseload electricity for **≈ 15 rural households** (assuming 150 W each), plus 24 Nm³/day of cooking gas.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — C/N Ratio for Co-digestion Optimisation

**Problem:** Mix rice straw (C/N = 80:1) with poultry droppings (C/N = 8:1) to achieve optimum C/N = 25:1. Determine the mass ratio.

Let $x$ = mass of rice straw, $y$ = mass of poultry droppings.

Using the **harmonic mean** rule (since C/N is a ratio, the blended C/N is mass-weighted only approximately; for accurate work, use the linear-mixing rule on **inverse ratios**):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{mix} &= \frac{x \cdot C_s + y \cdot C_p}{x \cdot N_s + y \cdot N_p} = 25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assume representative carbon fractions:** $C_s = 40\,\%,\ N_s = 0.50\,\%$ for rice straw; $C_p = 30\,\%,\ N_p = 3.75\,\%$ for poultry droppings.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{0.40\,x + 0.30\,y}{0.0050\,x + 0.0375\,y} &= 25 \\
0.40\,x + 0.30\,y &= 25 \times (0.0050\,x + 0.0375\,y) \\
0.40\,x + 0.30\,y &= 0.125\,x + 0.9375\,y \\
0.275\,x &= 0.6375\,y \\
\frac{x}{y} &= 2.32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Mix rice straw and poultry droppings in the mass ratio **≈ 2.3 : 1** to attain the optimum C/N of 25:1.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operating-Parameter Effects on Biogas Yield

| Operating Parameter | Below Optimum | Optimum Range | Above Optimum |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature (°C)** | Psychrophilic (10–20 °C) → slow kinetics | **Mesophilic: 35–40 °C** or Thermophilic: 50–55 °C | > 60 °C kills archaea; sudden shifts > ±2 °C/day cause failure |
| **pH** | < 6.5 → VFA accumulation, souring | **6.8 – 7.4** | > 8.0 → ammonia toxicity, free NH₃ inhibition |
| **C/N Ratio** | < 15 → NH₃ toxicity | **20 – 30** | > 35 → N-starvation, low microbial growth |
| **HRT** | < 10 d → washout of methanogens | **15 – 40 d (mesophilic)** | > 60 d → under-utilisation, large reactor cost |
| **OLR (kg VS/m³·d)** | < 1 → poor gas yield | **2 – 4 (CSTR)** | > 6 → overloading, souring, foam |
| **Toxicity** | — | H₂S < 200 mg/L; NH₃ < 1700 mg/L | Antibiotics, detergents, heavy metals → fatal |
| **Mixing** | Settling / scum formation | Gentle, intermittent (5–10 min/h) | Excessive shear disrupts syntrophic flocs |

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Energy Recovery from Biogas Combustion

**Given:** Daily biogas production $V_g = 24$ Nm³, methane fraction = 60 %.

**Step 1 — Methane volume:**

$$
V_{CH_4} = 24 \times 0.60 = 14.4 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{d}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy content (calorific value of CH₄ = 35.88 MJ/Nm³):**

$$
E_{CH_4} = 14.4 \times 35.88 = 516.7 \;\text{MJ/d}
$$

**Step 3 — Useful electrical energy at 35 % engine efficiency:**

$$
E_{el} = 516.7 \times 0.35 = 180.8 \;\text{MJ/d} = 50.2 \;\text{kWh/d}
$$

**Step 4 — Useful thermal energy at 45 % heat-recovery efficiency:**

$$
E_{th} = 516.7 \times 0.45 = 232.5 \;\text{MJ/d}
$$

**Step 5 — Total CHP efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{CHP} = 0.35 + 0.45 = 0.80 \quad \text{or} \quad \mathbf{80\,\%}
$$

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
Define the term **biogas**. List the four sequential biological stages involved in its production.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Biogas is a renewable gaseous fuel produced by the **anaerobic digestion** of organic biomass in the absence of oxygen, comprising primarily **methane (50–70 %)** and **carbon dioxide (30–40 %)**, with trace H₂S, NH₃ and water vapour.

**The four stages are:**
1. **Hydrolysis** — complex polymers broken into monomers by extracellular enzymes.
2. **Acidogenesis** — monomers fermented into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂ and CO₂.
3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs converted into acetic acid, H₂ and CO₂.
4. **Methanogenesis** — acetic acid and H₂/CO₂ converted into CH₄ by methanogenic archaea.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
What is the significance of the **C/N ratio** in anaerobic digestion? State the optimum range.

**Model Answer:**

The **Carbon-to-Nitrogen (C/N) ratio** of the feed substrate determines the nutritional balance for the microbial consortium. Microbes need roughly **25–30 times more carbon than nitrogen** for synthesising their cellular biomass.

- **Optimum range: 20 : 1 to 30 : 1**
- If C/N < 15 → excess nitrogen is released as free ammonia (NH₃), which is toxic to methanogens and inhibits digestion.
- If C/N > 35 → nitrogen becomes limiting, slowing microbial growth and lowering gas yield.

Hence, blending high-C substrates (rice straw, sawdust) with high-N substrates (poultry droppings, food waste) is the standard practice to bring C/N into the optimum window.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the **KVIC floating-drum biogas plant**. Discuss its advantages and limitations over the Janata fixed-dome plant.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Construction (KVIC — Khadi and Village Industries Commission design):**
- An underground cylindrical masonry/brick digester (typically 4–20 m³) houses the fermentation chamber.
- On top of the slurry sits an **inverted mild-steel drum** (the gas holder), which rises and falls vertically along a central guide pipe as gas accumulates or is drawn off.
- A water seal around the rim of the drum ensures gas-tightness.
- An inlet mixing tank on one side, an outlet displacement chamber on the other, and a gas outlet pipe at the top of the drum.

**(ii) Working Principle:**
As biogas is generated in the digester, it bubbles up and collects inside the inverted drum. The drum rises proportionally to the gas volume — the **vertical displacement** is a direct visual indicator of gas storage. Gas pressure remains **constant** (≈ 7–10 cm of water column) irrespective of the gas volume stored.

**(iii) Advantages:**
- Constant gas pressure → uniform burner flame.
- Direct visual indication of stored gas volume.
- Easy gas-quantity measurement via float gauge.
- Quick gas collection (no waiting for pressure build-up as in fixed dome).
- Easier removal of scum, as the drum can be lifted.

**(iv) Limitations:**
- Mild-steel drum corrodes quickly in H₂S environment → needs anti-corrosive paint, periodic maintenance (every 3–5 years).
- Higher capital cost (≈ 40–60 % more than Janata).
- Moving parts require maintenance.
- Not suitable for very cold climates (gas line freezes).

**(v) Comparison with Janata Fixed-Dome:**
| Feature | KVIC Floating Drum | Janata Fixed Dome |
|---|---|---|
| Capital cost | Higher | Lower (≈ 50 % less) |
| Gas pressure | Constant | Variable (rises with gas) |
| Corrosion | Severe (steel) | None (brick) |
| Lifespan | 15–20 years | **30+ years** |
| Visual gas-level indicator | Yes | No |

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 Marks]** A community kitchen generates **200 kg of food waste per day** with 22 % total solids and 90 % volatile solids (of TS). Design the digester volume for **30 days HRT** and estimate the **daily methane production**. Assume specific methane yield = 0.25 Nm³/kg VS, methane fraction = 60 %, slurry density = 1050 kg/m³, and TS of slurry = 10 %.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Daily VS mass:**

$$
m_{VS} = 200 \;\text{kg/d} \times 0.22 \times 0.90 = 39.6 \;\text{kg VS/d}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily slurry volume at 10 % TS:**

$$
m_{slurry} = \frac{200 \times 0.22}{0.10} = 440 \;\text{kg slurry/d}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{440}{1050} = 0.419 \;\text{m}^3/\text{d}
$$

**Step 3 — Digester volume:**

$$
V = Q \times \theta = 0.419 \times 30 = 12.57 \;\text{m}^3
$$

With 10 % safety factor: $V_{design} = 12.57 \times 1.10 \approx \mathbf{13.8 \;\text{m}^3}$ — adopt standard **15 m³ digester**.

**Step 4 — Daily methane production:**

$$
\dot{V}_{CH_4} = 39.6 \times 0.25 = 9.9 \;\text{Nm}^3\text{ CH}_4/\text{d}
$$

**Step 5 — Raw biogas volume:**

$$
\dot{V}_{biogas} = \frac{9.9}{0.60} = \mathbf{16.5 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{d}}
$$

**Step 6 — Energy output:**

$$
E = 9.9 \times 35.88 = 355 \;\text{MJ/d} \approx 98.6 \;\text{kWh/d}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Buswell stoichiometric equation** for complete anaerobic digestion of **glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)**. Hence compute the theoretical specific methane yield in Nm³ per kg.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — General Buswell form** (with $n=6,\ a=12,\ b=6,\ c=0,\ d=0$):

$$
\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n - \tfrac{a}{4} - \tfrac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \left(\tfrac{n}{2} - \tfrac{a}{8} + \tfrac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2 + \left(\tfrac{n}{2} + \tfrac{a}{8} - \tfrac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4
$$

**Step 2 — Coefficient evaluation:**

- $n = 6,\; a = 12,\; b = 6$
- $n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2} = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0$ → **no water required** (glucose is already oxidised enough)
- Moles of CH₄ $= \frac{6}{2} + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3$
- Moles of CO₂ $= \frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3$

**Step 3 — Balanced reaction:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 3\,\text{CH}_4 \;+\; 3\,\text{CO}_2
$$

**Step 4 — Specific methane yield at STP:**

Moles of glucose per kg $= \frac{1000}{180} = 5.556$ mol.

Moles of CH₄ per kg glucose $= 3 \times 5.556 = 16.667$ mol.

Volume at STP ($22.414$ L/mol $= 0.022414$ m³/mol):

$$
Y_{CH_4} = 16.667 \times 0.022414 = 0.3736 \;\text{Nm}^3\text{ CH}_4/\text{kg}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always show units at *every* step and quote the ideal-gas molar volume (22.414 L/mol at STP) explicitly — failure to mention STP is a common cause of **½-mark deduction**.

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **factors affecting biogas production**, emphasising temperature, pH, C/N ratio and hydraulic retention time.

**Model Solution:**

Biogas yield is sensitive to **four primary operating parameters**:

**(1) Temperature:**
- Microbial activity obeys Arrhenius kinetics. Yield roughly **doubles for every 10 °C rise** within the biological range.
- Three operating regimes:
  - **Psychrophilic:** 15–25 °C — very slow, suitable for temperate climates.
  - **Mesophilic:** **35–40 °C** — most common, robust microbial community.
  - **Thermophilic:** 50–55 °C — faster kinetics but less stable; methanogens are sensitive.
- Sudden temperature shocks > ±2 °C/day cause digester failure.

**(2) pH:**
- Optimum range is **6.8 – 7.4**.
- Acidogenesis produces VFAs → pH drop if methanogenesis cannot keep pace → "souring".
- Bicarbonate alkalinity (1500–5000 mg/L as CaCO₃) provides buffering capacity.
- Excess ammonia raises pH above 8.0, inhibiting methanogens.

**(3) C/N Ratio:**
- Optimum **20 : 1 to 30 : 1**.
- C/N > 35 → nitrogen-starved microbes, slow reaction.
- C/N < 15 → ammonia toxicity.
- Co-digestion (mixing substrates) is the standard remedial practice.

**(4) Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):**
- $\theta = V/Q$; minimum $\theta$ depends on temperature.
- At 35 °C, minimum HRT ≈ 10–15 d (shorter → wash-out of slow-growing methanogens).
- Typical design $\theta$ = **20–40 days** for mesophilic CSTR.
- Trade-off: higher $\theta$ → larger reactor cost but more complete digestion.

**Other secondary factors:** mixing intensity (gentle is best), presence of inhibitors (antibiotics, heavy metals, detergents), feedstock pre-treatment, and volatile-solids content of slurry.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks (Most Frequent Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 10 % safety / head-space factor** in digester volume — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up TS % and VS %** — TS = total dried solids, VS = the *organic* fraction of TS (≈ 70–90 % of TS for most biomass). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Not stating STP** when converting moles of CH₄ to Nm³ — deduct ½ mark.
> 4. **Confusing "biogas volume" with "methane volume"** — always clearly state *which* gas you are computing.
> 5. **Skipping unit balancing** in numerical answers — full marks require consistent SI units throughout.
> 6. **In KVs/Janata comparisons,** many students write "fixed dome is cheaper" but *omit the reason* (no moving parts, no steel corrosion) — partial credit only.
> 7. **C/N ratio questions:** students often confuse the *blending formula* (linear interpolation vs harmonic mean vs the actual mass-balance equation derived in Section 3.3) — practice the mass-balance derivation repeatedly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Biogas** = CH₄ (50–70 %) + CO₂ (30–40 %) + trace gases, produced by **anaerobic digestion**.
- [ ] **Four stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.
- [ ] **Buswell equation** (for any C_n H_a O_b N_c S_d) gives theoretical gas yields.
- [ ] **Glucose stoichiometry:** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 3 CH₄ + 3 CO₂ (50 % CH₄, 0.373 Nm³ CH₄/kg).
- [ ] **Methane calorific value = 35.88 MJ/Nm³** at STP.
- [ ] **Optimum C/N ratio = 20 : 1 to 30 : 1.**
- [ ] **Optimum pH = 6.8 – 7.4** (methanogens are pH-sensitive).
- [ ] **Optimum temperature = 35–40 °C (mesophilic)** or 50–55 °C (thermophilic).
- [ ] **HRT** = V/Q; mesophilic design range = **20–40 days**.
- [ ] **OLR** = 2–4 kg VS/m³·day for stable CSTR operation.
- [ ] **Digester sizing formula:** $V = Q \cdot \theta$ + 10 % head-space.
- [ ] **Methanogenic archaea** (NOT bacteria) are responsible for CH₄ formation.
- [ ] **Co-digestion** is used to balance C/N when single substrate is unsuitable.
- [ ] **KVIC** plant: floating mild-steel drum, constant pressure, more expensive, shorter life.
- [ ] **Janata** plant: fixed masonry dome, variable pressure, lower cost, longer life.
- [ ] **Deenbandhu** plant: modified fixed dome with partition wall for improved gas retention.
- [ ] **Digestate** = nitrogen-rich biofertiliser (secondary economic product).
- [ ] **H₂S** in raw biogas must be scrubbed (< 200 mg/L) to prevent engine corrosion.
- [ ] **Carbon-neutral** classification: CO₂ released was recently photosynthetically fixed.
- [ ] **CHP efficiency** (electric + thermal) of biogas engines ≈ 80–85 %.
- [ ] Typical **methane yield:** 0.20–0.30 Nm³ CH₄/kg VS for cattle dung; lower for lignocellulosic wastes.
- [ ] **Activity-doubling rule:** yield approximately doubles every 10 °C within biological range (Arrhenius).
- [ ] **India's SATAT scheme** targets 15 MMT compressed biogas annually by 2030.
